Tafseer:
يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْٓا اَوْفُوْا بِالْعُقُوْدِ ڛ اُحِلَّتْ لَكُمْ بَهِيْمَةُ الْاَنْعَامِ اِلَّا مَا يُتْلٰى عَلَيْكُمْ غَيْرَ مُحِلِّي الصَّيْدِ وَاَنْتُمْ حُرُمٌ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ يَحْكُمُ مَا يُرِيْدُ 

**The Meaning of the Word 'Uqud' (Covenants/Contracts) and its Scope:** 'O you who have believed, fulfill the covenants.' The word *'uqūd* (plural of *'aqd*) is more general in meaning compared to the words *'ahd* and *mīthāq* (covenant/treaty). It encompasses everything from verbal agreements, oaths, and the responsibility of giving testimony in any matter, to the primordial covenant made between God and His servants. Accordingly, this Surah details the entire history of the covenant of the Shariah along with all its consequences and outcomes, and clarifies the responsibilities associated with oaths and testimonies.

**The Difference Between 'An'am' and 'Bahimah':** 'Lawful to you is the beast of cattle.' In Arabic, the word *an'ām* is commonly used for sheep, goats, camels, and cattle. The Quran itself has clarified this in Surah Al-An'am, verses 143 and 144. The word *bahīmah* is more general than *an'ām*, as it also includes other quadrupeds of the same nature as cattle. The genitive construction of *bahīmah* with *an'ām* indicates that camels, cattle, goats, and all such quadrupeds—whether domestic or wild—have been made permissible for you. 'Made permissible' means that those restrictions which you imposed on yourselves based on superstitious whims are abolished, and those based on the traditions of previous scriptures are also nullified.

'Except that which is recited to you.' This refers to the prohibitions mentioned ahead in verse 3.

**The Prohibition of Hunting While in the State of Ihram and its Importance:** 'Not permitting hunting while you are in the state of ihram.' This is the mention of the very first of these prohibitions. That is, all quadrupeds of the cattle category, whether domestic or wild, are permissible for you, subject to the condition that you do not make hunting permissible for yourselves while in the state of Ihram. The use of the circumstantial clause (*hāl*) and placing it at the very beginning of the mention highlights its importance, which requires a bit of detail to be fully understood.

We have already hinted in the introductory discussion that the rulings set forth in this Surah are of a perfecting and completing nature, and they also prominently feature the aspect of trial and tribulation. Because of this very aspect, they are fittingly placed in this Surah, which is Surah Al-Mithaq (The Surah of the Covenant). Taking a covenant upon these rulings means, on one hand, that a covenant was taken to adhere to the entire Shariah, and on the other hand, a covenant was taken regarding things that had proved to be a stumbling block for other nations. If you reflect here, you will find that both of these aspects are kept in view.

Regarding food and drink, the prohibitions and permissions declared here are of the final nature. Before this, many rulings on this subject were mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, and in even greater detail in Surah Al-An'am, which is a Meccan Surah. Only some minor details and specifics remained, which have been explained in this Surah, thereby bringing this subject to absolute completion. This reality will become so clear from the upcoming verses that it will stand in no need of proof.

From the perspective of trial and testing, the prohibition of hunting while in the state of Ihram is highly similar to the command given to the Jews regarding the sanctity of the Sabbath. They were forbidden from hunting on the Sabbath, but they could not uphold this covenant. Instead, they devised various ruses to make it permissible, as a result of which Allah cursed them. The similarity to the Sabbath ruling is made clear by the Quran itself in the upcoming verses 93 to 96 of this very Surah.

The meaning of the verse is: O you who believe, fulfill the covenant you made with your Lord to adhere to His Shariah. All quadrupeds of the cattle category have been made lawful for you, except those that are about to be mentioned, on the condition that you do not hunt while in the state of Ihram. Finally, by stating 'Indeed, Allah ordains what He intends,' He pointed to the testing aspect of this command—indicating that this ruling is to test your loyalty, so do not look for loopholes or ways to evade it. God commands what He wills, and the absolute obedience to His commands without questioning is where the well-being and blessing lie for His servants. It should be kept in mind here that the wisdom behind rulings intended for testing is often hidden from the servants. Therefore, unless the belief that God possesses absolute authority to command and that His every command is for the welfare of His servants is firmly rooted in the heart, sincere and loyal compliance cannot be achieved.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا لَا تُحِلُّوْا شَعَاۗىِٕرَ اللّٰهِ وَلَا الشَّهْرَ الْحَرَامَ وَلَا الْهَدْيَ وَلَا الْقَلَاۗىِٕدَ وَلَآ اٰۗمِّيْنَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرَامَ يَبْتَغُوْنَ فَضْلًا مِّنْ رَّبِّهِمْ وَرِضْوَانًا ۭوَاِذَا حَلَلْتُمْ فَاصْطَادُوْا ۭوَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَـنَاٰنُ قَوْمٍ اَنْ صَدُّوْكُمْ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ اَنْ tَعْتَدُوْا ۘوَتَعَاوَنُوْا عَلَي الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوٰى ۠ وَلَا تَعَاوَنُوْا عَلَي الْاِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوَانِ ۠وَاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ شَدِيْدُ الْعِقَابِ 

**Respect for the Sacred Symbols (Sha'air):** 'Do not violate the sacred symbols of Allah.' A detailed discussion on *Sha'ā'ir Allāh* (the sacred symbols of Allah) has already passed under Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 158. Sacred symbols represent and embody significant religious and spiritual realities. The primary focus is on the spiritual and meaningful realities hidden within these symbols, as they were designated as signs and marks to evoke these very realities. However, since they are ordained by God, equal respect is demanded for both their outward form and inward essence. No one has the right to violate the rules and conditions set for their respect, or to make lawful the things that are forbidden in connection with them. For example, the four sacred months—Dhu al-Qa'dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, Muharram, and Rajab—which were declared sacred in connection with Hajj and Umrah, are months in which fighting is prohibited. If any group initiates war during these months, it means they have made these sacred months lawful for themselves and desecrated them.

**The Meaning of 'Hady' and 'Qala'id':** *Hady* refers to the sacrificial animals brought to the House of Allah as gifts to be offered in His presence. *Qalā'id* is the plural of *qalādah*, which means a collar or band. Here, a noun is omitted in the genitive construction (*mudāf mahdūf*), meaning *dhawāt al-qalā'id*—that is, those animals of sacrifice and vow offering that have been marked with collars so they can be recognized and no one interferes with them. The mention of *qalā'id* after *hady* is of the nature of mentioning the specific after the general, and its purpose is to highlight the severity of interfering with them; attacking animals that have been marked with God's collars is like attacking God's own domain. 

Similarly, regarding those heading to the Sacred House (*āmmīna al-bayta al-harām*), the mention of the attribute 'seeking bounty from their Lord and His pleasure' is to make the prohibition effective. It implies that seeking to harm those servants of Allah who have left their homes in search of His bounty and pleasure is equivalent to opposing God Himself.

'And let not the hatred of a people... lead you...' *Shin'ān* means hatred and enmity, and *lā yajrimannakum* means: let it not be the cause or motive for you, let it not incite you. 'A people' here refers to the Quraysh, and 'because they obstructed you from the Sacred Mosque' is the detail of the cause of this hatred and enmity. That is, although the Quraysh committed a great injustice against you by blocking you from the House of Allah, the grief and anger of this action must not incite you to transgress the limits of Allah regarding His sacred symbols. Do not cause any harm to their pilgrim caravans or to their animals of sacrifice.

**Transgressing the Limits of Allah Due to the Incitement of Others is 'Cooperation in Sin':** 'And cooperate in righteousness and piety...' This is an emphasis from another angle of the preceding statement. That is, for the group whom Allah brought forth in the world to establish goodness and piety, the desirable path is not to get provoked by the injustices of others and start committing similar injustices. If they do so, it means they have cooperated in sin and transgression, adding blocks to the foundation of evil laid by the wicked, whereas their duty was to cooperate in righteousness and piety. (1)

*(1) It should be noted that what is prohibited here is that Muslims themselves, out of provocation from the conduct of others, should not aggressively do something that violates the respect of Allah's sacred symbols. If Muslims are forced to take a step for self-preservation and defense, that is excluded. Defensive war can be fought even in the sacred months, and indeed within the Sanctuary itself. This discussion has already passed in Al-Baqarah.*

Having understood the individual parts, let us look at the collective structure of the verse once more:

In the preceding verse, hunting was prohibited while in the state of Ihram, as this is contrary to the sanctity of Ihram and its ascetic spirit, and is a desecration of one of Allah's sacred symbols. Now, in connection with this, the respect for all of Allah's sacred symbols is first emphasized collectively, followed by a reference to several specific symbols. Then, regarding the prohibition of hunting, it is clarified that this is restricted only to the state of Ihram. Once you leave the state of Ihram, this prohibition is lifted.

Then, the provocative cause that was freshly present at that time is mentioned. There was a risk that Muslims, overcome by it, might do something contrary to the respect of the sacred symbols. The Quraysh had deprived them of the Hajj and visitation to the House of Allah. This was an extremely delicate and testing situation, and now that Muslims had gained political power, there was a significant fear that some excess might occur from them in respecting this covenant. This situation made it necessary for Allah to warn them against slipping; the injustices of others cannot provide justification for any injustice on their part. They have risen as the standard-bearers of respect for the sacred symbols of Allah and of righteousness and piety in the world. Therefore, unless forced by the necessity of self-preservation, it is not permissible for them to be overcome by emotions and take any step against righteousness and piety. Following this, a profound point was stated: adopting the same wrong path as others out of influence from their bad behavior is actually cooperating with them in the evil they have established, and this is not fitting for the believers. What is fitting for believers is to cooperate in deeds of righteousness and piety. Even if some good work is being done by the enemy, they should not obstruct it but rather encourage it. Finally, the reference to 'severe in penalty' is meant as a stern warning to the Muslims that the sanctity of the covenant of Allah must be maintained even under the harshest circumstances. Otherwise, remember that the God who honored you with the leadership of the world through His covenant also inflicts an extremely severe punishment for breaking the covenant.

حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةُ وَالدَّمُ وَلَحْمُ الْخِنْزِيْرِ وَمَآ اُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ اللّٰهِ بِهٖ وَالْمُنْخَنِقَةُ وَالْمَوْقُوْذَةُ وَالْمُتَرَدِّيَةُ وَالنَّطِيْحَةُ وَمَآ اَ كَلَ السَّبُعُ اِلَّا مَا ذَكَّيْتُمْ ۣ وَمَا ذُبِحَ عَلَي النُّصُبِ وَاَنْ تَسْـتَقْسِمُوْا بِالْاَزْلَامِ ۭذٰلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ ۭ اَلْيَوْمَ يَىِٕسَ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا مِنْ دِيْنِكُمْ فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ ۭ اَلْيَوْمَ اَ كْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِيْنَكُمْ وَاَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِيْ وَرَضِيْتُ لَكُمُ الْاِسْلَامَ دِيْنًا ۭ فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ فِيْ مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجَانِفٍ لِّاِثْمٍ ۙ فَاِنَّ اللّٰهَ غَفُوْرٌ رَّحِيْمٌ

**Details of 'Maytah' (Dead Animals):** The mention of *maytah* (carrion), blood, pork, and that which has been dedicated to other than Allah has already passed under Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 173. *Munkhaniqah* refers to an animal that dies of strangulation. *Mawqudhah* is one that dies from a violent blow—for instance, if a wall falls on an animal or it is run over by a truck. *Mutaraddiyah* is one that dies from falling from a height. *Natihah* is one that dies after being gored by another animal's horn. *Mā akala as-sabu'* is that which has been partly eaten by a wild beast.

The mention of these five things is actually a detailed elaboration of *maytah* (carrion), and this detail completes the ruling that was previously stated in Al-Baqarah and before that in Al-An'am. This detail was necessary because a doubt could arise in some minds that there should be some difference between a dead animal that died a natural death and one that died suddenly due to a blow or an accident. Indeed, some people raise this doubt even in this era, and many have used it as an excuse to deem a chicken with a twisted neck permissible. This detailed explanation by the Quran cleared up this doubt.

**Prohibition of Sacrifice at Altars, Shrines, and Tombs:** 'And that which is sacrificed on stone altars.' *Nusub* refers to stone altars and shrines. In Arabia, there were countless such altars where sacrifices were made to please idols, deities, spirits, and jinn. The Quran declared such sacrifices unlawful as well. The wording of the Quran clearly indicates that the unlawfulness in these cases arises solely from the intention of seeking nearness and pleasure by sacrificing at these shrines, regardless of whether the name of Allah or someone else is invoked over them. If their unlawfulness were only due to invoking a name other than Allah's, there would have been no need to mention them separately, as the preceding mention of 'that which has been dedicated to other than Allah' would have been sufficient. In our view, this ruling also includes those sacrifices offered at tombs and shrines, where the intention is to please the saint of the tomb or shrine. Whether the name of Allah or the saint is invoked at the time of slaughter, the source of unlawfulness is not the name but the place.

**The Nature of 'Istiqsam bil-Azlam':** 'And that you seek decision through divining arrows.' *Istiqsām* means seeking to know one's share, fortune, or destiny. *Azlām* refers to arrows used for gambling or divination. In Arabia, there was a practice of using divining arrows to determine, according to their belief, the decrees of the unseen, as well as gambling arrows to obtain portions of meat or other things. We explained under the discussion of *khamr* (intoxicants) and *maysir* (gambling) in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah that Arabs would hold drinking gatherings, and in their drunken state, they would slaughter whichever camel they wished, satisfying the owner by paying whatever price he demanded, and then gamble over its meat. The portions of meat they won would be roasted, eaten, and distributed while they drank wine, often leading to fierce fights in their state of intoxication, embroiling entire tribes for years and costing hundreds of lives. I believe that *istiqsām bil-azlām* here refers to this latter form.

'That is grave disobedience.' *Dhālikum* (that) points to all the things mentioned above, and the word *fisq* (disobedience/transgression) here is not used in its general jurisprudential sense but in its Quranic sense. In the Quran, this word is used to denote open disobedience, rebellion, disbelief, and polytheism. Regarding Iblis, it says: 'And he rebelled (*fafasaqa*) against the command of his Lord.'

**Declaration of Social Disassociation from Disbelievers:** 'This day those who disbelieve have despaired of your religion...' The word *al-yawm* (this day) does not refer to a specific calendar day but to the era in which these verses were revealed. We indicated in the introduction that this Surah consists entirely of rulings and guidance belonging to the final, perfecting phase of Islam. The disbelievers' despairing of this religion means that up until then, they had harbored the vain hope that they would either subdue it or reach a compromise on the principle of give-and-take so both sides could coexist. But now, this vain hope of theirs has come to an end. They have seen with open eyes that the two paths have diverged so completely that their meeting at any point is entirely impossible. It should be kept in mind that sharing food and drink plays a major role in social relations. If a situation arises where what is lawful and pure for one is declared filthy and unlawful for the other, it means a complete social dissociation has been declared, leaving no way for the two to sit together. Naturally, this development caused them to completely despair of Islam and the Muslims. Extreme despair sometimes produces a final burst of frantic effort, but this is merely the final gasp of a dying patient, after which nothing remains but the final breath. For this reason, the Quran said that there is no longer any need to fear them. Even if they exert their power now, what strength do they have left? Now, fear only Me and do not worry about them.

**Completion of Religion and Fulfilling of Favor:** 'This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you...' By the perfection of religion, what is meant is the perfection of the original religion (*asl ad-dīn*), and by the completion of favor, what is meant is the completion of this final Shariah. As far as the original religion is concerned, its beginning was with Adam. With the passage of time, in accordance with the circumstances and the demands of divine wisdom, it continued to be revealed to various prophets and messengers until it was perfected with the Seal of the Prophets, Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). The religions that came before this were parts of this religion; they did not possess the status of the complete religion. Only this religion holds the status of the complete religion. Indications of this reality are also present in the previous divine scriptures, references to which have passed in this book. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is the final brick in the chain of prophethood and the last brick in the corner of this palace of religion.

As for the completion of favor upon this final Ummah, its beginning was with the first revelation in the Cave of Hira, and step by step over a period of 23 years, Allah completed this favor. Thus, in this stage, on one hand, Allah's religion reached its perfection, and on the other hand, Allah's favor upon this Ummah was completed. Its collective name is Islam, which has always been the religion of Allah and which was transferred to the unlettered Prophet and his Ummah as the inheritance of Abraham and Ishmael. The phrase 'and have approved for you Islam as religion' expresses Allah's approval and choice of this religion, the reasons and arguments for which have passed in detail in Al-Baqarah and Ali 'Imran. By expressing this approval, His disapproval of Judaism and Christianity is indirectly expressed as well, indicating that they are not the religion of Allah but rather different forms of deviation from it.

**The Shariah Limit of Necessity:** 'But whoever is forced by severe hunger with no inclination to sin.' *Makhmatsah* means hunger. Being forced by hunger means that a person is caught in such a calamity of hunger that no other way is outwardly left open except death or resorting to what is unlawful. In such a state, he is permitted to use even unlawful things to save his life. Along with this, the restriction of *ghayra mutajānifin* (with no inclination) expresses the same concept conveyed elsewhere by the words *ghayra bāghin wa-lā 'ādin* (without willful disobedience nor transgressing). That is, neither desiring it in his heart nor exceeding the absolute minimum required to sustain life. The restriction of *makhmatsah* (severe hunger) clearly shows that where other dietary alternatives are available, no one has the right to make the unlawful lawful merely on the excuse that meat slaughtered according to Shariah is not available, as is the case in most European and American countries. Meat is not indispensable for the survival of life. With other foods, not only life but also health can be maintained at a very high standard. The restriction of 'with no inclination to sin' reflects the reality that a concession is, after all, a concession, and the prohibited remains prohibited. An unlawful thing cannot become as wholesome as mother's milk, nor is a concession an eternal permit. Therefore, it is not permissible for anyone to go beyond the limit of relieving necessity. If a person saves his life through some prohibited thing while observing these restrictions, then Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. If he uses this permission to open up ways for his self-gratification, the responsibility lies on him, and this permission will not serve as an excuse for him on the Day of Resurrection.

Having clarified the individual parts, let us also take a look at the collective structure of the verse. This is the detailed explanation of those prohibitions that were referred to in the first verse by the words 'except that which is recited to you.' First, those things are mentioned whose prohibition had already been stated, and they are repeated here for further emphasis and to complete the discussion. After that, the details of *maytah* (carrion) are given: just as an animal that dies a natural death is carrion, so are animals that die from sudden and accidental mishaps; both carry the same ruling. Similarly, an animal gored or mauled by a wild beast is also carrion, unless you find it alive and slaughter it. In the same way, sacrifices offered at altars and meat distributed through gambling are also unlawful. Just as an animal slaughtered in a name other than Allah's is contaminated with the impurity of polytheism and becomes prohibited, so do these things become prohibited due to seeking the pleasure of other than Allah and through association with gambling. Since the declaration of these prohibitions was equivalent to declaring complete social dissociation from the disbelievers, it was said that the disbelievers have now despaired of you and your religion. They no longer possess the strength to subdue your religion or make it soft. Now, even if they do something, it will only be an exhibition of despair, so do not worry about them; worry only about Me. After this, glad tidings are given to the Muslims that now Allah's religion has reached the point of perfection, your Shariah has reached the stage of completion, and God has approved Islam for you as your religion. Finally, the concession of utilizing the prohibited in a state of necessity to save life is mentioned.

Some narrations indicate that 'This day I have perfected for you...' was revealed during the Farewell Pilgrimage. Our view is that it was revealed in this sequence prior to the Farewell Pilgrimage, but since the public announcement of this glad tiding took place during the Farewell Pilgrimage, some people thought it was revealed on that occasion.

يَسْـــَٔلُوْنَكَ مَاذَآ اُحِلَّ لَهُمْ ۭقُلْ اُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّبٰتُ ۙوَمَا عَلَّمْتُمْ مِّنَ الْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِيْنَ تُعَلِّمُوْنَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللّٰهُ ۡ فَكُلُوْا مِمَّآ اَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَاذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللّٰهِ عَلَيْهِ ۠ وَاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ سَرِيْعُ الْحِسَابِ 

**The Ruling on Game Caught by Trained Animals:** 'They ask you what has been made lawful for them.' The question is reported briefly in accordance with the well-known Quranic style, but the answer indicates that the question was about game caught by trained and taught animals, specifically when they catch the prey and it dies before there is a chance to slaughter it: what is its ruling? This question must have arisen because the preceding verse deemed an animal mauled by a wild beast permissible only if it is found alive and slaughtered.

**A Universal Principle in Matters of Prohibition and Permission:** 'Say, "Lawful for you are the good things."' This is only a part of the answer, which serves as a universal principle. It is also a style of the Quran that when it answers a question, it generally begins with a comprehensive statement so the answer is not limited to the question itself but guides the questioner in a broader scope. Thus, it first stated that 'good things' (*tayyibāt*) are lawful for you. The word *tayyibāt* is the opposite of *khabā'ith* (filthy/evil things). *Tayyibāt* refers to good, clean, and pure things. Since the question is about animals, it refers to animals that, first of all, are inherently good and pure in their nature, temperament, utility, and effects on humans. Secondly, they must be slaughtered in the name of Allah. In this way, all animals that do not align with the wholesome nature of humans are excluded—such as pigs, dogs, monkeys, wild beasts, birds of prey, etc. Or animals that align with human nature but have developed filth or corruption due to an external cause—such as an animal that died on its own, or was slaughtered in a name other than Allah's, or at an altar. These are included in *khabā'ith*. The Quran's answer guides us to the fact that among hunted animals, only the pure ones are lawful; the filthy ones are excluded from this lawfulness.

'And what you have trained of hunting animals, training them as dogs, teaching them of what Allah has taught you.' *Jawārih* refers to hunting animals, whether they are beasts—such as dogs, lions, cheetahs, etc.—or birds, such as falcons and hawks. *Kalb* means dog. From this, the verb *taklīb* was formed, which means training a dog for hunting. Initially, this word was used for this specific meaning, but then its usage became general for training any hunting animal, whether a dog or other hunting beasts and birds. 'Teaching them of what Allah has taught you' expresses the nature of this training—that you have instructed and taught them using the skill and aptitude that Allah has taught you. It is obvious that in training, the trainer's taste, preferences, and objective of training are reflected, and just as a trainee human adopts these, animals also adopt them to the extent of their instinctive capacity. This sets trained animals completely apart from other animals, making the difference between the hunt of an ordinary dog and that of a trained dog a natural matter. In fact, there will even be a difference in inclination and skill between a dog trained by a Muslim and one trained by a Christian. In my view, the words 'teaching them of what Allah has taught you' point to this specific training and discipline that a trained animal receives from its Muslim trainer. Due to this training, the animal becomes an instrument and extension of its trainer, and the game it catches becomes as lawful for him as if slaughtered by his own hand.

**The Sign of a Trained Animal:** 'So eat of what they catch for you.' *Imsāk* means holding and retaining. When it is accompanied by the preposition *'alā* (upon/for), as in 'retain your wife for yourself' (*amsik 'alayka zawjaka*), it also carries the meaning of exclusivity—that is, keeping something for someone specific. This is the actual answer to the question. It states that if the animal is trained according to the aforementioned conditions, then among the game it catches, those that it holds specifically for you are lawful. Since the meaning of exclusivity is found here, I consider more robust the opinion of those scholars who say that if the hunting animal eats from the prey, the game is not lawful. This is also proven by some Ahadith. In my view, there is no strong basis to differentiate between the hunt of beasts and that of birds in this matter. Experienced trainers state that falcons, eagles, and hawks also accept this degree of training just as beasts of prey do.

**The Meaning of 'And Mention the Name of Allah Upon It':** Regarding the referent of the pronoun in the phrase 'and mention the name of Allah upon it,' three views have been narrated from the predecessors (*salaf*). First, mention the name of Allah (recite *Bismillah*) when releasing the hunting animal; according to the proponents of this view, the referent is 'what you have trained.' Second, if the prey is caught alive, slaughter it after reciting *Bismillah*; according to this group, the referent is 'what they catch.' Third, recite *Bismillah* when eating the game; according to these, it is connected to 'so eat.' Supporting the first view is a Hadith recorded in Al-Bukhari from Adi bin Hatim, who said: I asked the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), 'If I release my trained dog on game and another dog joins it, what is the ruling?' He replied, 'Do not eat it, for you mentioned the name of Allah upon your dog, not upon the other dog.'

The weakness in the second view is that since it has already been stated above that game mauled by a beast can be eaten if found alive and slaughtered, repeating the exact same ruling for trained animals would be entirely redundant. The third view, while free from such weakness or difficulty, relates to general dining etiquettes, and its placement here does not seem fitting.

**Hunting as an Economic Necessity for Desert-Dwelling Peoples:** The importance of this question and its answer must be kept in view: hunting in Arabia was not merely a recreational sport but was considered a major source of livelihood. Their livelihood depended on three things: pastoralism, trade, and hunting. Due to this economic importance, the art of training hunting animals had highly developed among them. When Imru' al-Qays describes his hunting dog in his poetry, one is amazed at whether he is describing a dog or a fiery, skilled warrior. This was not unique to the Arabs but is a shared characteristic of all desert-dwelling peoples. For this reason, this question arose in this discussion of lawfulness and prohibition, and the Quran answered it. From this answer, the reality becomes beautifully clear that while observing the limits of lawfulness, prohibition, purity, and impurity, Islam has elevated the status of hunting, the art of hunting, and hunting animals. It elevated the status of a trained beast by declaring its catch lawful even if it dies before slaughter; it elevated the art of training by calling it a part of divine teaching; and it guided that even in training dogs and beasts, a Muslim must keep his specific Islamic perspective in view. Thus, in Islam, the art of hunting has a distinct character compared to that of others.

Finally, by stating 'And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is swift in account,' He reminded them here too of respecting the limits set by Allah and His covenant, warning them not to forget the limits of lawfulness and prohibition in their greed and passion for hunting, for the Day of Account is not far off. This reminder was highly necessary because when hunting is an economic necessity, the potential for negligence is immense.

اَلْيَوْمَ اُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّبٰتُ ۭ وَطَعَامُ الَّذِيْنَ اُوْتُوا الْكِتٰبَ حِلٌّ لَّكُمْ ۠ وَطَعَامُكُمْ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ ۡ وَالْمُحْصَنٰتُ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنٰتِ وَالْمُحْصَنٰتُ مِنَ الَّذِيْنَ اُوْتُوا الْكِتٰبَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ اِذَآ اٰتَيْتُمُوْهُنَّ اُجُوْرَهُنَّ مُحْصِنِيْنَ غَيْرَ مُسٰفِحِيْنَ وَلَا مُتَّخِذِيْٓ اَخْدَانٍ ۭوَمَنْ يَّكْفُرْ بِالْاِيْمَانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهٗ ۡ وَهُوَ فِي الْاٰخِرَةِ مِنَ الْخٰسِرِيْنَ 

There is no difficult word or grammatical structure in this verse. All its components have been discussed in previous Surahs. However, its context and placement need to be properly understood.

This verse is the announcement of the general favor that was to be bestowed upon the entire world in general, and the People of the Book in particular, through the sending of the Seal of the Prophets, Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). In the previous scriptures, the prophecies regarding the sending of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—some of which we referenced in the commentary of Al-Baqarah and Ali 'Imran—clearly state that when the final Prophet comes, he will inform the People of the Book of God's commands and prohibitions regarding pure and filthy things, and will free them from all the restrictions and shackles they had either imposed on themselves or that Allah had imposed on them due to their rebellion. The Quran refers to all these prophecies in Surah Al-A'raf in these words: 'Those who follow the Messenger, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written in what they have of the Torah and the Gospel, who enjoins upon them what is right and forbids them what is wrong and makes lawful for them the good things and prohibits for them the evil things and relieves them of their burden and the shackles which were upon them. So they who have believed in him, honored him, supported him and followed the light which was sent down with him—it is those who will be the successful.' (157)

This refers to the things that were to manifest through the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Indeed, his blessed presence practically confirmed every single one of these details. He made lawful all pure and wholesome things, some of which were forbidden among the Jews; he prohibited all filthy things, some of which the Jews and Christians had made lawful; and he abolished all the restrictions and shackles that they had either placed upon themselves or that were from Allah due to their obstinacy, rebellion, and stubbornness. When these restrictions were lifted following the sending of the unlettered Prophet, Muslims were permitted to eat the food of the People of the Book, as there was no longer any fear of becoming contaminated by anything filthy. Simultaneously, it was declared that your food is lawful for them, because in accordance with Allah's promise, those restrictions that were on them were abolished after the sending of the unlettered Prophet.

**A Question and its Answer:** A question may arise in someone's mind: Were the Jews waiting for the Quran to declare Muslims' food lawful for them? What then was the benefit of this? Is this not like showing unreciprocated generosity? The answer is that the Jews were indeed waiting, and why would they not, when the prophecy of the final Prophet was present in their own scriptures with the clarification that he would save the Children of Israel from all burdens and shackles? However, because this Prophet was sent from among their rivals—the Children of Ishmael—they deliberately, out of jealousy, opposed him, as explained in Al-Baqarah and Ali 'Imran, thereby depriving themselves of all the blessings and favors of which they were the most deserving had they believed in the unlettered Prophet.

Furthermore, even if the Children of Israel were not waiting for it, the promise Allah had made to them had to be fulfilled. Since they had been promised that through the final Prophet, all restrictions on food and drink imposed due to their rebellion would be lifted, Allah fulfilled this promise when the time came by making the food of Muslims lawful for them. As for the fact that they did not appreciate it, that is their own deprivation and misfortune. Why should God forget His promise because of their unworthiness? The sun shines whether one keeps his eyes closed or open. The morning breeze wishes to fragrance every soul, and the demand of its general grace is that it benefits everyone, but those unfortunate ones who close their noses and mouths remain deprived of it. Similarly, the Generous Lord spread the table of His bounty before the entire world through this Ummah, and invited the People of the Book to partake of it as well. Since they did not benefit from it, it is their own misfortune.

**The Food of the People of the Book is Lawful Subject to Islamic Rules of Lawfulness and Prohibition:** From this detail, it becomes clear that Muslims were permitted to benefit from the food and drink of the People of the Book at a time when they had already been informed of the final guidance on this subject, when the lawful and prohibited had been clearly distinguished, and when the innovations of both the People of the Book and the polytheists had been detailed to them. Obviously, the purpose of all this preparation was to tell Muslims that they may maintain social relations with other nations of the world, but within the limits of lawfulness and prohibition established for them. In this verse, the word *al-yawm* (this day) holds special significance. Its meaning is that since you now possess complete distinction between the filthy and the pure, you are being granted this permission. There is no longer a risk that you will fall into something forbidden or doubtful while sitting at their table. (The criterion for the lawfulness of the slaughter of the People of the Book is also the same as mentioned here; in fact, this discussion, as is clear from the context, relates primarily to slaughtered meat).

**The Condition for Marriage with Women of the People of the Book:** Following this, it was stated that just as marriage with chaste, honorable Muslim women is lawful for you, marriage with chaste, honorable women from the People of the Book is also lawful. The word *muhsanāt* is used here. This word appears in the Quran in three different meanings, and we have explained all three meanings elsewhere. Here, the context indicates that it refers to honorable, chaste women of good character. That is, this permission is conditional—on the condition that these women are not unchaste, promiscuous, or of loose character. Just as only the pure things from their table are lawful for you, only the chaste among their women are lawful.

A group of our pious predecessors (*salaf sālih*) considered marriage with women of the People of the Book in the land of war (*Dar al-Harb*) or land of disbelief (*Dar al-Kufr*) to be disliked (*makrūh*). In their view, being in the land of Islam (*Dar al-Islam*) is a condition for its permissibility. I find this opinion to be highly robust. I believe they deduced this from the spirit of the text. I draw attention once again to the word *al-yawm* (this day). This indicates that the circumstances of the time also play a role in this permission. The verses 'This day those who disbelieve have despaired...' and 'This day I have perfected for you...' have already passed, and 'So fear them not, but fear Me' has also been decreed. This shows that in that era, the dominance of the disbelievers had ended, and Muslims had become an invincible power. There was no fear that if they were permitted to marry women of the People of the Book, they would suffer from an inferiority complex and be influenced by them in culture, social life, deeds, or morals. Rather, it was expected that when Muslims married them, they would influence them, leading to a wholesome change in the beliefs and actions of these women, and it was not unlikely that many of them would be honored with faith and Islam.

Moreover, it is also worth noting that the permission to marry women of the People of the Book was granted as a concession (*'ala sabīl at-tanzīl*). The danger it poses to the faith of the man himself, his children, and his family is not hidden. Indeed, this is why Muslim men are permitted to marry women of the Book, but a Muslim woman is under no circumstances permitted to marry a non-Muslim, whether he is from the People of the Book or not. This is proof that this is merely a permission; it is not something highly recommended. If the environment is one of Islamic culture and society and a man marries a chaste woman of the Book, there is no harm. However, in a disbelieving environment where disbelief and its adherents are dominant, such a marriage, even if not contrary to the literal words of this verse, is certainly contrary to its spirit, intent, and context.

There is little need to remind here that many Islamic laws are conditioned upon the state of *Dar al-Islam*. Similarly, some concessions and permissions are also conditioned upon a specific environment and specific circumstances. Some important points regarding this sequence will be mentioned ahead.

We have discussed *muhsinīna ghayra musāfihīna* (desiring chastity, not unlawful sexual intercourse) in detail under Surah An-Nisa, verses 24-25.

**The Meaning of 'Denying the Faith' (Kufr bil-Iman):** 'And whoever denies the faith—his work has become worthless.' Denying the faith means that a person acts in open violation of the commands of God and His Messenger, creating laws and Shariah based on his desires and practicing them. This is the same type of faith that the Quran describes as 'We believe in some and disbelieve in some.' Such a mixture of disbelief and faith has no value with God. Only that faith is acceptable to Allah which is on Allah's terms. The faith of those who believe on their own terms will be thrown back in their faces, and all the deeds performed under such faith will go to waste. This has also been discussed previously.

The upcoming subject (Verses 6 to 11): Having purified foods and marriages by mentioning the pure and filthy things in those areas, the purification for prayer is now mentioned. The purification for prayer is wudu (ablution), and in the state of ritual impurity (janabah), ghusl (bathing). In this connection, permission for tayammum (dry ablution) is granted in the absence of water or due to a valid excuse, which represents the completion of favor upon this Ummah, and is the perfecting ruling of this chapter.

Following this, while reminding of this completion of favor, He warned: Always remember the covenant you entered into with God by saying 'We hear and we obey.' Fear Allah; nothing is hidden from Him, and He is fully aware of the secrets of the hearts. After that, He reminded of the position of establishing justice and being witnesses over mankind, on which Allah has appointed the Muslims, so it may become clear that they are now the balance of truth and justice in this world. If they deviate, everything will become crooked. In this connection, reference is also made to the greatest motive for deviation, which proved to be a stumbling block for past nations, so this Ummah may remain fully alert against it. Along with this, it is made clear that if you remain steadfast on truth and justice for both friend and foe, the ultimate great reward in the Hereafter is for you, and in this world too, you will remain successful and triumphant; your enemies will not be able to harm you in the least. In the light of this, recite the upcoming verses:

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْٓا اِذَا قُمْتُمْ اِلَى الصَّلٰوةِ فَاغْسِلُوْا وُجُوْهَكُمْ وَاَيْدِيَكُمْ اِلَى الْمَرَافِقِ وَامْسَحُوْا بِرُءُوْسِكُمْ وَاَرْجُلَكُمْ اِلَى الْكَعْبَيْنِ ۭ وَاِنْ كُنْتُمْ جُنُبًا فَاطَّهَّرُوْا ۭ وَاِنْ كُنْتُمْ مَّرْضٰٓى اَوْ عَلٰي سَفَرٍ اَوْ جَاۗءَ اَحَدٌ مِّنْكُمْ مِّنَ الْغَاۗىِٕطِ اَوْ لٰمَسْتُمُ النِّسَاۗءَ فَلَمْ تَجِدُوْا مَاۗءً فَتَيَمَّمُوْا صَعِيْدًا طَيِّبًا فَامْسَحُوْا بِوُجُوْهِكُمْ وَاَيْدِيْكُمْ مِّنْهُ ۭ مَا يُرِيْدُ اللّٰهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِّنْ حَرَجٍ وَّلٰكِنْ يُّرِيْدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهٗ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُوْنَ 

**The Method of Obtaining Purification Through Wudu:** 'O you who have believed, when you rise to [perform] prayer, wash your faces and your forearms to the elbows.' When the verb *qāma* is followed by the preposition *ilā*, it means to intend. That is, when you intend to pray, obtain purification for it. Then He explained the method of this purification, which we have discussed previously and which is detailed in the books of jurisprudence. It is proven by the Sunnah of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and both reason and human nature bear witness, that purification once obtained remains intact until a state of invalidation occurs. Therefore, this guidance is for the state when a person's wudu is no longer intact; if it is intact, fresh wudu is not required. If someone performs a fresh wudu to gain spiritual refreshment, it is a virtuous act but not a requirement of the Shariah. As for the questions of how to wash, whether the mentioned parts should be washed once, twice, or thrice, whether they should be rubbed or just have water poured over them, and what to do regarding the temples, beard, and elbows—these relate to etiquettes (*ādāb*) rather than fundamental rulings, and the best way to learn these etiquettes is the Sunnah of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Whatever practices are proven from his Sunnah, even if their forms vary, all contain goodness and blessing.

'And wipe over your heads.' *Masah* means to pass the wet hand over, and the letter *bā* in such contexts indicates encompassing. For this reason, I find more robust the school of thought of those who advocate for wiping the entire head. However, in the case of wearing a turban, etc., wiping a partial portion of the head is also sufficient to relieve hardship.

'And your feet to the ankles.' This is coordinate (*'atf*) with 'your forearms' (*aydiyakum*). Therefore, it is included among those parts for which washing is commanded. It has been placed after the mention of wiping the head to clarify the order of the parts in wudu. This indicates that this order is both natural and prescribed by Shariah. Some have placed it under the ruling of wiping (*masah*), but this view is contrary to the mutawatir recitation, the mutawatir Sunnah, and Arabic grammar. If it were a wipe of the feet, the restriction of 'to the ankles' would have been entirely unnecessary. See how in wudu, He placed the restriction 'to the elbows' for washing the arms, but in tayammum where wiping is commanded, the restriction 'to the elbows' was dropped, because such a restriction in wiping would be entirely useless.

'And if you are in a state of janabah, then purify yourselves...' This segment, with minor variations in wording, passed in Surah An-Nisa, verse 43, where we discussed it in detail.

**The Wisdom and Reason Behind the Rulings of Wudu and Tayammum:** 'Allah does not intend to make difficulty for you...' This explains the wisdom and reason behind this ruling, so that if some people felt these restrictions were difficult, their benefits would become clear to them. There is no doubt that for undisciplined temperaments, the restriction of ghusl and wudu is quite demanding and a significant trial. However, Allah did not impose this restriction to put His servants into hardship. If hardship were intended, why would the permission for tayammum be granted during illness and travel? Rather, it was imposed to purify the servants so they may become worthy of attaining nearness to God and His holy assemblies. Ultimately, it is the purity of the inner self that is desired, and prayer was prescribed for this very inner purity. However, there is a deep connection between the outward and the inward; the outward affects the inward, and the inward affects the outward. For this reason, Islam has commanded wudu for prayer, and ghusl in the state of ritual impurity. This aids in attaining that inner purity which is the actual objective of prayer.

'And to complete His favor upon you' indicates that with these rulings of wudu and tayammum concerning prayer, Allah's favor upon you was completed. While discussing tayammum in Surah An-Nisa, we pointed out that the Jews had extremely harsh restrictions and constraints regarding purification. First of all, the rulings of their Shariah were inherently demanding, and then their jurists added further rigidity. Tayammum was entirely foreign to them, and they considered it a supreme act of irreligion for someone to suffice with mere tayammum and pray while in a state of minor or major ritual impurity, regardless of the severity of their excuse or constraint. Consequently, when the permission for tayammum was revealed in the Quran, they not only mocked it but went so far as to claim that the polytheists of Mecca were more guided than these Muslims. These rigidities of the Jews were also among those burdens and shackles that were to be removed through the Seal of the Prophets (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Thus, the permission of tayammum completed Allah's favor in the domain of purification, and for the ease and blessings this completion of favor brought to this Ummah, gratitude is due at every moment.

وَاذْكُرُوْا نِعْمَةَ اللّٰهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَمِيْثَاقَهُ الَّذِيْ وَاثَقَكُمْ بِهٖٓ ۙ اِذْ قُلْتُمْ سَمِعْنَا وَاَطَعْنَا ۡ وَاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ عَلِيْمٌۢ بِذَاتِ الصُّدُوْرِ 

Now, the duty toward this completion of favor is stated: Do not forget, like the Jews did, the favor and grace Allah has bestowed upon you by revealing His final and complete Shariah, and the honor and elevation He has granted you. Rather, always keep it in mind. 'Keep it in mind' in its true sense—that is, fulfilling its right both outwardly and inwardly. After that, He clarified the nature of this responsibility: it stands as a firm covenant between you and your Lord, which He took from you through His Messenger (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and you undertook the responsibility of this covenant by saying 'We hear and we obey' in front of the Prophet. The promises Allah has made to you for success in this world and the Hereafter are contingent upon this covenant. If you break it, the punishment is extremely severe. Therefore, fear Allah, and remember that God is aware of the secrets of the hearts.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا كُوْنُوْا قَوّٰمِيْنَ لِلّٰهِ شُهَدَاۗءَ بِالْقِسْطِ ۡ وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَاٰنُ قَوْمٍ عَلٰٓي اَلَّا تَعْدِلُوْا ۭاِعْدِلُوْا ۣ هُوَ اَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوٰى ۡ وَاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ خَبِيْرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُوْنَ 

**The Collective Responsibility of the Shariah Covenant on the Muslim Ummah:** 'O you who have believed, be persistently standing firm for Allah, witnesses in justice.' With a slight variation in wording, this segment passed in Surah An-Nisa, verse 135, where it was explained. Here, the collective responsibility of this covenant is clarified: it is the responsibility of Muslims as a Muslim Ummah to become the standard-bearers of this truth and justice which has been given to them in the form of this final Shariah. They must establish it within themselves and bear witness to it before the world.

'And let not the hatred of a people... lead you...' This segment passed in verse 2 of this very Surah. Here, warning is given against the greatest trial on the path of truth and justice: that the enmity of any nation and their wrong behavior must not succeed in turning us away from truth and justice. The primary tool Satan has used to lead people astray from the path of truth is mutual enmity. The Jews, purely out of enmity toward the Children of Ishmael and the Muslims, threw away the entire covenant of which they were made witnesses and guardians. For this reason, a covenant was taken from the Muslims that they must protect themselves from this trial of Satan. They must possess only one weight and one scale for both friends and foes. 

'Be just; that is nearer to righteousness.' This very justice is nearest to piety. That is, piety—which is the essence of the entire religion and Shariah, and the criterion for every word and deed of the believers—demands that despite the enemy's enmity, no matter should be dealt with in a manner devoid of justice and truth. This highlights the position of piety (*taqwa*) in religion: that all virtues actually stem from its root.

9-10: وَعَدَ اللّٰهُ الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا وَعَمِلُوا الصّٰلِحٰتِ ۙ لَهُمْ مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَّاَجْرٌ عَظِيْمٌ۔ وَالَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا وَكَذَّبُوْا بِاٰيٰتِنَآ اُولٰۗىِٕكَ اَصْحٰبُ الْجَحِيْمِ 

This states the outcome of fulfilling or not fulfilling the aforementioned covenant: those who remain firm on this covenant will have forgiveness and a great reward, while those who break it will have Hellfire. From this, one point that emerges is that just as Allah has placed the responsibility of this covenant upon us, He has also taken upon Himself a corresponding responsibility in return. This is expressed by the words 'Allah has promised.' What immense benevolence of the Generous Lord that He enters into an agreement with His own created and nurtured creation, and in return takes a responsibility upon His own Self! This is an honor bestowed upon humans in which no other creature shares. The second point is that 'faith and righteous deeds' is a comprehensive term that encompasses everything the Lord has given us in the form of His Shariah and to which we have pledged our adherence.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوا اذْكُرُوْا نِعْمَتَ اللّٰهِ عَلَيْكُمْ اِذْ هَمَّ قَوْمٌ اَنْ يَّبْسُطُوْٓا اِلَيْكُمْ اَيْدِيَهُمْ فَكَفَّ اَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنْكُمْ ۚ وَاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ ۭ وَعَلَي اللّٰهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُوْنَ 

In verse 8 above, where the covenant was taken from Muslims to stand firm on truth and justice under all circumstances, and to uphold and bear witness to it despite the opponents, there was an indication that you must not worry about the opposition of the opponents. If you remain firm on this covenant, God's help and support will be with you at every step, and your enemies will not be able to harm you. The exact same concept passed in 'So fear them not, but fear Me.' Now, in support of this, historical evidence is presented: look, a people attempted to obstruct you on this path, but they could not harm you; God bound their hands. Similarly, if you remain firm on the covenant of your Lord, God will help you against every nation that rises against you. Since you have believed in God, the requirement of your faith is that you place your trust in your Lord.

In this verse, the reference of 'a people' (*qawm*) is, in my view, to the Quraysh. In verses 3 and 8 above, the reference is also to them. The indefiniteness (*tankīr*) of the word also points to a depreciation of their status, and indicates that the Speaker's focus is on stating a reality rather than identifying a specific nation. Nonetheless, as an indication, as I have submitted, it refers to the Quraysh. From the contents of this Surah, as we hinted above, it can be estimated that this is a Surah from the era when Muslims had become a political power. By the sixth or seventh year of Hijrah, circumstances had developed such that the Quraysh, after multiple trials of strength, were forced to make a treaty with the Muslims, and the Jews had also lost heart after the bitter experiences of their failed secret conspiracies.

The upcoming subject (Verses 12 to 14): Next, the covenant taken from the Jews and Christians is mentioned, along with a brief reference to the consequences they faced by breaking that covenant. The purpose is to warn Muslims that the covenant being taken from you now is of the same nature, and if you deal with it the same way the Jews and Christians did, you will face the same outcome they faced. Recite the verses:

وَلَقَدْ اَخَذَ اللّٰهُ مِيْثَاقَ بَنِيْٓ اِ سْرَاۗءِيْلَ ۚ وَبَعَثْنَا مِنْهُمُ اثْنَيْ عَشَرَ نَقِيْبًا ۭوَقَالَ اللّٰهُ اِنِّىْ مَعَكُمْ ۭلَىِٕنْ اَقَمْــتُمُ الصَّلٰوةَ وَاٰتَيْتُمُ الزَّكٰوةَ وَاٰمَنْتُمْ بِرُسُلِيْ وَعَزَّرْتُمُوْهُمْ وَاَقْرَضْتُمُ اللّٰهَ قَرْضًا حَسَـنًا لَّاُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّاٰتِكُمْ وَلَاُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنّٰتٍ تَجْرِيْ مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْاَنْھٰرُ ۚ فَمَنْ كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذٰلِكَ مِنْكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَاۗءَ السَّبِيْلِ 

**The Meaning of 'Naqib':** *Naqīb* means an investigator, one who tracks down matters, or inquires into people's conditions. From this, it came to be used in the sense of a chief, leader, guardian officer, or monitor of a tribe or nation, because the primary duty of guardians and monitors is to oversee people's conditions and protect them. The history of the Children of Israel shows that Prophet Moses (peace be upon him), after taking the covenant of adhering to and protecting the Shariah from them, appointed one *naqīb* over each tribe of the Children of Israel to oversee that the people adhered to the limits and boundaries of the Shariah, and that nothing entered among them that would turn them away from God's covenant. Since the tribes of the Children of Israel were twelve, twelve *naqībs* were appointed. Since Moses (peace be upon him) appointed them under the guidance of Allah, Allah attributed this act to Himself.

**The Covenant with the Children of Israel:** 'And Allah said, "Indeed, I am with you..."' This is the statement of the covenant taken from the Children of Israel. It took a covenant upon the establishment of prayer, the payment of zakat, believing in the messengers to come, supporting them, and spending in the way of God. In return, Allah promised them His companionship, forgiveness for their general lapses, and Paradise for them. 'And Allah said, "Indeed, I am with you"' is a comprehensive expression of the promise of divine support and help. Obviously, those with whom God is have the entire universe with them. 

'If you establish prayer and give zakat' shows that just as prayer and zakat hold fundamental importance in the covenant of the Muslim Ummah, they held fundamental importance in the covenant of the Children of Israel as well. The status of these two from the perspective of divine wisdom in religion was discussed in detail at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah. 'And believe in My messengers' refers to all the messengers to come afterward, but it has a specific reference to the final Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), whose mention appeared in the Torah with highly distinct signs. Some references passed in Al-Baqarah, and further discussion will come in Al-A'raf. 

'And loan Allah a goodly loan' refers to spending that is distinct from the prescribed zakat. It refers to spending carried out for Jihad in the cause of Allah and other such religious, national, and collective objectives. The reason for calling it a loan and the conditions for it being a 'goodly loan' have been discussed in detail elsewhere. 'I will surely remove from you your misdeeds'—here, *sayyi'āt* (misdeeds) refers to lapses and shortcomings. If the fundamental aspects of religion are cared for, Allah forgives the minor errors committed by a servant. This issue has also been discussed previously. 'And whoever disbelieves after that...'—this segment is also part of the agreement, meaning that despite this covenant and the arrangements for its protection, if anyone deviates from this agreement, they have strayed from God's highway. It should be noted here that deviation from this covenant is termed disbelief (*kufr*).

فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِمْ مِّيْثَاقَهُمْ لَعَنّٰهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَا قُلُوْبَهُمْ قٰسِـيَةً ۚ يُحَرِّفُوْنَ الْكَلِمَ عَنْ مَّوَاضِعِهٖ ۙ وَنَسُوْا حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوْا بِهٖ ۚ وَلَا تَزَالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلٰي خَاۗىِٕنَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ اِلَّا قَلِيْلًا مِّنْهُمْ فَاعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَاصْفَحْ ۭ اِنَّ اللّٰهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِيْنَ 

**The Consequences of the Jews' Covenant-Breaking:** The curse upon the Jews, their hardness of heart, and their distortions (*tahrīf*) have been discussed in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Just as Allah honors a nation with elevation and status by making a covenant with them, He also repels them with similar humiliation if they break the covenant. The comprehensive term for this repelling is 'curse' (*la'nah*), which means declaring someone expelled from divine mercy. The first effect of being expelled from divine mercy that befalls a nation is that the fear of God—which guarantees the life of the heart—is removed from within them, and the heart becomes like stone, entirely barren to the growth of repentance and turning to God. Although this state arises as a result of the covenant-breaking nation's own actions, it occurs according to Allah's established law, which is why Allah attributed it to Himself. This hardness of heart breeds audacity within the covenant-breaking nation, resulting in them not merely violating the divine covenant but also distorting its words and kalamat to fit their desires. The various forms in which the Jews carried out this distortion were detailed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah.

'And they forgot a portion of that of which they were reminded.' In our view, 'that of which they were reminded' refers to the Torah, because it was in the Torah that the entire record of the divine covenant was preserved, specifically so it would serve as a reliable reminder for the Children of Israel and their future generations. But when they forgot a portion of this very reminder, what else did they have left to remind them? A lamp of the house is what is used for light; if it is extinguished or hidden, what other thing will bring light?

This forgetting is the natural result of distortion and concealment. The Jews used to hide certain parts of the Torah from the common people, as clarified in the commentary of Al-Baqarah. Similarly, regarding the prophecies of the Torah that were contrary to their desires, they carried out verbal distortions to change their meaning. Through biased interpretation (*ta'wīl*), they also transformed the essence of realities. To make matters worse, the Torah was not compiled during the lifetime of Moses; rather, its compilation took place long after his death, when no one even knew where his grave was. Even the names of its compilers and their qualities are unknown. In Deuteronomy, chapter 34, it is written: 'but no man knoweth of his sepulchre unto this day.' Similarly, these words are also found there: 'And there arose not a prophet since in Israel like unto Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face.' Obviously, for a book compiled such a long time after its bringer's death that people had even forgotten his grave, how was it possible for them to preserve its teachings? Consequently, they forgot many things of the Torah. Furthermore, even the things that were compiled were not preserved in their original words; they were left with mere translations instead of the original Torah, and these translations changed so much over time that it became impossible to distinguish how much of the original remained and how much was the commentary of the compilers and translators. Thus, a very large part of the Torah was lost by its custodians.

'And you will still observe deceit from them.' *Khā'inah* means deceit or betrayal, just as *lā'imah* means blame. Deceit is well known in Arabic for betrayal and breaking of covenants. The Quran has exposed many of the Jews' betrayals and distortions, details of which passed in previous Surahs, and further examples are coming ahead. It is also known from the Quran that it only addressed those distortions whose exposure was necessary from the perspective of renewing the Shariah; those that were not necessary to address were ignored, and their quantity is also very large. Thus, ahead in verse 15, it is declared: 'making clear to you much of what you used to conceal of the Scripture and overlooking much.'

'Except a few of them' refers to that small group of Jews who remained safe from the breaking of the covenant and its aforementioned consequences, although this group could neither save their nation from trials nor protect the Torah from the hands of the wicked. Nonetheless, to the extent of their knowledge, they remained firm on the original Shariah and bore witness to it. It is this righteous group that welcomed Islam. In 'So pardon them and overlook,' pardoning and overlooking do not mean forgiving from the heart but merely passing over. A parallel for this meaning of pardon is found in the segment of verse 15 quoted above. The meaning is: ignore them for now and give them time; the time to deal with them will come ahead.

وَمِنَ الَّذِيْنَ قَالُوْٓا اِنَّا نَصٰرٰٓى اَخَذْنَا مِيْثَاقَهُمْ فَنَسُوْا حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوْا بِهٖ ۠ فَاَغْرَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ الْعَدَاوَةَ وَالْبَغْضَاۗءَ اِلٰي يَوْمِ الْقِيٰمَةِ ۭ وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ اللّٰهُ بِمَا كَانُوْا يَصْنَعُوْنَ 

**The Consequences of the Christians' Covenant-Breaking:** Following the breaking of the covenant by the Jews, the breaking of the covenant by the Christians is now mentioned. The introduction to their mention is set in a style that reveals that these Christians, in the view of the Quran, are not true followers of Christ but merely claimants to being Christians. Indeed, the followers of Paul have no relation to the original teachings of Christ; they even changed their name. In Al-Baqarah, a detailed discussion on the Christians passed. Further discussion is coming under verses 82-85 of this Surah.

'So We caused among them animosity and hatred...' This is the stated outcome of distorting the Divine Book and losing a portion of it. The integration of a nation is achieved only through the covenant of Allah and His Book. If corruption and disorder creep into it, what else can save the nation from discord, division, and bloodshed? This situation is both a natural consequence of covenant-breaking and a punishment for this crime, which is why Allah attributed it to Himself. The only way for the Christians to escape this was to accept the guidance of the Quran, emerging from these darknesses into the light of guidance and the path of peace. But their prejudice prevented them from taking this straight path. Since no other book or messenger is to come, there is no longer any possibility for them to escape this mutual conflict until the Day of Resurrection. 'And Allah will soon inform them' is a threat, meaning that soon the time will come when Allah will place all these doings of theirs before them, and they will see the consequences of all their mischief with their own eyes. It should be kept in mind that this history of covenant-breaking is not narrated to the Muslims merely as a story of the past, but so they may take a lesson from it and remember that if they deal with their covenant the same way the Jews and Christians did, they will face the same fate.

The upcoming subject (Verses 15 to 19): The Way of Salvation for the Jews and Christians: Next, both the Jews and Christians are addressed by way of warning: You broke Allah's covenant and lost His books, falling into darkness and religious conflict. To extract you from this and bring you to the path of peace, Allah has shown you light again and sent a clear Book. Now, after this Book and this Messenger, no excuse remains for you. If you remain in this darkness even after this completion of proof, remember that Allah is powerful over all things, and no one can escape His grasp. Recite the verses:

**Commentary on Verses 15 to 16:**

**The Word 'Concealment' (Ikhfa) is a Comprehensive Expression for Distortion:** 'making clear to you much of what you used to conceal of the Scripture and overlooking much.' Whether distortion is of the nature of changing words or changing meanings, its actual purpose is to cover up the truth and throw dust in the eyes of the people. For this reason, the Quran used the comprehensive word *ikhfā'* (concealment), which covers both their verbal and conceptual distortions, as well as those verses of their books that the scholars of the People of the Book used to hide from the common people out of fear that their actions contrary to the Shariah would be exposed, or that a proof regarding the final prophet would be established against them. It stated: This Messenger is exposing many of your distortions, and there are also many that he is overlooking, because the purpose is to manifest the original truth and renew and complete the Shariah, not to humiliate or disgrace you.

**The Meaning of 'Light' and 'Clear Book':** 'There has come to you from Allah a light and a clear Book.' 'Light' refers to the Quran, and 'clear Book' is used by way of explanation. The Quran is a collection of both wisdom and Shariah. It extracts from intellectual darkness and also defines the correct path of action for life, which is why it is both a light and a clear Book. The People of the Book had lost the light Allah had granted them and were surrounded by darkness again; the original truth was lost and the highway was missing, resulting in the fire of conflict and war breaking out among them, as mentioned above.

**Seeking and Searching is a Condition for Guidance:** 'With which Allah guides...' This explains the purpose of this Book: if you believe in it, Allah will guide you through it, saving you from this conflict and bringing you to paths of peace and safety, from darkness into light, and from the valleys of deviation and error to the straight path of God—on the condition that the desire for God's pleasure is present within you, and you remove the bandages of prejudice from your eyes to see this light and appreciate it. This points to the fact that those who do not search for the pleasure of Allah but remain worshipers of their desires do not have the door of facilitation opened for them. The word *bi-idhnihi* (by His permission) points to this very Sunnah of Allah. That is, this blessing is attained by God's permission, and this permission is for those who search for the pleasure of God.

لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِيْنَ قَالُوْٓا اِنَّ اللّٰهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيْحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۭ قُل *فَمَنْ يَّمْلِكُ مِنَ اللّٰهَ شَـيْـــًٔـا اِنْ اَرَادَ اَنْ يُّهْلِكَ الْمَسِيْحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَاُمَّهٗ وَمَنْ فِي الْاَرْضِ جَمِيْعًا ۭوَلِلّٰهِ مُلْكُ السَّمٰوٰتِ وَالْاَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۭ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَاۗءُ ۭوَاللّٰهُ عَلٰي كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيْرٌ

**The Actual Core of Paul's Belief:** Now, an example of the Christians' covenant-breaking is stated: they struck at the very root of the entire covenant—monotheism (*tawhīd*)—and made Jesus, the son of Mary, God instead of Allah. Modern Christianity, founded by Paul, is fundamentally based on the belief of incarnation (*hulūl*) and union (*ittihād*)—that is, in Paul's view, God Himself manifested in the form of Christ. However, to escape certain objections, as we hinted in Surah An-Nisa, he presented it in the form of a riddle called the Trinity. Incarnation is the soul of this belief, and the Trinity is its expression. The Quran has sometimes clarified the core of this deviation and sometimes pointed to it using its well-known expression. Paul's primary objective was to prove Christ as God. For this, he gathered materials from his extraordinary birth and his being the Word of Allah and a Spirit from Him. However, since the Father and the Holy Spirit were also mentioned in the Gospels, he had to work to choose an expression for the concept of divinity so that a place could be found for all of them. But this entire web remains only in the minds of some hair-splitters or within some riddle-like books; the common mind prefers simplification and does not enter into these subtleties. Consequently, the masses simplified this entire fable into the belief that Christ is God. The same happened with the Arabs: they also associated others with God under a presumed philosophy, but then the forms and idols became gods, and God either disappeared entirely or was pushed into the background so that His existence and non-existence became equal. A detailed discussion on this will come in the Meccan Surahs.

**Expression of Anger Against the Christians:** 'Say, "Then who could prevent Allah at all..."'—this means that nothing can obstruct God's intention. For example, 'I will ask forgiveness for you, but I do not possess for you from Allah anything.' This is an expression of anger from Allah against making Christ God. The meaning is: O foolish ones, what kind of talk is this? What Christ, what his mother, and what this entire creation? God is independent of and free from need of all. He is the One who granted existence to all, and if He willed to destroy all of them, who is there to hold His hand? The kingdom of the heavens and the earth and all that is between them belongs to God alone. No one is His partner in it. He creates what He wills, how He wills, and in whatever measure He wills. Someone's birth without a father does not become proof that he has become God or a partner in divinity. If God wills, He can create without a father, and indeed He can create without both mother and father; He is powerful over all things.

وَقَالَتِ الْيَھُوْدُ وَالنَّصٰرٰى نَحْنُ اَبْنٰۗؤُا اللّٰهِ وَاَحِبَّاۗؤُهٗ ۭقُلْ فَلِمَ يُعَذِّبُكُمْ بِذُنُوْبِكُمْ ۭ بَلْ اَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقَ ۭيَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَّشَاۗءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَّشَاۗءُ ۭوَلِلّٰهِ مُلْكُ السَّمٰوٰتِ وَالْاَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۡ وَاِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيْرُ

**The Vain Pretension of the People of the Book Being God's Beloved:** 'We are the children of Allah and His beloved.' This segment was discussed in detail in the previous Surahs. It was this vain pretension of the People of the Book that made them most indifferent to the responsibilities of the divine covenant. They assumed that because they were the descendants of God's beloved and chosen ones, they were free from the responsibilities of action and obedience. Paradise was their birthright. As for Hell, they would either not be cast into it at all, or if they were, it would only be for a few days. The primary originators of this trial were the Jews, but why would the Christians let them be the sole monopolizers of Paradise? Thus, the Quran mentioned this here as a shared belief of both.

**Refutation of the Vain Pretension from Their Own History:** 'Say, "Then why does He punish you for your sins?"' This refutes their vain pretension using their own history. The meaning is: if you are free from God's accountability and punishment because of being His beloved and chosen ones, why did this beloved status not benefit you in this world? Here, your entire history bears witness that whenever you rebelled against God, He inflicted highly exemplary punishments upon you—so exemplary that no parallel to such punishments can be found in the history of any other nation. The enslavement of the entire nation, the wandering of the entire nation in the desert, the exile of the entire nation, the repeated massacres of the entire nation, and the exemplary destruction of Jerusalem—all these events are present in the Torah itself. If you possess a clearance certificate from God due to being the descendants of Abraham and Isaac, why did this clearance certificate not save you from these punishments?

**Declaration of the True Reality:** 'Nay, you are but human beings from among those He has created.' This is the declaration of the true reality: emerge from the delusion of being the children and beloved of Allah; you are His creation just as the rest of His creation is, and just as everyone else attains a connection to God through faith and righteous deeds, you too can attain any connection to Him only through faith and righteous deeds. 'He forgives whom He wills and punishes whom He wills'—that is, forgiveness and punishment are solely in God's authority. He will forgive those whom He finds deserving of forgiveness, and punish those whom He finds deserving of punishment. If someone breaks God's covenant while relying on their connection to righteous ancestors or on their presumed intercession, no one will be able to save them from God's punishment. 'And to Allah belongs the kingdom...'—this emphasizes the preceding statement: the heavens, the earth, and all that is between them are God's property, and to Him is the final return of all. There is no partnership for anyone in this universe, nor is there to be an appearance before anyone other than God, so that hope may be attached to them.

يٰٓاَهْلَ الْكِتٰبِ قَدْ جَاۗءَكُمْ رَسُوْلُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلٰي فَتْرَةٍ مِّنَ الرُّسُلِ اَنْ تَقُوْلُوْا مَا جَاۗءَنَا مِنْۢ بَشِيْرٍ وَلَا نَذِيْرٍ ۡ فَقَدْ جَاۗءَكُمْ بَشِيْرٌ وَنَذِيْرٌ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ عَلٰي كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيْرٌ

**The Meaning of 'Fatrah':** *Fatrah* refers to the interval that occurs between the manifestation of a thing and its subsequent manifestation. For example, the interval between two attacks of a periodic fever is called *fatrah*. In the verse, it refers to the interval of time that occurs between the sending of two prophets.

**Warning to the People of the Book:** This is a warning to the People of the Book: Our Messenger has come to you, clarifying all those things of your book that you concealed or lost, and guiding you to the straight path of God. Now, you no longer have the excuse to persist in your deviation that a long time had passed since the sending of previous messengers and you were in need of a bringer of glad tidings and a warner. To eliminate this excuse of yours, We sent a bringer of glad tidings and a warner. If you still do not change your path after this completion of proof, do not think of God as helpless; He is powerful over all things. It should be kept in mind that this excuse of the People of the Book was not a highly valid excuse, but when Allah took even this into consideration, it means that proof was completed against them in the highest degree.

The upcoming subject (Verses 20 to 26):

**Reminding the Children of Israel of an Important Event in Their History:** Next, a reminder of an important event from the early history of the Jews is given, which, on one hand, brings to light the reality that this nation has been highly weak and deficient from the very beginning regarding God's covenant and its rights and duties. On the other hand, it also clarifies that God has always inflicted highly exemplary punishments upon them for their covenant-breaking and unworthiness from the very beginning, so they are not safe from the punishment of the Hereafter. In this light, recite the verses:

20-21: وَاِذْ قَالَ مُوْسٰى لِقَوْمِهٖ يٰقَوْمِ اذْكُرُوْا نِعْمَتَ اللّٰهِ عَلَيْكُمْ اِذْ جَعَلَ فِيْكُمْ اَنْۢبِيَاۗءَ وَجَعَلَكُمْ مُّلُوْكًا ڰ وَّاٰتٰىكُمْ مَّا لَمْ يُؤْتِ اَحَدًا مِّنَ الْعٰلَمِيْنَ ۔ يٰقَوْمِ ادْخُلُوا الْاَرْضَ الْمُقَدَّسَةَ الَّتِيْ كَتَبَ اللّٰهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَرْتَدُّوْا عَلٰٓي اَدْبَارِكُمْ فَتَنْقَلِبُوْا خٰسِرِيْنَ 

**A Page from the History of the Jews:** 'When He appointed among you prophets and made you kings and gave you what He had not given to anyone among the worlds.' We explained under Surah An-Nisa, verse 54, that Allah's promises relating to the future are sometimes expressed in the past tense. This is an eloquent style used to express the absolute certainty of promises, which is frequently used in the Quran. It is as if these promises are not mere promises but events that have already occurred. Although some prophets had been sent among the Children of Israel before Moses, the continuous chain of prophethood began after him and lasted until the sending of Christ. The succession of kings also belongs entirely to the era after Moses. Before that, the patriarchs of the family possessed a kind of leadership and fatherly authority, but it cannot be called kingship, nor is it termed kingship in the Torah.

**A Political Point:** Here, another difference in the style is also worth noting. For the chain of prophethood, He said: 'He appointed among you prophets' (*ja'ala fīkum anbiyā'*). But for the succession of kingship, He chose the expression: 'and made you kings' (*ja'alakum mulūkan*).
