From the difference between these two styles, it emerges that prophethood is a station of specific selection, restricted only to the one whom Allah appoints to this office; others do not share in it. In contrast, kingship is a collective office in which the entire nation shares with the king. If a nation does not share in a kingship, it is tyranny and despotism. 'And gave you what He had not given to anyone among the worlds' refers to that office of leadership and bearing witness to the truth on which Allah had appointed the Children of Israel, and which was not possessed by anyone other than them prior to the appearance of the Muslim Ummah.

**The Holy Land Refers to Canaan and Palestine:** 'the holy land which Allah has assigned to you.' 'The Holy Land' refers to the region of Canaan and Palestine. It is called holy because it is the region where Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (peace be upon them) initiated the call to Allah's religion. Although this region later fell into the hands of disbelievers and idolaters, the call to monotheism and God-worship first resounded in this area, which is why it was termed the Holy Land. After emerging from Egypt, Allah designated this region as the inheritance of the Children of Israel, and the Torah indicates that Allah swore an oath promising them: I have given this land to you. See Numbers, chapter 13.

**A Speech of Prophet Moses:** This refers to the speech Moses (peace be upon him) delivered in the Wilderness of Paran on the occasion when he urged the Children of Israel to invade Palestine. From the study of the Torah, Book of Numbers, chapters 13-14, it is known that after emerging from Egypt and traversing all the stages of the journey, when Moses arrived in the Wilderness of Paran and the region of Palestine drew near—this region being the destination—he sent a party of 12 leaders to investigate the conditions of the land. When this party returned after completing its mission, it gave a highly encouraging report regarding the fertility and lushness of the land, but the description it gave regarding the stature and strength of the inhabitants occupying the land proved to be highly discouraging to the Children of Israel. Upon hearing this report, they began to wail, and far from invading the land to occupy it—the very hope that had brought them there—they began talking about returning to Egypt. They completely forgot that God had promised to give them this land as an inheritance with an oath. Two of the members of the investigative mission, identified in the Torah as Joshua and Caleb, tried hard to encourage them and referred to God's promises and the fruits of resolve and courage, but the Children of Israel, instead of showing courage to invade Palestine, sought to stone these two brave men.

It was on this occasion that Moses delivered this speech. Although the Quran has only presented a summary of the speech, because the purpose was merely to point to the event briefly, all the necessary aspects are present in it to restore courage and warn against the evil consequences of cowardice. Moses referred to those favors and blessings of Allah that had continuously accompanied the Children of Israel like a shadow since their exit from Egypt. He referred to those definite and final promises Allah made to establish the chain of prophethood and make the Children of Israel a great ruling nation. He referred to that inheritance in the form of a lush and fertile region that they were to receive, and which Allah had written for them. Along with all these promises and assurances, he invited them to invade the Holy Land, and simultaneously warned them of the evil consequence of cowardice and loss of heart—that if you turn back, you will end up completely unsuccessful. Behind was the slavery of Egypt, and if you do not show courage for what lies ahead, it is wandering in the desert in which you will die and perish.

قَالُوْا يٰمُوْسٰٓى اِنَّ فِيْهَا قَوْمًا جَبَّارِيْنَ ڰ وَاِنَّا لَنْ نَّدْخُلَهَا حَتّٰي يَخْرُجُوْا مِنْهَا ۚ فَاِنْ يَّخْرُجُوْا مِنْهَا فَاِنَّا دٰخِلُوْنَ 

**The Overwhelmed State of the Children of Israel:** 'a people of tyrannical strength' (*qawman jabbārīn*). *Jabbār* means of towering stature, strong, powerful, and robust. In Arabic, *jabbār* is also used for palm trees that are very tall. In the Torah, their portrait is drawn in these words: 'The land, through which we have gone to search it, is a land that eateth up the inhabitants thereof; and all the people that we saw in it are men of a great stature. And there we saw the giants, the sons of Anak, which come of the giants: and we were in our own sight as grasshoppers, and so we were in their sight.' (Numbers 13:32-33).

These are the words of the report given by the members of the investigative mission regarding the inhabitants of Palestine. The word 'giants' (translated as *jabbār* in Arabic versions) is used for the sons of Anak. It seems this word has been in use since that era, and the same was chose by the Quran. Arabic and Hebrew are closely related languages, which is why they share many roots and words. This is the reply of the Children of Israel to the speech of Moses mentioned above: since such giants and powerful people occupy this land, we are not ready to become food for their swords. However, if through some miracle of nature—like the miracles you have been showing us so far—they emerge from this region on their own, then we are certainly ready to occupy it.

قَالَ رَجُلٰنِ مِنَ الَّذِيْنَ يَخَافُوْنَ اَنْعَمَ اللّٰهُ عَلَيْهِمَا ادْخُلُوْا عَلَيْهِمُ الْبَابَ ۚ فَاِذَا دَخَلْتُمُوْهُ فَاِنَّكُمْ غٰلِبُوْنَ ۥ ۚ وَعَلَي اللّٰهِ فَتَوَكَّلُوْٓا اِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُّؤْمِنِيْنَ 

**The Exemplary Character of Joshua and Caleb:** 'Said two men of those who feared, upon whom Allah had bestowed favor.' By 'two men' (*rajulān*), what is meant is Joshua and Caleb, as mentioned above, who were members of the investigative mission sent to explore the conditions of Palestine. Regarding 'those who feared' (*alladhīna yakhāfūna*), commentators generally consider the object of *yakhāfūna* to be omitted—that is, *yakhāfūna Allāh* (those who feared Allah). Although my teacher also inclined toward this, my heart does not find peace with this interpretation for two reasons. First, this was an occasion to express the object rather than omit it, because ambiguity could arise here, and in situations of ambiguity, expression is preferred over omission. Second, in this case, it would have to be assumed that a group of those who feared God was present at that time, among whom were Joshua and Caleb. If that is so, why was the specific mention of Allah's favor made only for these two men? In that case, it should have been 'upon whom Allah had bestowed favor' in the plural (*alayhim*) instead of the dual (*alayhimā*). Furthermore, both the Torah and the Quran make it clear that at that time, the hearts of the entire nation had sunk out of fear of the sons of Anak; only these two servants of God emerged from the entire nation who were not struck with fear but remained firm on the covenant of God. Therefore, the interpretation nearer to accuracy in my view is that although Joshua and Caleb belonged to the very nation on which fear and cowardice had cast a shadow of death, it was Allah's grace and favor upon them that they did not agree to die in this general epidemic, but found the facilitation to remain firm on faith and resolve. There is no doubt that when an entire nation loses heart the way the Children of Israel did, the nerves of even the bravest of men can give way. Truly loyal and sincere is that man of truth who maintains his loyalty and sincerity in such a delicate situation. It is this aspect of the character of Joshua and Caleb because of which the Quran, in this Surah of the Covenant, mentioned them and made them immortal, so those who intend to walk on the path of God may learn from their exemplary character how those who stay awake do so when everyone else falls asleep, and how those who remain alive do so when everyone else dies. The Quran has highlighted the brave within the cowards and the living within the dead so that it may be known that although brave among the brave and living among the living are many, those life-giving souls are highly rare who give life to the dead, even if they have to lose their own lives in this path.

**The Historic Speech of Joshua and Caleb:** 'Enter upon them through the gate, for when you have entered it, you will be predominant.' This is the speech of these two men of truth delivered to restore the courage of their nation that had lost heart. They cried out: Charge upon them through the gate of the city. Once you take this step, you will be dominant. The law of Allah is that when servants put into the field whatever power and strength they possess to fulfill their duty, He blesses them with His support and help; His heavenly support does not descend upon those who remain sitting in their homes. In the Torah, this speech is in these words: 'And Joshua the son of Nun, and Caleb the son of Jephunneh, which were of them that searched the land, rent their clothes: And they spake unto all the company of the children of Israel, saying, The land, which we passed through to search it, is an exceeding good land. If the Lord delight in us, then he will bring us into this land, and give it us; a land which floweth with milk and honey. Only rebel not ye against the Lord, neither fear ye the people of the land; for they are bread for us: their defence is departed from them, and the Lord is with us: fear them not. But all the congregation bade stone them with stones.' (Numbers 14:6-10).

'And upon Allah rely, if you should be believers.' That is, if you have faith in God, then God has promised with an oath to give you this land as an inheritance; therefore, place your trust in God and resolve to obey His command. When you rise to fulfill your duty, He will surely fulfill His promise.

قَالُوْا يٰمُوْسٰٓى اِنَّا لَنْ نَّدْخُلَهَآ اَبَدًا مَّادَامُوْا فِيْهَا فَاذْهَبْ اَنْتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقَاتِلَآ اِنَّا هٰهُنَا قٰعِدُوْنَ 

This was the final reply from the Children of Israel. Although this reply is not present in these exact words in the Torah, the wailing and mourning of the Children of Israel is recorded:

**The Wailing of the Children of Israel:** 'And all the congregation lifted up their voice, and cried; and the people wept that night. And all the children of Israel murmured against Moses and against Aaron: and the whole congregation said unto them, Would God that we had died in the land of Egypt! or would God we had died in this wilderness! And wherefore hath the Lord brought us unto this land, to fall by the sword...' (Numbers 14:1-3). Obviously, for those whose cowardice and terror had reached such a stage, the assurance of Moses, Joshua, and Caleb that 'the Lord is with us, fear them not' was entirely useless. In response to this assurance, they must have said: if the Lord is with you, go you and your Lord and fight; we are sitting right here.

قَالَ رَبِّ اِنِّىْ لَآ اَمْلِكُ اِلَّا نَفْسِيْ وَاَخِيْ فَافْرُقْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ الْقَوْمِ الْفٰسِقِيْنَ 

**The Supplication of Moses in the Divine Court:** After this final reply of the Children of Israel, any hope of good from them ended. Therefore, Moses supplicated to his Lord with immense grief and sorrow: O my Lord! I have no power over anyone except my own soul and my brother; therefore, separate us from this disobedient people. The intent of 'separating us' was obviously to be relieved of the heavy burden of leading and reforming them. After such a long struggle, and after witnessing countless miracles and signs, when their lack of faith is such that not a single person is ready to listen, how can I make these stones feel? Now, pass Your judgment between me and them. Since Aaron was a minister appointed by God Himself and had proven his loyalty at every stage, trust in him was an obvious matter, but the rest of the nation, except for the two servants of God mentioned above, turned out to be entirely dead.

قَالَ فَاِنَّهَا مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ اَرْبَعِيْنَ سَـنَةً ۚ يَتِيْھُوْنَ فِي الْاَرْضِ ۭ فَلَا تَاْسَ عَلَي الْقَوْمِ الْفٰسِقِيْنَ 

**The Punishment of Wandering in the Desert for the Children of Israel:** Allah did not grant Moses' request for separation, because a prophet is like the soul for his nation. His separation from the nation, especially with a declaration of dissociation, would be a message of destruction for the entire nation. However, as a punishment for their ingratitude and lack of faith, He declared the Holy Land forbidden to them for forty years, and decreed that they would spend this period wandering in this very desert. In the Torah, this is recorded as follows: 'And the Lord spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying, How long shall I bear with this evil congregation, which murmur against me? I have heard the murmurings of the children of Israel... say unto them, As truly as I live, saith the Lord, as ye have spoken in mine ears, so will I do to you: Your carcases shall fall in this wilderness... Doubtless ye shall not come into the land, concerning which I sware to make you dwell therein, save Caleb the son of Jephunneh, and Joshua the son of Nun. But your little ones, which ye said should be a prey, them will I bring in, and they shall know the land which ye have despised. But as for you, your carcases, they shall fall in this wilderness. And your children shall wander in the wilderness forty years, and bear your whoredoms...' (Numbers 14:26-33). (It should be kept in mind here that the word 'whoredom/prostitution' is frequently used in the Torah for breach of covenant and unfaithfulness to God).

**An Important Lesson in Sociology:** During this period of wandering, the Children of Israel also lost the leadership of Moses and Aaron, and that entire generation which had been nurtured under the shadow of Egyptian slavery perished. Only the generation that was born and grew up in the free environment of the desert remained. It was this generation that later conquered the Promised Land under the leadership of Joshua. From this, some of our sociologists have deduced—and correctly so—that self-confidence and high resolve are essential for the responsibilities of freedom and governance. Egyptian slavery had eliminated this quality within the Children of Israel. Nature forged them anew in the furnace of the desert to produce this quality within them, only after which they became capable of conquering a land and ruling over it. This event from the history of the Children of Israel completely refuted their delusion, referenced above, that they were the beloved of Allah and therefore free from the responsibilities of action and obedience. The meaning is: if this delusion of yours carried any reality, you were even more beloved in the presence of Moses; why then did it not happen that when you refused to take a step, God carried you on His shoulders and made you the kings of Palestine? Why then are you caught in the delusion of attaining God's Paradise for free?

The upcoming subject (Verses 27 to 31): Next, the story of the two sons of Adam is narrated. This story is recorded in the Torah as well, but in accordance with the general style of the Torah, its nature is merely that of an early historical account of the descendants of Adam. It does not clarify the wisdom and lesson for which this story is told. The Quran has narrated it with its wisdom and lesson, and clarified those parts that the narrators of the Torah had lost, although they were highly necessary for learning lessons. The details of the realities this story is meant to clarify here will come within the commentary of the verses. However, we point out several fundamental points here to keep the sequence of discourse clear.

First, it clarifies that to remain firm on the covenant of God, the primary requirement is for a person to possess such fear of God as will keep his feet steadfast on the path of truth even in the most severe trials. Second, the cause of covenant-breaking is those corrupt emotions that arise from the incitement of Satan, which ultimately prepare a person to commit crimes entirely contrary to the divine covenant. Third, just as God's righteous servants, Joshua and Caleb, remained firm on God's covenant despite the general corruption of morals and character, and did not care for their lives, similarly, God's righteous servant, Abel, maintained his stance of truth and justice against the oppression and transgression of his brother Cain. Cain's enmity could not deviate him from truth and justice, to the extent that he sacrificed his life in protecting this truth and justice. This is proof that the history of the standard-bearers of 'let not the hatred of a people prevent you from being just...' and 'be persistently standing firm for Allah, witnesses in justice' is highly ancient. The very first martyr on this path is Adam's son Abel, who practically established an immortal example for subsequent generations that dying for the truth is thousands of times better than living on falsehood. Fourth, faith in God, worship of God, the condition of sincerity and piety for worship, the concept of justice, the crime of murder, and the belief in Paradise and Hell—all these have been taught to humans from the very beginning of creation. Their covenant was taken from Adam and his descendants just as it was taken from every prophet and his nation. This completely refutes the view of those who think that early humans were entirely devoid of these concepts of truth and justice, and arrived at them only after a long evolutionary journey of thought and morals. We have discussed the falsity of this view in detail elsewhere. In this light, recite the upcoming verses:

وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَاَ ابْنَيْ اٰدَمَ بِالْحَقِّ ۘاِذْ قَرَّبَا قُرْبَانًا فَتُقُبِّلَ مِنْ اَحَدِهِمَا وَلَمْ يُتَقَبَّلْ مِنَ الْاٰخِرِ ۭ قَالَ لَاَقْتُلَنَّكَ ۭ قَالَ اِنَّمَا يَتَقَبَّلُ اللّٰهُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِيْنَ 

'And recite to them the story of the two sons of Adam, in truth...' The pronoun in 'to them' (*'alayhim*) refers to the People of the Book, specifically the Jews, whose mention has been running from above. Although the lesson and advice in the event are general, being as instructive for this Ummah as they are for the Jews—indeed, a Hadith records that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) said: the event of the two sons of Adam was narrated as an example for this Ummah, so follow the example of the better of the two—nonetheless, the direct address is to the Jews, because they adopted the exact same path toward this Ummah that Cain adopted toward Abel. Just as Cain was seized with the fever of jealousy upon Abel's acceptance with God, descending to spill innocent blood in violation of truth and justice, similarly, the Jews, when they saw God's favor upon this Ummah, were so bewildered by the madness of jealousy that they reached the lowest depths of wretchedness and misfortune.

*Naba'* refers to the news of an important event or occurrence. Since this event is the very first occurrence under this heaven of the conflict between justice and oppression, loyalty and covenant-breaking, fear of God and transgression, and since it was the first time that innocent blood was spilled on this earth of God in the path of truth, the Quran termed it *naba'* to highlight its importance. 'In truth' (*bil-haqq*) means, as we clarified elsewhere, to narrate it accurately and while keeping the aspect of wisdom and lesson in view. If events are narrated merely for storytelling, it is a futile exercise; the benefit of narrating them is achieved only when they are narrated with the wisdom and lesson hidden within them, and narrated accurately. In the Torah, the misfortune is that events are neither narrated accurately nor is the wisdom and lesson clarified from them. The same is the case with most of our history books, which has reduced the field of history to a completely useless discipline. We quote the mentioned event here from the Torah. Read it and compare it with the Quran's account to estimate what narrating 'in truth' means. In the Torah, the event is narrated as follows:

**The Story of Abel and Cain in the Torah:** 'And Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain, and said, I have gotten a man from the Lord. And she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground. And in process of time it came to pass, that Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto the Lord. And Abel, he also brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof. And the Lord had respect unto Abel and to his offering: But unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect. And Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell. And the Lord said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen? If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted? and if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him. And Cain talked with Abel his brother: and it came to pass, when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him. And the Lord said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother? And he said, I know not: Am I my brother's keeper? And he said, What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground. And now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand; When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength; a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth.' (Genesis 4:1-12).

**Clear Difference Between the Quranic and Torah Accounts:** This account differs from the Quranic account in several aspects. Had we not feared straying from our scope of discussion, we would have pointed them out. However, one thing is so clear that it strikes the eye at first glance: in the Torah, all those aspects of Abel's character are completely missing which are the very soul of this narrative and serve as a model and example for all of humanity. Since the Quran presented the narrative 'in truth,' it highlighted these aspects, and every fair-minded person will acknowledge that by highlighting them, Abel's narrative attained the status of the very first link in the golden chain of those who stand firm for justice. Cain's character is also presented in a highly incomplete form in the Torah. Ahead, you will see that the Quran has exposed some of those corners of his character that are necessary for understanding the wisdom and interest behind certain rulings of the Shariah. From this perspective, you will find that the very details that lie like pebbles in the repository of the Torah shine like jewels in the Quran.

**The Non-Acceptance of Cain's Offering:** 'when they both offered an offering, and it was accepted from one of them but was not accepted from the other.' *Qurbān* (offering) is used for both charity (*sadaqah*) and sacrifice. Whatever is presented in the presence of Allah with the intention of seeking nearness to Him is *qurbān*. Here, the Quran has not clarified how Abel and Cain came to know of the acceptance and non-acceptance, because this clarification was unnecessary for the purpose the Quran had in view. However, from the aforementioned account of the Torah, it is known that the Lord informed them. One of the means of hearing and knowing the Lord's word is a voice from the unseen (*Hatif Ghayb*), which is mentioned in many places in the Torah.

**Cain's Fit of Jealousy:** 'He said, "I will surely kill you." He said, "Indeed, Allah only accepts from the righteous."' Upon knowing that his offering was not accepted, Cain, instead of reflecting on the defect of his own intention, directed his anger toward Abel: why was Abel's offering accepted? Abel had no role in Cain's offering being rejected; the fault lay entirely within Cain and his offering. But when a person is seized with a fit of jealousy, he does not see his own unworthiness; instead, he looks for the causes of his failures within others, and in this anger, seeks to avenge and harm them. Cain was the first to present an example of this wretched character among individuals, and the Jews were the first to present it among nations. For this reason, as we indicated above, this narrative was told to the Jews so they could see their own faces in this mirror—that today, the same story of Abel and Cain is being repeated, the account of which reached the world through the Jews themselves.

**The Statement of Quranic Philosophy through Abel:** 'Indeed, Allah only accepts from the righteous' (*Innamā yaqabbalu Allāhu mina al-muttaqīn*). This states through Abel's tongue the same wisdom expressed in the Quran in the words: 'Their meat will not reach Allah, nor will their blood, but what reaches Him is piety from you.' Abel said this to draw Cain's attention to the actual reality: you are seeking to kill me out of anger because your offering was not accepted, whereas the fault lies neither with me nor with the Lord, but entirely with you and your offering. With the Lord, only that offering is accepted which is presented by God-fearing servants while observing its rules and conditions. Since this rule applies to me just as it applies to you, if you are grieved by the rejection of your offering, seek to cultivate piety, not to kill me; how will killing me open the way for your offering to be accepted?

لَىِٕنْۢ بَسَطْتَّ اِلَيَّ يَدَكَ لِتَقْتُلَنِيْ مَآ اَنَا بِبَاسِطٍ يَّدِيَ اِلَيْكَ لِاَقْتُلَكَ ۚ اِنِّىْٓ اَخَافُ اللّٰهَ رَبَّ الْعٰلَمِيْنَ 

**Abel's Faithful Stance Against Cain's Intent of Murder:** *Bast yad* means stretching out the hand and committing transgression. Here, it refers to stretching out the hand with the intent to kill, which is why it means initiating murder. The meaning is: if you, as you are threatening, wish to initiate murder against me, I will not, assuming that you are seeking to kill me, be the first to stretch out my hand to kill you. I fear Allah, the Lord of the worlds, who created me and you, and who commanded the respect of each other's life and property. It should be kept in mind that this is not a situation of facing an open religious enemy on the field of battle, but a matter between brother and brother. One brother is threatening another with murder. In such a situation, the correct faithful stance is that a person, even knowing that his brother is seeking to kill him, should not be the first to initiate murder. But while he should not initiate, it does not mean he should not defend himself. Abel negated initiating, not self-defense. Defending one's life or property is not contrary to the fear of God. A Hadith records that a person asked the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), 'What should I do if a person wants to seize my property?' He replied, 'Remind him of the fear of God.' The questioner asked, 'What if he does not fear God?' He replied, 'Seek help against him from the Muslims around you.' The questioner asked, 'What if there is no authority nearby?' He replied, 'Fight to protect your property until you save it or die as a martyr.'

اِنِّىْٓ اُرِيْدُ اَنْ تَبُوْۗاَ بِاِثْمِيْ وَاِثْمِكَ فَتَكُوْنَ مِنْ اَصْحٰبِ النَّارِ ۚ وَذٰلِكَ جَزٰۗؤُا الظّٰلِمِيْنَ 

**The Meaning of 'My Sin' (Ithmi) and 'Your Sin' (Ithmika):** In both 'my sin' and 'your sin,' a noun is omitted (*mudāf mahdūf*). That is: I do not wish to initiate your murder because I do not want to return to my Lord carrying any burden of sin. If you wish to initiate this crime, go ahead. If you kill me, the burden of my murder will be on your head, and if you are harmed or killed as a result of my self-defense, the burden of that sin will also be on your head, because the cause of it was not me but you. This points to the principle of justice expressed in a Hadith: 'The burden of sin is upon the initiator as long as the oppressed does not transgress.' That is, if the oppressed committed no excess, whatever he had to do to protect himself, its burden of sin lies on the initiator. 'With my sin' alongside 'with your sin' is expressed on the principle of correspondence, which is well known in the Arabic language. For example: *dānahum kamā dānū* (He repaid them as they dealt), or 'the recompense of an evil is an evil like it.'

**The Punishment for Murdering a Believer is Hell:** 'and you will be among the companions of the Fire. And that is the recompense of the wrongdoers.' The punishment for murdering a believer, when committed intentionally, is Hell. We wrote in detail on this under Surah An-Nisa, verse 82. This verse shows that the punishment for this crime has been recognized as such in the Shariah of God from the very beginning.

30-31: فَطَوَّعَتْ لَهٗ نَفْسُهٗ قَتْلَ اَخِيْهِ فَقَتَلَهٗ فَاَصْبَحَ مِنَ الْخٰسِرِيْنَ ۔ فَبَعَثَ اللّٰهُ غُرَابًا يَّبْحَثُ فِي الْاَرْضِ لِيُرِيَهٗ كَيْفَ يُوَارِيْ سَوْءَةَ اَخِيْهِ ۭ قَالَ يٰوَيْلَتٰٓى اَعَجَزْتُ اَنْ اَكُوْنَ مِثْلَ هٰذَا الْغُرَابِ فَاُوَارِيَ سَوْءَةَ اَخِيْ ۚ فَاَصْبَحَ مِنَ النّٰدِمِيْنَ 

**The Failure of Cain:** 'And his soul permitted to him the murder of his brother...'—this means that his soul ultimately prepared him to murder his brother. This style of expression reflects the inner conflict that initially arose within him regarding this intent of murder. Within humans, nature has deposited a self-reproaching soul (*Nafs Lawwāmah*) which protests against any intent of crime until a person silences its voice through various excuses and justifications. Cain also had to pass through this stage, but ultimately, his jealousy prepared him to commit this horrific crime. Initially, every criminal faces this hesitation, but as he commits crime after crime, his self-reproaching soul—or in other words, his conscience—becomes entirely dead, and he becomes completely audacious in committing crimes. 'And he became among the losers' points to the reality that every such conflict opens a field of trial for humans, in which both failure and success are possible. If a person subdues his soul in this conflict, he remains successful and his soul is defeated; if his soul subdues him, his soul is successful and he himself fails. Cain was subdued by his soul, which is why he failed.

**Satan in the Guise of a Crow:** 'And Allah sent a crow...' It seems that after the murder, Cain left his brother's body lying there and did not attempt to hide it. At that moment, a crow appeared. The crow is highly intelligent. It is its habit that when it snatches some food and does not wish to eat it immediately, it scratches the earth or hides it under something for a time of need. This crow performed the same action in front of Cain, thereby guiding him to also hide his brother's body so that it would not be exposed to the sight of others and his crime would not be revealed. Seeing this intelligence of the crow, Cain beat his head: Woe to my wretchedness that I turned out to be even less capable than this crow, as this plan did not occur to me so I could also cover my brother's body! Consequently, he felt immense regret for this foolishness of his.

Through this segment, the Quran has highlighted that those who do not fear God fear creation, and those who do not listen to the command of God and the voice of conscience receive inspiration from a crow, and after committing a crime, they look for plans to hide it instead of confessing and repenting. In our view, this crow is an example of Satan. Satan first whispered within Cain's soul to prepare him to murder his brother, and once he committed this crime, he suggested the plan to hide the crime through the crow, thereby taking a highly successful step toward fulfilling the oath he took to mislead the descendants of Adam.

**An Established Law of Allah:** The attribution of sending the crow to Allah is actually the attribution of the law of Allah under which this action occurred. Multiple examples of this have been discussed in previous pages. This law of Allah is that when a person closes his ears and eyes to the verses of Allah, His commands, and His warnings, Allah appoints over him a devil who becomes his close companion to make him wander in the valleys of his desires. In Surah Az-Zukhruf, this law of Allah is referred to in these words: 'And whoever turns away from the remembrance of the Most Merciful—We appoint for him a devil, and he is to him a companion.' In Surah Fussilat, it says: 'And We assigned to them companions who made attractive to them what was before them and what was behind them.' It passed above that Abel gave highly effective and beautiful advice to Cain and proved the truth of his words as clear as day, but Cain's heart did not soften in the least. For such a hard-hearted person, falling under the grip of the aforementioned law of Allah is an obvious matter.

Generally, commentators have understood that the crow came to teach Cain how to bury his brother's body—that he carried his brother's body on his back for a long time, but did not understand what to do with it. Ultimately, when the body began to decay, God sent a crow which killed another crow and buried it in the earth, only after which Cain learned the method of disposing of his brother's body. We believe that after what we have submitted above, there remains no need to refute this strange assertion.

The upcoming subject (Verses 32 to 34):

**The Foundation of the Law of Retribution:** Next, He stated that since human beings, by nature, are creatures among whom there are both God-fearing ones who stand firm on truth and justice like Abel, and hard-hearted, murderous ones like Cain, Allah made retribution (*qisās*) a collective obligation in the Shariah of the Children of Israel. Thus, the killer of one is deemed the killer of all, and the savior of one is deemed the savior of all. Then, to renew and remind them of this law, Allah sent His prophets and messengers among them, but the Children of Israel paid no heed to all this preparation. Instead, they continually cause corruption and bloodshed on God's earth, following the evil path of Cain.

Afterward, He described the punishment for those who, while living under a just Islamic government, attempt to disrupt its law of justice and equity and cause corruption in the land—regardless of whether this attempt is made by nominal Muslims or non-Muslims, openly or in the form of hidden conspiracies, through organized gangs or hooliganism. Whichever corrupt activity poses a threat to the political, social, and societal system of the country and creates a law and order problem, the authorities of the government are granted broad powers to eradicate it and punish its perpetrators, so they may fulfill the duty of establishing justice laid upon them. In this light, recite the upcoming verses:

مِنْ اَجْلِ ذٰلِكَ ۃ كَتَبْنَا عَلٰي بَنِيْٓ اِسْرَاۗءِيْلَ اَنَّهٗ مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْسًۢابِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ اَوْ فَسَادٍ فِي الْاَرْضِ فَكَاَنَّمَا قَتَلَ النَّاسَ جَمِيْعًا ۭوَمَنْ اَحْيَاهَا فَكَاَنَّمَآ اَحْيَا النَّاسَ جَمِيْعًا ۭوَلَقَد *جَاۗءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا بِالْبَيِّنٰتِ ۡ ثُمَّ اِنَّ كَثِيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ بَعْدَ ذٰلِكَ فِي الْاَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُوْنَ

**The Meaning of 'Min Ajli Dhalik' (Because of That):** 'Because of that' does not mean that this specific event was the direct cause for the obligation of the law of retribution. This event, as is clear, occurred long before the history of the Children of Israel. Furthermore, the law of life for life is not unique to the Children of Israel; it has existed in every nation from the beginning. It was present in the nation of Noah and Abraham. The guidance given to Noah and his descendants in this regard is mentioned in the Torah as follows: 'And surely your blood of your lives will I require; at the hand of every beast will I require it, and at the hand of man; at the hand of every man's brother will I require the life of man. Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed: for in the image of God made he man.' (Genesis 9:5-6). Therefore, it is incorrect to assume that this specific event was the direct cause for making the law of retribution obligatory upon the Children of Israel. It should be kept in mind that the purpose here is not to narrate the history of the law of retribution, but to clarify that the Children of Israel are so audacious and reckless regarding Allah's covenant that despite knowing that the killer of one is the killer of all, and the protection of one is the protection of all, they continuously cause corruption on God's earth. This was their way in the past, and it remains their way today.

In this light, the reference of 'because of that' (*min ajli dhālik*) is not to the event itself, but to that mentality of mischief and evil which Cain displayed, and which continues to be displayed by those who follow his evil path. That is, those who spill the blood of God's servants under the influence of base desires and satanic motives, and then, instead of confession, turning to God, and showing repentance, spend all their intelligence in hiding the crime. Even if they feel regret for their crime, it is not because they violated the greatest right of God's servants, but because they fell short in their schemes to conceal the crime.

**The Wisdom and Greatness of the Law of Retribution:** 'that whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land—it is as if he had slain mankind entirely. And whoever saves one—it is as if he had saved mankind entirely.' This is not the statement of the actual ruling of retribution given to the Jews, but rather its underlying argument, wisdom, and greatness. The law of 'life for life' is present in the Torah, and reference to it is coming ahead in this Surah. Since the purpose here is to highlight the mischief and wretchedness of the Jews, the core philosophy of the law of retribution was stated: to make the gravity of murder clear to the Jews, this command was given to them with the clarification that the killer of one is the killer of all, and the savior of one is the savior of all, yet they remained entirely reckless regarding murder and causing corruption in the land.

**The Responsibilities of the Law of Retribution Upon Every Individual of the Nation:** A nation entrusted with the law of retribution based on the philosophy mentioned above necessarily bears several responsibilities, which we will point out here:

First, every occurrence of murder must create a stir throughout the entire nation. Until retribution is taken, every individual should feel that they have been deprived of the security they previously enjoyed. The law is the protector of all; if the law is demolished, it is not just the victim who has been killed, but everyone is now targeted for murder. Second, tracking down the killer is not the responsibility of the victim's heirs alone, but of the entire community, because the killer has not just killed the victim but has killed everyone. Third, if a person sees someone in danger, it is not permissible for him to ignore it as someone else's fight; rather, protecting and supporting the victim to the best of his ability is obligatory upon him, even if he has to bear some risk himself. This is because the one who stands up in support and defense of an oppressed person does not stand up in support of that oppressed person alone, but in support of all creation, including himself. Fourth, if a person hides a murder, or gives false testimony in favor of the killer, or acts as a guarantor for the killer, or shelters him, or knowingly acts as his legal advocate, or knowingly acquits him of the crime, he is doing all this for the killer of himself, his father, his brother, and his son, because the killer of one is the killer of all. Fifth, helping the heirs of the victim or the authorities in taking retribution actually gives life to the victim, because the Quran has declared: 'And there is for you in legal retribution [saving of] life.'

We have pointed out several major aspects derived from this principle. If you reflect further, more of its wisdoms will become clear. How lamentable then is the state of the nation that, despite being aware of this principle, became entirely reckless in committing murder and causing corruption in the land.

**The Arrangement of Reminding of the Law Along with the Law Itself:** 'And our messengers had certainly come to them with clear proofs. Then indeed many of them, after that, throughout the land, were transgressors.' This means that Allah did not suffice with merely telling them this duty; rather, to complete the proof, messengers of God continuously came among them, waking and shaking them through highly clear commands, guidance, and powerful warnings so they would not remain negligent of the responsibilities of Allah's covenant. Yet, despite all this preparation, they continuously committed various transgressions on God's earth.

**The Meaning of Corruption (Fasad) and Transgression (Israf):** There is no significant difference in meaning between 'causing corruption in the land' (*ifsād fī al-ard*) and 'transgressing in the land' (*isrāf fī al-ard*). The peace and order of the earth depend on that law of truth, justice, and equity which God revealed for it. Just as any disruption in the physical laws of the universe would throw the entire cosmic system into chaos, similarly, if any disruption is caused in the legislative system that its Creator chose for it, the social, societal, and political system is thrown into chaos. Consequently, neither the harmony of the political system with the cosmic system remains, nor does any connection remain within the social and political system itself. It is this state of affairs that is expressed here as corruption and transgression.

Alongside this fundamental reality, it is also necessary to keep in mind the historical reality that Muslims were continuously experiencing from the Jews at the time of the revelation of these verses. Multiple Jewish tribes, such as the Banu Nadir, Banu Qurayzah, and Banu Qaynuqa, lived in the vicinity of Medina. Although they had entered into treaties of peace, security, and mutual defense with the Muslims, they did not respect these treaties for even a single day. Instead, they continually conspired to harm the Muslims and uproot them from Medina. In every attack the Quraysh launched against the Muslims, the Jews were secretly involved. They also repeatedly tried to sow discord between the Ansar and the Muhajirun. They even plotted multiple times to assassinate the Companions, and indeed the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself. Although most of their plots failed, several highly tragic incidents did occur. They were extremely cunning and cold-blooded in kidnapping and murdering women and children. Muslims were constantly in fear for their lives and honor due to the Jews. To such an extent that even when they invited some Muslims to settle a dispute or discuss a matter, they would have a plot ready beforehand to destroy them. The phrase 'Then indeed many of them, after that, throughout the land, were transgressors' points to this entire state of affairs.

33-34: اِنَّمَا جَزٰۗؤُا الَّذِيْنَ يُحَارِبُوْنَ اللّٰهَ وَرَسُوْلَهٗ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِي الْاَرْضِ فَسَادًا اَنْ يُّقَتَّلُوْٓا اَوْ يُصَلَّبُوْٓا اَوْ تُـقَطَّعَ اَيْدِيْهِمْ وَاَرْجُلُهُمْ مِّنْ خِلَافٍ اَوْ يُنْفَوْا مِنَ الْاَرْضِ ۭ ذٰلِكَ لَهُمْ خِزْيٌ فِي الدُّنْيَاوَلَهُمْ فِي الْاٰخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيْمٌ

**The Meaning of 'Muharabah' (Waging War):** 'who wage war against Allah and His Messenger and strive upon earth [to cause] corruption.' Waging war against Allah and His Messenger means that a person, group, or gang attempts with audacity, insolence, and recklessness to disrupt the system of truth and justice established by Allah and His Messenger. If such an attempt is made by external enemies, the rulings of war and Jihad to counter them are detailed separately. Here, instead of external enemies, the penal code is stated to suppress those internal enemies of the Islamic government who, while being subjects of the Islamic state—regardless of whether they are Muslims or non-Muslims—challenge its law and order. One form of violating the law is when an individual commits a crime, in which case action will be taken against him under the general penal code of the Shariah. The second form is when a person or group attempts to take the law into their own hands, disrupting the peace and order of the area through their evil and mischief, leaving people constantly in danger regarding their lives, property, honor, and chastity. When murder, robbery, highway dacoity, arson, kidnapping, rape, sabotage, smuggling, and other such severe crimes create a law and order problem for the government, the Islamic government is authorized to take the following actions instead of relying on the general penal code:

'that they be killed' (*an yuqattalū*): that these perpetrators of corruption in the land be executed. Here, instead of the simple verb *qubila*, the intensive form *tuqtīl* (from the grammatical paradigm *taf'īl*) is used. This paradigm indicates intensity and frequency of action, which implies that they should be executed in an exemplary and instructive manner to serve as a lesson to others. Only those methods of execution that are forbidden in the Shariah—such as burning in fire—are excluded. Aside from that, the government may adopt other methods deemed necessary to deter criminals, terrorize them, and instill respect for law and order in the hearts of the people. In our view, stoning (*rajm*) is also included under *tuqtīl*. Therefore, for those criminals and hooligans who pose a threat to the honor and chastity of noble citizens, who make kidnapping and rape their profession, who loot people's honor in broad daylight, and openly commit rape, the punishment of stoning is included in the meaning of this word. Regarding the distinction made in our jurisprudence between the married and unmarried for stoning, we will discuss it in detail in the commentary of Surah An-Nur.

'or crucified' (*aw yusallabū*): that such people be crucified. Here, instead of the simple verb *salb*, the intensive form *taslīb* is used, which indicates that methods of crucifixion and hanging that are more painful and exemplary may also be adopted. In our view, some modern methods that have been developed also fall under the meaning of this word.

'or that their hands and feet be cut off from opposite sides' (*aw tuqatta'a aydīhim wa-arjuluhum min khilāfin*): that their hands and feet be cut off in an alternating pattern. This instruction of cutting from opposite sides is also from the perspective of making it exemplary and painful. The purpose is that if any such malicious person is spared his life, he is spared in such a state that all his instruments of mischief and corruption are rendered useless.

'or that they be exiled from the land' (*aw yunfaw mina al-ard*): that they be banished from the country. The literal meaning of *nafy* is exile. While imprisonment and confinement are not its literal meanings, they are certainly included in its scope. If exiling such criminals is difficult or contrary to religious and political interests, they may be imprisoned, restricted, or house-arrested in a specific area. This will not be contrary to the meaning of this word.

**The Government's Authority to Take Appropriate Action Based on the Nature of the Situation:** The words of the Quran clearly prove that the government may take whichever of these actions it deems appropriate, based on the nature of the situation and the current and expected effects of disorder and lawlessness. The use of the particle *aw* (or) in the Arabic language expresses this option. For this reason, I find sound the opinion of those who grant the government the authority to choose whichever form it finds useful, effective, and in line with the public interest for establishing peace and law and eradicating trials. Under such circumstances, one does not merely look at whether the criminal gang only damaged property, but more importantly, one must keep an eye on the time, place, and the designs and impacts of the organized criminals. For instance, if it is a time of war or civil unrest, severe action will necessarily be required; similarly, if the place is a border area or the target of enemy conspiracies, effective action will be essential. If the ringleader of the mischief is a highly dangerous man and there is a fear that if he is given slack, the lives, property, and honor of many will be put at risk, then an effective step must be taken based on the circumstances. In short, the primary importance here does not lie in individual minor incidents, but in the collective impact of rebellion and the interests of the country and nation.

**Punishment Will Be Inflicted Collectively:** Under such circumstances, punishment is also inflicted not individually but collectively. If incidents of murder, kidnapping, rape, arson, and sabotage have occurred, there will be no investigation into exactly which hands committed these crimes; rather, every member of the rebel group will be considered a partner in the responsibility, and they will be dealt with as such, because the collective impact of all of them operated in the commission of every crime.

**Some Examples of the Application of This Law:** The exemplary punishment the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) inflicted upon the people of Ukl and Uraynah for stealing state camels and murdering their shepherds was placed by Imam Al-Bukhari under this verse. The treatment meted out by the Prophet to the Banu Nadir, Banu Qurayzah, and Banu Qaynuqa was also, in our view, under this divine command. The suppression of those who refused to pay zakat by Sayyiduna Abu Bakr also fell under this wisdom. The trial of Musaylimah the Liar also falls under this 'waging war against Allah and His Messenger,' and his suppression occurred under this divine law. The final expulsion of the Jews from Arabia by Hazrat Umar during his caliphate was also in compliance with this divine command.

**Discussion on the Modern Philosophy of Crime and Criminals:** 'That is for them a disgrace in this world...' This eliminates the doubt regarding the aforementioned punishments that arises or can arise in the minds of those who fail to correctly estimate the gravity of the crime of challenging Allah and His Messenger. True honor in this universe belongs to Allah and His Messenger: 'And to Allah belongs honor, and to His Messenger.' Therefore, those who display audacity against God and His Messenger and declare rebellion deserve to be disgraced in this world and face a painful punishment in the Hereafter. Their disgrace in this world will serve as a source of lesson and insight for others, instilling a fear and respect for the law in those who lack the capacity to respect the law merely for its utility and greatness. The sympathetic and compassionate theories regarding crime and criminals that have emerged under the name of philosophy in modern times are the very reason why the more humans outwardly progress, the more the world becomes like Hell. Islam does not encourage such absurd theories. Its law is based on human nature, not on airy theories. 'and for them in the Hereafter is a great punishment.'

**The Ruling on Those Who Reform Before Being Subdued:** 'Except for those who return [with repentance] before you apprehend them.' That is, these special powers shall be used only against those rebels who persisted in their rebellion until the government brought the situation under control, subduing and conquering them through its power. Those who repented and reformed their behavior before the government's action shall not be subjected to any such step based on their past conduct; rather, they will now be dealt with under the general law. If the rights of common citizens were violated by their hands, they will be compensated as far as possible.

If you keep in mind the force of the word *fa'lamū* (then know) in the verse, it clearly emerges that the government is not permitted to take any retaliatory action against those who repented and reformed before falling under apprehension. God is Forgiving and Merciful. When He forgives those who repent and reform before being apprehended, why should the behavior of His servants be different?
O you who have believed, fear Allah and seek the means [of nearness] to Him and strive in His cause that you may succeed.

36-37: اِنَّ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا لَوْ اَنَّ لَهُمْ مَّا فِي الْاَرْضِ جَمِيْعًا وَّمِثْلَهٗ مَعَهٗ لِيَفْتَدُوْا بِهٖ مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِ الْقِيٰمَةِ مَا تُقُبِّلَ مِنْهُمْ ۚ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ اَلِيْمٌ۔ يُرِيْدُوْنَ اَنْ يَّخْرُجُوْا مِنَ النَّارِ وَمَا هُمْ بِخٰرِجِيْنَ مِنْهَا ۡ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيْمٌ

**The Fate of Those Who Rely on Means Other Than God:** 'Indeed, those who disbelieve...' The context proves that 'those who disbelieve' here refers to those who chose a path other than the path of success mentioned in the preceding verse. That is, instead of fearing God, seeking His nearness, and striving in His cause, they spent their lives relying on baseless supports and imaginary intercessions, expecting that all the successes of the Hereafter would be theirs. He declared: Even if these people possessed all the treasures of the earth, and along with them another equal amount, and offered it all as a ransom to escape the punishment of the Hereafter, their ransom would not be accepted. No matter how much they struggle to emerge from the Fire, they will never emerge from it; they will face an eternal and everlasting punishment.

38-39: وَالسَّارِقُ وَالسَّارِقَةُ فَاقْطَعُوْٓا اَيْدِيَهُمَا جَزَاۗءًۢ بِمَا كَسَـبَا نَكَالًا۔ فَمَنْ تَابَ مِنْۢ بَعْدِ ظُلْمِهٖ وَاَصْلَحَ فَاِنَّ اللّٰهَ يَتُوْبُ عَلَيْهِ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ غَفُوْرٌ رَّحِيْمٌ 

**The Punishment for Theft:** 'And the male thief and the female thief...' is coordinate (*'atf*) with the perpetrators of waging war (*muhāribīn*) mentioned above; the two intervening verses are, as we indicated, by way of warning and reminder within the discussion of penal laws and limits. Since 'thief' (*sāriq* and *sāriqah*) are active participles denoting attributes, they indicate that the nature of the act must be such that theft can be applied to the act, and thief can be applied to the perpetrator. If an uncultivated person plucks a few fruits from someone's tree along the way, or takes some vegetables from someone's field, or picks up a few pieces of wood from someone's yard, or takes some food item from someone's kitchen, then although these are unseemly acts for which he deserves warning and discipline, this is not the theft for which the punishment of cutting off the hand is given. For this reason, our jurists have placed several conditions on defining this crime and executing its punishment, details of which are present in the books of jurisprudence. While we cannot enter into all those details here, we will point out a few aspects to give an idea of the nature of the issue.

**Conditions for the Punishment of Cutting Off the Hand:** Jurists have laid down the following restrictions to execute the punishment of cutting off the hand for theft:

1. The theft must be of something possessing value; the hand will not be cut off for stealing valueless or minor items. During the era of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), this punishment was not inflicted for the theft of trivial things. Jurists differ on the exact estimation of the value, and this difference is proof that there is room for variation. According to the Hanafis, the punishment of cutting off the hand will not be executed for anything valued at less than one dinar.
2. The theft must be from a secure place (*hirz*). If someone leaves their property lying around anywhere, or leaves their livestock wandering unattended in the wilderness, their theft will not fall under this law.
3. Property in which the thief has a joint share, or which is under his guardianship or trust, is excluded from the scope of this law.
4. The law does not apply to theft committed by an insane person or a minor.
5. If someone's wife, children, or domestic servants steal some of his property, this also falls outside the scope of this law.
6. Where there is any suspicion of necessity (*shubhat al-idtirār*), the punishment will not be executed. It is well known that Hazrat Umar suspended the punishment of cutting off the hand during the year of the famine (*'Am al-Ramadah*).
7. Being in the land of Islam (*Dar al-Islam*) is also a condition for the execution of this punishment. Limits and penal laws require, first of all, an empowered government, and secondly, they belong not to the land of disbelief or war, but to the land of Islam, because these rulings and limits are part of a collective system; executing them apart from this system is like fitting a square peg in a round hole. The era of the revelation of these rulings is itself proof that *Dar al-Islam* is a condition for their execution, as Allah revealed these rulings only when the land of Islam had practically been established.

**The Wisdom Behind Cutting Off the Hand:** 'cut off their hands in recompense for what they committed as an exemplary punishment from Allah.' Two reasons for cutting off the hand are mentioned here: first, it is the punishment for the criminal's crime; second, it is an exemplary punishment (*nakāl*). *Nakāl* means inflicting a punishment on someone that serves as a deterrent to others. The absence of a coordinating conjunction between these two reasons proves that both are desired simultaneously in this punishment: it is both a recompense for the deed and a means of lesson for others. Those who do not look at both of these aspects simultaneously sometimes fall into the confusion that the punishment is too severe for the crime, whereas this punishment does not merely punish the specific crime committed by the criminal, but also deters those many crimes he could have incited by his action had he not been given a punishment that crushes the audacity of others. Like the physical appetite, the hunger for wealth is highly intense within humans. If this greed is given even a little slack, what disastrous outcomes it can produce is easily observable in the conditions of the modern era, provided one has eyes to see. If one compiles the horrific crimes that occur in just one year in any highly civilized country of this era solely due to theft, it would be enough to open one's eyes. Yet, the human overawed by modern civilization feels his brow break into sweat upon hearing of a hand being cut off for theft, but his heart does not soften at the thousands of heart-wrenching events that occur directly or indirectly due to theft. Theft is not an isolated crime; it is a cluster of crimes from which various horrific crimes arise. If the path of theft is blocked, these will either disappear entirely or at least be reduced to the absolute minimum, alongside an extreme reduction in other crimes. If cutting off a few hands secures thousands of lives, thousands of homes, and thousands of honors, bringing an end to many chapters of oppression, cruelty, and destruction of crops and livestock, sound reason declares that this is not a costly deal but a highly blessed one. However, the dealers of intellect in this era do not understand this. 'And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise'—this points to both the power and wisdom of this divine law. All laws and commands of Allah are reflections of His attributes. He is Exalted in Might and Dominant, which is why He has the right to command whatever He wills, and He is Wise, which is why His every command is based on wisdom and interest; it is neither permissible for His servants to turn away from His command nor is it fitting for them to declare any of His commands contrary to wisdom and interest.

'But whoever repents after his wrongdoing...'—the word 'wrongdoing' (*thulm*) in 'after his wrongdoing' (*min ba'di thulmihi*) can be grammatically construct with either its subject or its object. If constructed with the object, it means: after his wrongdoing which he committed. Although parallels for the first construction also exist in the Quran, and it is also a reality that for a human, especially a Muslim, to commit a base crime like theft is a great wrongdoing against his own soul—as he harms his own soul far more than he violates the rights of others—nonetheless, I prefer the second meaning, because it includes both the wrongdoing the thief commits against his own soul and the wrongdoing he commits against the one whose property he steals.

**The Condition of Reform Along with Repentance:** The mention of reform (*islāh*) along with repentance (*tawbah*) is actually an indispensable condition of repentance. When a servant commits such a crime, he disobeys God on one hand, and violates his own rights or the rights of others on the other. To set matters right with God, reforming one's behavior and compensating for one's wrongdoing to the extent of one's capability is indispensable. Without this dual action, repentance remains entirely meaningless. It should also be remembered that repentance and reform clear the servant's matter in the Hereafter, but once the matter falls under the grip of the law, no limit (*hadd*) of the Shariah can be dropped because of repentance; it must be executed under all circumstances.

اَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ اَنَّ اللّٰهَ لَهٗ مُلْكُ السَّمٰوٰتِ وَالْاَرْضِ ۭ يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَّشَاۗءُ وَيَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَّشَاۗءُ ۭوَاللّٰهُ عَلٰي كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيْرٌ

**A Warning:** 'Do you not know...'—the address is general. This is a warning to all: the entire authority in the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah alone; He will punish whom He wills and forgive whom He wills, and there is no room for anyone else to question or interfere. Therefore, it is the duty of everyone to submit themselves to the law of Allah; no one should attempt to flee from it, nor should anyone scheme to save others from it, nor should anyone become reckless regarding God and His Shariah by relying on someone's power, influence, or intercession. This warning was necessary because, in reality, all these rulings regarding murder, retribution, robbery, and theft mentioned in this Surah proved to be a stumbling block for other nations. To escape them, they devised many backdoors, until all these laws were rendered entirely ineffective. If one tracks down the cause of this, it will be clearly seen that these nations failed to keep in mind that reality of monotheism which is recalled in this verse.

The upcoming subject (Verses 41 to 50): Next, in several verses, He pointed to the collusion between the hypocrites and the Jews, which they had established to escape the grip of Shariah rulings and laws—especially limits and penal laws—and to protect each other from them. Although by the time of the revelation of these verses, Islam had gained political power in Medina and its surrounding areas, this power was not yet absolute. The Jewish settlements around maintained a tribal type of authority in their territories, and their judges decided the disputes of those within their sphere of influence or who referred to them. However, these courts had become entirely dead and useless from the perspective of establishing truth and justice. First of all, the Jews themselves had distorted the law to fit their interests, rendering it ineffective; secondly, falsehood and bribery had such a strong grip among them that neither the testimony of witnesses had any weight left nor the justice of the courts. Witnesses and judges could easily be bought through bribery and used for one's purpose. This dual authority and the selling of justice by the Jewish courts provided a backdoor for those who wanted to evade the requirements of the law. Thus, the hypocrites and the Jews, to exploit this situation, committed the mischief of referring to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in matters where they expected his court's decision to align with their desires, but referred to the Jewish courts in matters where they did not expect his decision to align with their desires, so they could obtain decisions of their choice through false witnesses and bribery. The Quran expressed grief over this wretched path of theirs, and granted the Prophet the option to either accept their cases or reject them. However, if he decided to accept them, he must decide in accordance with the law of justice revealed by Allah.

Afterward, He expressed wonder at the state of the Jews: while knowing what the ruling of the Torah is in a certain matter, they make you a judge, and then turn away from the decision you make. This is proof that they have no faith in anything; they only want to follow their desires.

Following this, He first referred to the Torah and the Gospel: Allah revealed these scriptures as guidance and light, informing people of His commands and laws through them, and making their custodians witnesses and trustees over them, while informing them that those who do not judge in accordance with what is revealed in these books are disbelievers, wrongdoers, and defiantly disobedient. Yet, the Jews and Christians threw these books behind their backs and followed their desires and innovations. Then, He mentioned the Quran: Allah has now revealed this Book, which serves as a final decider among all differences and a guardian and criterion over previous scriptures; therefore, decide every matter in its light, and do not care for the innovations of the Jews and Christians. These Jews and Christians are not searchers of truth, which is why they will keep beating the same track they have been beating. Leave them to their state. The path of truth is found by those who seek it. If Allah had willed, He would have made everyone a single nation, but He granted people the blessing of choice to test them—to see who chooses the path of truth and who chooses falsehood. Therefore, instead of corrupting your path by following them, race toward good deeds. Tomorrow, everyone's matter will be presented in the court of God, where all differences will be decided.

Finally, He emphasized to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): No matter how much pressure the Jews and Christians exert, do not pay any heed to their innovations and desires in opposition to the Book of Allah under any circumstances. If they persist in turning away from the Book of Allah, then know that the time has come for Allah to punish them for some of their crimes in this world. In this light, recite the upcoming verses:

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الرَّسُوْلُ لَا يَحْزُنْكَ الَّذِيْنَ يُسَارِعُوْنَ فِي الْكُفْرِ مِنَ الَّذِيْنَ قَالُوْٓا اٰمَنَّا بِاَفْوَاهِهِمْ وَلَمْ تُؤْمِنْ قُلُوْبُهُمْ ڔ وَمِنَ الَّذِيْنَ هَادُوْا ڔ سَمّٰعُوْنَ لِلْكَذِبِ سَمّٰعُوْنَ لِقَوْمٍ اٰخَرِيْنَ ۙ لَمْ يَاْتُوْكَ ۭيُحَرِّفُوْنَ الْكَلِمَ مِنْۢ بَعْدِ مَوَاضِعِهٖ ۚ يَقُوْلُوْنَ اِنْ اُوْتِيْتُمْ هٰذَا فَخُذُوْهُ وَاِنْ لَّمْ تُؤْتَوْهُ فَاحْذَرُوْا ۭ وَمَنْ يُّرِدِ اللّٰهُ فِتْنَتَهٗ فَلَنْ تَمْلِكَ لَهٗ مِنَ اللّٰهِ شَـيْـــــًٔـا ۭ اُولٰۗىِٕكَ الَّذِيْنَ لَمْ يُرِدِ اللّٰهُ اَنْ يُّطَهِّرَ قُلُوْبَهُمْ ۭلَهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيَا خِزْيٌ ښ وَلَهُمْ فِي الْاٰخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيْمٌ

**The Messenger's Responsibility:** 'O Messenger...' Addressing him with the title 'Messenger' (*Rasūl*) highlights the reality that the messenger's core responsibility is solely to convey the religion of Allah and fulfill the duty of warning and giving glad tidings; he bears no responsibility for how people behave toward his call. If the messenger has fulfilled his duty of conveyance, he is absolved of his responsibility before God. If people race into disbelief, the question regarding it will not be asked of the messenger but of the people themselves; why then should the messenger grieve over what belongs to others? Since the purpose here is to console the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) over the hostile and conspiratorial path of the hypocrites and Jews, and to clarify that those whose falling into trial is decreed according to the law of God will surely fall into it, addressing him with 'O Messenger' was fitting, so his limit of responsibility would be clear from the address itself. Ahead, this hidden reality of the address is clarified in the words: 'And he for whom Allah intends trial—never will you possess for him from Allah a thing.' Further discussion on this will also come under verse 67.

**The Hypocrites' Friendship with the Jews:** 'who race into disbelief.' Racing into disbelief here refers to the hypocrites' friendship with the Jews, as these people look to the Jews as a refuge and sanctuary to flee from God and His Shariah. Although they claim faith with their tongues, when a dispute or case arises, their utmost effort is to take it to the Jewish courts instead of presenting it in the court of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), so they can obtain a decision of their choice. Yet, referring to anyone else's court while the court of Allah and His Messenger is present is leaving faith and Islam to return to disbelief.

**The Hypocrites' Favorite Food:** 'Listeners to falsehood.' Just as the verb *sami'a* means to hear, it also means to accept, and the particle *li* (to) here expresses the meaning of association. This describes that attribute of the hypocrites which explains their racing into disbelief: their favorite food is falsehood; they are lovers of falsehood and customers of falsehood, wanting false testimony, a false court, and a false decision. For this reason, they feel uneasy in the Prophet's court and run toward the Jews, because the market of the commodity they want to buy is abundant in that very place.

**The Hypocrites are Puppets in the Hands of the Jews:** 'listeners for another people who have not come to you.' This is the second attribute of these hypocrites, and since it is a reflection of the first attribute, it is mentioned without a coordinating conjunction. That is, if these hypocrites come to you, they do not come out of their own desire or search for truth and justice, but come sent and instructed by others. This points to the Jewish scholars and leaders, whose state is described as sitting behind the curtain, manipulating these puppets while they dance to their tunes; they accept whatever they say, and execute whatever is their hint.

**Removing Words from Their True Context is Distortion of Religion:** 'They distort words from after their [proper] usages. They say, "If you are given this, take it; but if you are not given it, then beware."' This exposes the behind-the-scenes conspiracies of those Jews, showing who these hypocrites are disciples of, from whom they receive inspiration, what their mischiefs are, and what lesson they instruct them with before sending them to you. One of their mischiefs is described as 'they distort words from after their usages.' This was also mentioned under verse 13. The only difference is that there it was 'distort words from their usages' (*'an mawādi'ihi*), and here it is 'from after their usages' (*min ba'di mawādi'ihi*). Although the core reality is the same in both places, this second style has made the meaning of the verse relatively clearer. A noun is omitted in the genitive construction (*mudāf mahdūf*) according to the general rule of Arabic grammar. The meaning is: despite the true context, target, and application of God's word and commands being established, they remove it from its proper place, entirely defeating the purpose of the command. If the context and application of a command are not clear, leading a judge or jurist to err in applying it, they can be excused; but removing a command from its proper place despite its application being established is open distortion of religion. We explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah that just as the Jews committed verbal distortions in their scriptures, they committed this distortive misapplication, especially in the domain of limits and penal laws, where they used the knife of distortion so mercilessly that no limit or punishment of the Shariah could remain safe from it. Regrettably, in committing this type of distortion, this Ummah has also not lagged far behind the Jews.

Their second mischief is described as instructing these hypocrites before sending them to you: if your dispute is decided in a certain way, accept it; but if it is not decided that way, do not accept it. Thus, they first open a way of escape for the criminal through the backdoor of distortion, and if they feel any difficulty in that, they refer the matter to the Prophet's court, but with the instruction to accept the decision only if it aligns with their desires, otherwise to turn away. The Quran's purpose behind exposing all this is to say: Leave those who are trapped by such mischief-makers, and are themselves lovers of mischief, to their fate; do not grieve over their state.

**The Divine Law Concerning Guidance and Misguidance:** 'And he for whom Allah intends trial—never will you possess for him from Allah a thing.' This states the divine law concerning guidance and misguidance, details of which have been explained at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah and in multiple other places. Allah granted humans the capacity to distinguish between truth and falsehood, good and evil, along with the blessing of choice, and tested them with both conditions: evil and falsehood present themselves to him from the soul and Satan, while good and truth present themselves from nature and the Merciful God. In this way, his intellect and will are tested to see whether he chooses good and truth or evil and falsehood. The law of God is that those who knowingly and deliberately prefer evil over good and falsehood over truth, taking no lesson from God's warnings nor advice from the people of truth, gradually render their conscience, intellect, and will so blunt and insensitive that no resolve to move toward the truth remains within them. Falsehood becomes their very covering. No matter how much you shake them and wake them, they refuse to leave this bed. These are the ones whom Allah leaves fallen face-down in the very trial they fell into. Out of sincere concern and love for the truth, the messenger exerts all his power to wake them, and when they do not wake, he sometimes grieves that perhaps their not waking is the result of some shortcoming on his part. The Mercy to the Worlds (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) also felt a similar sentiment. Upon this, Allah consoled him: the racing of these people into disbelief is because they have fallen under the grip of the divine law, and when someone falls under the grip of this divine law, who can possibly turn them away from falsehood to bring them to the path of truth?

**The Stage of Sealing the Hearts:** 'Those are the ones for whom Allah did not intend to purify their hearts.' This points to that divine law expressed in the Quran by the words 'sealing of the hearts' or 'rust' (*rayn*). The meaning is: for the purification and cleansing of hearts, there is a specific law with Allah. Those who walk on the path of goodness and piety, if they slip or fall along the way, rise again after falling, dusting themselves off and walking again through repentance and reform—no matter how many times they fall and rise, the stain does not settle on their heart; Allah continues to make their repentance and reform an expiation for their misdeeds. But those who make evil and disobedience their profession, remaining wallowed in the mud of sins and finding comfort in it, gradually have such darkness settle on their hearts that no polishing can affect them; then God leaves them for the furnace of Hell.

Having understood the components of the discourse, let us refresh this point from the perspective of structure: having mentioned those clauses of the divine covenant that proved to be a stumbling block for other nations, He now pointed to those backdoors through which they previously sought escape and still want to use. The purpose of pointing to these backdoors, as is clear from the spirit of the discourse, is to warn this Ummah: do not become evaders of God's covenant like previous nations did, but remain firm and steadfast upon it under all circumstances. Otherwise, just as disgrace in this world and a great punishment in the Hereafter was decreed for them, you too will deserve it. God's law is uniform and impartial for all.

سَمّٰعُوْنَ لِلْكَذِبِ اَكّٰلُوْنَ لِلسُّحْتِ ۭ فَاِنْ جَاۗءُوْكَ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ اَوْ اَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ ۚ وَاِنْ تُعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ فَلَنْ يَّضُرُّوْكَ شَـيْـــًٔـا ۭوَاِنْ حَكَمْتَ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِيْنَ 

**The Meaning of 'Suht':** 'devourers of *suht*.' *Suht* means unlawful gain. While unlawful gain can take various forms, the dominant usage of this word is for bribery. In this very meaning it is used here, and wherever it appears in the Quran, it carries this meaning.

**Falsehood and Bribery Destroy the System of Justice:** This describes further attributes of the group of hypocrites and Jews mentioned above: they are lovers of falsehood and inveterate devourers of bribes. It should noted that 'listeners' (*sammā'ūn*) and 'devourers' (*akkālūn*) are intensive active participles, and since they are mentioned as attributes of the entire group, it reveals that both of these diseases had seized the Jews and their associates like an epidemic, embroiling their entire nation. Falsehood and bribery are two such things that if they spread in a nation, truth and justice are eradicated from within them.

Establishing truth and justice, as mentioned in the verse 'be persistently standing firm for Allah, witnesses in justice,' depends on two things: first, the presence of those who give impartial testimony to the truth; second, the presence of those who make impartial decisions in accordance with the law of justice and equity. These two are the backbone of the system of truth and justice, and falsehood and bribery eradicate both. The word *suht*, used here for bribery, originally denotes the meaning of eradication in the Arabic language. I believe it was used for bribery for this very reason: that it demolishes the entire foundation of truth and justice.

**The Primary Duty of the Nation Carrying the Shariah:** The greatest divine duty laid upon the 'witnesses over mankind' and 'establishers of justice' is that they must be givers of testimony to the truth, maintainers of the truth, and impartial deciders according to the truth. This is the foundation of establishing and strengthening the covenant that Allah took from every nation whom He blessed with His Book and Shariah. However, fulfilling this duty is possible only as long as people do not develop a taste for falsehood and bribery. When a taste for falsehood is developed, when giving false testimony becomes a profession for many, when those who sell testimony, teach false testimony, turn falsehood into an art, and advocate for falsehood are not only born in every class of society but are also looked upon with respect and addressed with highly honorable titles, and when bribery becomes common—those who possess authority and decide matters turning their authority and justice into a commodity to be bought and sold, so that whoever can buy them, whether oppressor or oppressed, can use their authority, power, tongue, and pen in his favor—it means that truth and justice have been buried within that nation, and they have torn the covenant of God to pieces.
