**A Plot of the Jews and its Counter:** 'So if they come to you, judge between them or turn away from them. And if you turn away from them—never will they harm you at all.' He stated: These people who come to you sent by others, harboring the intention in their hearts to accept the decision only if it aligns with their desires and reject it otherwise—regarding such people, you have the option to take the responsibility of deciding their case or not. The meaning is: your responsibility to establish justice and equity relates fundamentally only to those who are within your sphere of authority and obedience. Those who are outside it, whose loyalty is still divided, who come to you but also conspire with others—their responsibility is not religiously laid upon you; if you see interest in taking their cases, do so; if not, reject them. He stated: If you reject them, they will not be able to harm you at all. This assurance was given because when the Jews sent or brought such cases to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), some corrupt motive was always hidden within it; they would attempt to gain political advantage against you through it. Allah assured you: If you decide to judge between them, do so in accordance with the law of justice and equity He revealed to you, and if you choose to reject them, that option is also yours; they will not be able to harm you at all; Allah protects the one who stands firm on the truth.

**Instruction to the Ummah to Stand Firm on Truth Under All Circumstances:** 'And if you judge, judge between them with justice. Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly.' This is like a covenant taken from the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and through him from the entire Ummah: whether the decision relates to one's own people or to outsiders, it must be completely impartial and in accordance with the law of justice and equity; this is the duty of the 'establishers of justice,' and for this very purpose, Allah deposed previous nations to bring forth this Ummah. 'Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly' points to the fact that God's love is not attached to any race or lineage, but to those who establish justice and equity; whichever group remains firm on this and establishes it, Allah will love them, and whoever is loved by Allah will be prosperous and successful in both this world and the Hereafter.

وَكَيْفَ يُحَكِّمُوْنَكَ وَعِنْدَهُمُ التَّوْرٰىةُ فِيْهَا حُكْمُ اللّٰهِ ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ مِنْۢ بَعْدِ ذٰلِكَ ۭ وَمَآ اُولٰۗىِٕكَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ 

**The Double Mischief of the Jews to Evade the Shariah:** 'But how is it that they come to you for judgement while they have the Torah, in which is the judgement of Allah? Then they turn away after that. And those are not [true] believers.' This expression of wonder is not about how they make you a judge despite possessing the Torah, but about how they turn away from your decision despite knowing well that your decisions align with the Divine Shariah. It should be kept in mind that, first of all, the fundamental rulings—especially limits and penal laws—are uniform in both the Torah and the Quran. Secondly, the practice of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was that in matters where no clear guidance was present in the Quran, he would decide in accordance with the rulings of the Torah, and these Jews knew well that his decision aligned with the law of the Torah. Under such circumstances, what shamelessness and insolence it was to make you a judge, have your decision align with the very divine law they claim to believe in, and then attempt to evade this decision. In the books of commentary, a case of adultery is mentioned where the Prophet's decision was exactly in accordance with the law of the Torah, but since the Jewish scholars had created different rules for the noble and the poor in such matters, they concealed the original laws of the Torah. Thus, in that case as well, they made this attempt, but ultimately had to confess the truth. Obviously, this path is entirely contrary to the claim of faith. First of all, taking the case to your court was with the desire to perhaps find some way of escape from the law of the Torah. But when no such way emerged from there, they attempted to evade it despite knowing that your decision was exactly in accordance with the ruling of the Torah. He stated: 'And those are not [true] believers'; these people have no faith in anything.

Commentary on Verses 44-45:

اِنَّآ اَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْرٰىةَ فِيْهَا هُدًى وَّنُوْرٌ ۚ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّوْنَ الَّذِيْنَ اَسْلَمُوْا لِلَّذِيْنَ هَادُوْا وَالرَّبّٰنِيُّوْنَ وَالْاَحْبَارُ بِمَا اسْتُحْفِظُوْا مِنْ كِتٰبِ اللّٰهِ وَكَانُوْا عَلَيْهِ شُهَدَاۗءَ ۚ فَلَا تَخْشَوُا النَّاسَ وَاخْشَوْنِ وَلَا تَشْتَرُوْا بِاٰيٰتِيْ ثَـمَنًا قَلِيْلًا ۭوَمَنْ لَّمْ يَحْكُمْ بِمَآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهُ فَاُولٰۗىِٕكَ هُمُ الْكٰفِرُوْنَ ۔ وَكَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِيْهَآ اَنَّ النَّفْسَ بِالنَّفْسِ ۙوَالْعَيْنَ بِالْعَيْنِ وَالْاَنْفَ بِالْاَنْفِ وَالْاُذُنَ بِالْاُذُنِ وَالسِّنَّ بِالسِّنِّ ۙ وَالْجُرُوْحَ قِصَاصٌ ۭ فَمَنْ تَصَدَّقَ بِهٖ فَهُوَ كَفَّارَةٌ لَّهٗ ۭ وَمَنْ لَّمْ يَحْكُمْ بِمَآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهُ فَاُولٰۗىِٕكَ هُمُ الظّٰلِمُوْنَ

**The Status of the Torah:** 'Indeed, We sent down the Torah, in which was guidance and light.' This clarifies the value of the Torah: when Allah revealed it, He did not reveal it to be made a plaything, but as a means to protect the high values of life, a guide to the paths shown by God and His prophets, a guidance to the straight path, and a light to extract from the darkness of desires and innovations.

**The Path of the Sincere Custodians of the Torah:** 'The prophets who submitted [to Allah] judged by it for the Jews, as did the rabbis and scholars with what they were entrusted of the Scripture of Allah, and they were witnesses on it.' This describes the path of the true and sincere custodians of the Torah: how the obedient prophets of God and the sincere scholars and jurists themselves obeyed it, deciding the disputes and cases of the Jews in accordance with its laws and commands, while constantly keeping alive within themselves the sense of responsibility that they were made trustees, guardians, and witnesses over it by God, so that neither was any betrayal permissible for them nor was any shortcoming in displaying and declaring it allowed. This is the covenant of God that they must fulfill under all circumstances. This mirror is placed before the Jews of the time so they may see what responsibilities were laid upon them regarding the Torah, how their righteous ancestors fulfilled these responsibilities, and how they themselves have turned this divine covenant into a plaything.

**The True Purpose of the Divine Book:** In 'The prophets judged by it,' the present-preterite verb is preceded by an omitted past auxiliary verb (*kāna*) according to the general rule of Arabic grammar, meaning: the prophets used to judge by it. The word 'judged' (*yahkumu*) clearly shows that the true purpose of the Divine Book is to serve as the source of command, ruling, judgment, and decree in the affairs and disputes of life, so that all social, political, and legal matters are carried out in accordance with its guidance and in its light. If this status is not maintained, and it is kept merely as a source of blessing, or its words are merely recited, or it is considered solely a means to convey reward to the dead, while life's affairs and issues are entirely disconnected from it, and indeed commands and laws are made in open opposition to its commands, then this is making a mockery of the Book of Allah.

**A Subtle Reference Against the Jews:** Describing the prophets with the attribute 'who submitted' (*alladhīna aslamū*) reveals the reality that these prophets, who judged the disputes of the Jews in accordance with the commands of the Torah, did not consider the Torah binding only upon others, but were themselves obedient to God and bound by the commands and laws of the Torah. This is a subtle reference against those Jewish scholars who, first of all, had entirely excluded the Torah from the affairs of life, and if they did give it space in any domain, its nature was such that they commanded others to follow it but did not consider themselves addressed by it. The Quran pointed to this state of theirs in the words: 'Do you order people to righteousness and forget yourselves?'

**A Reminder to the Jews:** *Rabbāniyyūn* (rabbis) and *Ahbār* (scholars) are words that entered the Arabic language from the People of the Book. *Rabbāni* refers to scholars, and the dominant usage of *Ahbār* is for jurists and judges. Both words are used here in their true sense, meaning godly scholars and honest, upright jurists and judges. The meaning is: just as the obedient prophets of God decided people's cases exactly in accordance with the Torah, similarly, the truth-loving scholars and upright jurists also issued their verdicts and decisions in its light. Here, too, the Jewish scholars and their jurists of the time are reminded in a highly subtle manner: among the ancestors whose descendants you are, there were those who were not thieves and dishonest regarding the Book of God like you are.

**Fear of God is the Guardian of the Divine Covenant:** 'with what they were entrusted of the Scripture of Allah, and they were witnesses on it' describes the responsibility they were made to carry, the correct sense of which guided them, facilitating them to fulfill the right of the Book of Allah. That is: Allah made them guardians and trustees over His Book, and His witnesses before creation; and every group entrusted with the Book of Allah is, in reality, its guardian and witness. These words also serve as a reminder to the Jews of the time and their scholars and jurists to look into their own hearts: how, as guardians, they broke into God's sanctuary, and how, as witnesses, they mastered the concealment of the Shariah.

'So fear not the people but fear Me, and do not exchange My verses for a small price.' Generally, commentators have taken this segment in isolation from the preceding context as an address to the Jews of the time. Although the words allow for this meaning, my inclination is that this is also connected to the preceding segment, and in accordance with the well-known style of the Quran, the address shifted from the third person (*ghā'ib*) to the second person (*hādir*). There are countless examples in the Quran of a discourse shifting suddenly from the third person to the second person. This variation brings diversity to the discourse and, by bringing the situation to life, affects the reader and listener. For example, in Surah Al-An'am: 'And the Day He will gather them all together, "O company of jinn, you have misled many of mankind..."' Then ahead: 'And thus We will make some of the wrongdoers allies of others for what they used to earn. "O company of jinn and mankind..."' This same style was used in the verse of Surah Al-Baqarah: 'And when We made the House a place of return for the people and security. And take, [O believers], from the standing place of Abraham a place of prayer.' We discussed this in detail under its context. In our view, the same style is present in this verse of Al-Ma'idah. That is: this statement was made as an address to those witnesses mentioned previously, but instead of expressing it in the third person, it was expressed in the second person to make the discourse more effective.

It should be kept in mind that wherever the Torah mentions taking a covenant from the Jews to adhere to the commands of the Shariah, Moses' emphasis on those things to which the Quran's words point is always present—namely: fearing the Lord God alone, paying no heed to anyone in His commands, and not sacrificing His Shariah for the petty benefits of the world. If you reflect, these things are the indispensable requirement of that trust and testimony mentioned in 'with what they were entrusted of the Scripture of Allah, and they were witnesses on it.' The group made a witness over the Book of Allah must fear God alone, removing the fear and awe of others from their hearts. Without this, fulfilling the responsibilities of bearing witness to the Book of Allah under all circumstances is impossible. Similarly, the community made a trustee over the Book of Allah is forbidden from betraying this trust of God for their worldly interests, or using the scissors of interpretation and distortion on the Book of Allah. If you reflect further, it was by forgetting these warnings of the divine covenant that the Jews, instead of being 'witnesses for Allah' and 'the children of Allah,' became 'listeners to falsehood' and 'devourers of *suht*,' because of which God cursed them.

**The True Disbelievers:** 'And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed—then it is those who are the disbelievers.' Since this segment is coordinate (*'atf*) with the preceding segment, the ruling that applies to that applies to this as well. That is: this outcome is also part of the warning mentioned above. The meaning is: those whom Allah made trustees and witnesses over His Shariah—if their own decisions do not align with the Shariah, they are the true disbelievers. The reason for declaring them disbelievers with this emphasis and force is the preparation Allah carried out to teach them His Shariah and Book, inform them of its responsibilities, and warn them of the dangers of this path. Those who went astray despite all this preparation stumbled in the full light of day, which is why they are blinder than all the blind. Although this verse relates to the Jews, if the exact same crime is committed by any group of Muslims—possessing authority and freedom yet failing to decide matters in accordance with the Book of Allah, and indeed openly deviating from it—their ruling will also be the same, and it must be so; its clarification is coming ahead.

'And We ordained for them therein a life for a life...' This refers to that law of the Torah mentioned in Exodus 21:23-25, Leviticus 24:20, and Deuteronomy 19:21. This reference is given to confirm what was stated in verse 43 above: in the presence of clear commands of limits and retribution in the Torah, how do these Jews make you a judge and then evade your decision? This makes it clear that it was indeed the matters of limits that proved to be the greatest stumbling block for the Jews. First, they distorted them, ruining their form. Then, those commands that escaped the hands of distortion were also evaded through various ruses.

Regarding the referent of the pronoun in 'for him' (*lahu*) in 'but whoever pardons it, it is an expiation for him,' commentators differ. One group considers the referent to be the injured person. That is: if the injured person forgives his perpetrator, not taking revenge, this good deed of his will serve as an expiation for his own sins. This is an encouragement to the injured person to forgive the criminal, as that is better. The second group—including great commentators like Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, Ibrahim, and Al-Sha'bi—considers the referent to be the perpetrator. The meaning is: if the injured person (and in the case of murder, the heirs of the victim) forgives the criminal, this forgiveness will serve as an expiation for the criminal. The government will not apprehend him, and if the criminal repents, this forgiveness will also serve as an expiation for him before God. My inclination is toward this second interpretation, which is supported by the words of the Quran.

**The True Wrongdoers:** 'And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed—then it is those who are the wrongdoers.' While expressing the same concept above, the word 'disbelievers' (*kāfirūn*) was used; here, the word 'wrongdoers' (*thālimūn*) is used. The word 'wrongdoing' (*thulm*) should not be understood in a light sense. In the Quran, it is also used for violating the greatest rights of God and His servants, which is why polytheism (*shirk*) is termed wrongdoing. Those who ignore the law of Allah's Book while possessing the Book and the freedom to act upon it violate the greatest right of God, and also violate the greatest right of their own souls and of Allah's other servants; in reality, these are the true wrongdoers. Although this verse also appears in the context of describing the crimes of the Jews, if the same crime is committed by Muslims (evidence of which is present in every Muslim country), I do not see on what basis their ruling would be different. God's law is uniform for all.

**Is Verse 45 Decisive (Muhkam) or Abrogated (Mansukh)?** This verse of retribution, as we indicated above, was introduced as a reference to a ruling of the Torah, but since no indication of its abrogation is present, and indeed the style of expression proves its decisive nature, this same law applies to this Ummah as well. This is supported by the practice of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Companions. While discussing the word 'retribution' (*qisās*) in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, we wrote that in its general usage, the word retribution is applied to both life and property; therefore, if an agreement is reached on blood money (*diyah*), or blood money itself becomes the demand of justice, the blood money will be considered the retribution. Its details are present in the books of jurisprudence.

Commentary on Verses 46-47:

وَقَفَّيْنَا عَلٰٓي اٰثَارِهِمْ بِعِيْسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ التَّوْرٰىةِ ۠ وَاٰتَيْنٰهُ الْاِنْجِيْلَ فِيْهِ هُدًى وَّنُوْرٌ ۙ وَّمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ التَّوْرٰىةِ وَهُدًى وَّمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِيْنَ ۔ وَلْيَحْكُمْ اَهْلُ الْاِنْجِيْلِ بِمَآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهَ فِيْهِ ۭ وَمَنْ لَّمْ يَحْكُمْ بِمَآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهَ فَاُولٰۗىِٕكَ هُمُ الْفٰسِقُوْنَ 

**Mutual Similarity Among Prophets is a Sign of Recognition:** 'And We sent, following in their footsteps, Jesus, the son of Mary...' Sending someone following in their footsteps means: I sent him behind them. The meaning is: on the very footsteps of those prophets mentioned above, We sent Jesus, the son of Mary, who came with the exact same purpose for which his predecessor prophets came. The phrase 'following in their footsteps' (*'alā āthārihim*) expresses the uniform nature of the prophets' call, their purpose, their temperament, character, and methodology, as well as their mutual similarity. This is among the signs of prophethood. Just as there is similarity in the leaves and fruits of the same pure tree, so that whoever recognizes one of them has recognized all of them, confusion in recognizing them occurs only to those who are either blind or choose to be blind. Those who possess insight are never deceived.

**A Prophet is Not Detached From His Call:** 'and We gave him the Gospel, in which was guidance and light and confirming that which preceded it of the Torah.' Since the sentence 'in which was guidance and light' (*fīhi hudan wa-nūr*) stands grammatically as a circumstantial clause (*hāl*), the coordination of 'confirming' (*musaddiqan*) with it is fitting. Here, regarding both Christ and the Gospel, it is stated: 'confirming that which preceded it of the Torah.' One reason for this is that a prophet is not detached from his call and mission; the second reason is that the prophecy of Christ was present in the scriptures of his predecessor prophets, which was confirmed by his sending, and this was among the proofs of his prophethood; the third is that as far as the Shariah is concerned, the Gospel did not introduce a new Shariah, but confirmed the entire previous Shariah while reforming some innovations of the Jews.

**Instruction to the People of the Gospel Upon Entrusting It:** 'And let the People of the Gospel judge by what Allah has revealed therein...' The context we established for 'So fear not the people but fear Me' is, in our view, the context of this segment as well—that is: upon entrusting the Gospel to the People of the Gospel, they were instructed to decide their affairs in accordance with the Book of Allah, otherwise they would be defiantly disobedient. Its purpose, as we indicated, is to clarify that whichever book God revealed, He revealed it specifically for people to make it a judge in the affairs and disputes of life, not to wrap it up and put it away while carrying their disputes to *taghut* or inventing arbitrary methods to settle them. Some have also read the verb as *li-yahkuma* (that he should judge). In our view, all such variations in recitations have the status of differences in interpretation. Through their recitation, these scholars also wanted to convey the same point: that the People of the Gospel were also given the Gospel specifically to decide their affairs in its light.

**The True Defiantly Disobedient:** 'and whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed—then it is those who are the defiantly disobedient.' The word 'defiantly disobedient' (*fāsiqūn*) here is not used in its general jurisprudential sense, but, as we have explained in various places, in the sense of betrayal, breaking of covenants, and rebellion against God. That is: those who knowingly, while possessing the freedom and capacity to choose, decide or seek decisions contrary to the commands and laws of Allah are disbelievers, wrongdoers, and defiantly disobedient. This warning has always been an inseparable part of the divine covenant that Allah took from the People of the Book upon handing over the Book to them.

Commentary on Verses 48-50:

وَاَنْزَلْنَآ اِلَيْكَ الْكِتٰبَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتٰبِ وَمُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِمَآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهَ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ اَهْوَاۗءَهُمْ عَمَّا جَاۗءَكَ مِنَ الْحَقِّ ۭ لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مِنْكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَّمِنْهَاجًا ۭوَلَوْ شَاۗءَ اللّٰهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ اُمَّةً وَّاحِدَةً وَّلٰکِنْ لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِيْ مَآ اٰتٰىكُمْ فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْرٰتِ ۭ اِلَى اللّٰهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيْعًا فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ فِيْهِ تَخْتَلِفُوْنَ ۔ وَاَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِمَآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهَ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ اَهْوَاۗءَهُمْ وَاحْذَرْهُمْ اَنْ يَّفْتِنُوْكَ عَنْۢ بَعْضِ مَآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهَ اِلَيْكَ ۭ فَاِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَاعْلَمْ اَنَّمَا يُرِيْدُ اللّٰهُ اَنْ يُّصِيْبَهُمْ بِبَعْضِ ذُنُوْبِهِمْ ۭ وَاِنَّ كَثِيْرًا مِّنَ النَّاسِ لَفٰسِقُوْنَ ۔ اَفَحُكْمَ الْجَاهِلِيَّةِ يَبْغُوْنَ ۭوَمَنْ اَحْسَنُ مِنَ اللّٰهِ حُكْمًا لِّقَوْمٍ يُّوْقِنُوْنَ 

**The Responsibility of the Bearers of the Quran:** Now, He stated: With the exact same responsibilities and under the same covenant, this Book is being handed over to you; therefore, judge between them in accordance with this Book of Allah under all circumstances. We explained elsewhere that in this context, 'in truth' (*bil-haqq*) means a final decider. Through distortions, the People of the Book had made their scriptures a mixture of truth and falsehood. The Quran cleared the religion of Allah of all mixtures and distortions and presented it accurately. The interpretation of 'confirming' (*musaddiqan*) has passed in multiple places.

**The Meaning of 'Muhaymin':** *Muhaymin* is originally *mu'āmin*. The second hamzah was changed to *yā*, and the first to *hā*. This word is used as an attribute of Allah, and also as an attribute of the Quran. The phrase 'the bird is *muhaymin* over its chicks' means: the bird is hovering over its chicks with its wings spread, as if taking them under its protection. 'So-and-so is *muhaymin* over such-and-such' means: so-and-so became the guardian and overseer of that thing. The Quran being *muhaymin* over previous scriptures means that the Quran is the original, reliable copy of the Divine Book; therefore, it serves as the criterion to distinguish truth from falsehood in other scriptures. Whatever is proven true on this criterion is true; whatever is proven false is distorted. The reason for using the singular word 'the Book' (*al-kitāb*) here is that prior to the Quran, the status of the Divine Book regarding the original Shariah belonged fundamentally only to the Torah; other scriptures had the status of its parts and branches.

'So judge between them by what Allah has revealed and do not follow their inclinations away from what has come to you of the truth.' Regarding the Quran, this same covenant is taken from the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and through him from his Ummah, just as the covenant regarding the Torah and the Gospel was taken from their bearers, as mentioned above. The meaning is: since this Book is now the criterion of truth and falsehood and the reliable collection of divine commands, decide between people in accordance with it, and never turn away from the truth that has come to you to follow the inclinations and innovations of those hypocrites and Jews who come to you to obtain decisions in accordance with their desires. It is obvious here without expression that leaving this truth to decide in accordance with any falsehood is disbelieving, wrongdoing, and defiantly disobedient, just as mentioned above regarding the Torah and the Gospel.

'To each of you We prescribed a law and a method. And if Allah had willed, He would have made you one nation [joint in law], but [He intended] to test you in what He has given you; so race to [all that is] good.' To understand the true context and correct meaning of this segment, take a look at the following verses of Al-Baqarah. These verses belong to the exact same context and clarify the same reality: 'And if you brought to those who were given the Scripture every sign, they would not follow your qiblah. Nor will you be a follower of their qiblah. Nor would some of them be followers of the qiblah of others. And if you should follow their desires after what has come to you of knowledge, indeed, you would then be among the wrongdoers. Those to whom We gave the Scripture know him as they know their own sons. But indeed, a party of them conceals the truth while they know [it]. The truth is from your Lord, so never be among the doubters. For each [religious party] is a direction which he faces; so race to [all that is] good. Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you forth together. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent.' (145-148).

Just as the context there proves that 'For each is a direction which he faces; so race to all that is good' was not stated to express tolerance toward the People of the Book, but to express dissociation from their path, similarly, in the verse of Al-Ma'idah under discussion, 'To each of you We prescribed a law and a method' was also not stated to express tolerance toward the People of the Book, but to express dissociation from their path, while offering consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims, inviting them to race ahead on the path of truth.

Similarly, in Surah Al-Hajj: 'For every religion We have appointed rites which they perform. So, [O Muhammad], let the disbelievers not contend with you over the matter but invite to your Lord. Indeed, you are upon straight guidance. And if they dispute with you, say, "Allah is most knowing of what you do. Allah will judge between you on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which you used to differ."' (67-69).

In the verse under discussion, 'each' (*likullin*) refers to those three groups mentioned above—namely: the Jews, the Christians, and the Muslims. He stated: We have prescribed for each of you a distinct *shir'ah* (law) and *minhāj* (method). *Shir'ah* and *minhāj* refer to that outward form and framework of the Shariah chosen in each religion to manifest the realities of the faith. For example, the worship of God is a reality manifested in different religions through various forms of prayer, sacrifice, and pilgrimage; for some realities, the form was prescribed by Allah Himself, while for others, the prophet prescribed it with the permission of Allah. This is probably why two words are used here: *shir'ah* for the former, and *minhāj* for the latter.

**The Wisdom Behind the Differences in the Shariah of Various Nations:** As far as the realities of the faith are concerned, they have always been unchanging and will remain unchanging. However, the outward forms and rites of the Shariah were prescribed differently by Allah for each nation, so this would serve as a means of testing them—to see who gets trapped in the prejudice of outward rites to turn away from the realities, and who becomes a seeker of reality, advancing to accept it in whichever form it comes to him from God and His Messenger. In Surah Al-Baqarah, within the discussion of the Qiblah, this test is mentioned as follows: 'And We did not make the qiblah which you used to face except that We might make evident who would follow the Messenger from who would turn back on his heels. And indeed, it was difficult except for those whom Allah guided. And never would Allah have caused you to lose your faith. Indeed Allah is, to the people, Kind and Merciful.' (143).

This explains the wisdom behind why Allah kept the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims facing the Qiblah of the People of the Book initially, and then, after some time, commanded them to leave it and face the Kaaba. Why did it not happen that the Kaaba was designated as the Qiblah from the very first day? He stated: Allah's wisdom demanded that this change serve as a means of distinction between the sincere and the hypocrites. Through this test, He separated the seekers of truth and the sincere followers of the Messenger from those who had associated with the Messenger merely outwardly, failing to accept any real change and remaining trapped in their past rites.

Similarly, in the verse under discussion, He consoled the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): Do not care for the hypocrites and Jews who recoil from you and the Shariah you brought. They are trapped in their past rites, and their prejudice does not allow them to free themselves to accept this truth you presented to them with an open heart. Allah prescribed a distinct *shir'ah* and *minhāj* for each nation. If He had willed, He would have given everyone a single method, but His wisdom demanded that He make this change of method a means of testing people—to see who becomes a seeker of truth and who remains merely a blind follower of tracks and a slave to outward rites. In the blessings of intellect, choice, and Shariah He grants, Allah tests people to see who benefits from these blessings, appreciates them, and distinguishes between their essence and their shell, and who remains entirely blind and deaf, serving as a mere worshiper of custom. Therefore, leave these blind and deaf ones to their state, and race with one another to win the field of nearness to God opened before you by the Prophet. Here, refresh in your mind the segment: 'O Messenger, let not those grieve you who race into disbelief.' The meaning is: if the hypocrites and their Jewish guides are racing into disbelief, there is no need to grieve over their misfortune; let the believers race to win the field of faith. 'To Allah is your return all together...'—that is, in this world, freedom is granted; anyone may choose the path of disbelief or faith, but everyone's destination is the same. Everyone must return to God, and one day this entire difference will be presented before Him, and He will judge it.

'And judge between them by what Allah has revealed...' I repeatedly think that this segment is coordinate (*'atf*) with 'So judge between them by what Allah has revealed and do not follow their inclinations away from what has come to you of the truth' mentioned above. The intervening segment 'To each of you We prescribed a law...' was introduced as a parenthetical clause (*jumlah mu'taridah*). After the parenthetical clause ended, He recalled the core point of the sequence, warning further: 'and beware of them, lest they tempt you away from some of what Allah has revealed to you.' This further warning was necessary because this stage was not easy; the opposing forces were not easily dismissed. The word 'tempt/trial' (*yaftinūka*) itself indicates that they will exert all their power to turn the Prophet and the Muslims away from the divine covenant. To protect against this danger, He warned: No matter how much power they exert and pressure they apply, you must follow only the Shariah revealed by Allah, never leaving it to follow their inclinations and innovations.

**Threatening the Opponents:** 'And if they turn away—then know...'—the meaning is: stand firm on the stance of truth; if they turn away from the Shariah of Allah, persisting in following the Shariah of ignorance, then know that their ruin has arrived, and the time has come for God's punishment to fall upon them for some of their crimes. It should be kept in mind here that Allah inflicts the punishment for the collective crimes of nations in this world; in the Hereafter, people will be accountable for their deeds individually. 'And indeed, many of mankind are defiantly disobedient' explains why they deserve to have God's punishment fall upon them. He stated: Because the majority of them are rebels and disobedient to God. 'Then is it the judgement of [the time of] ignorance they desire?'—that is: if they leave the Shariah revealed by God to seek decisions elsewhere, it means they prefer the law of ignorance over the Shariah of God, and what greater misfortune can there be for a group claiming adherence to the Shariah? For those who believe in God and the Hereafter, whose law and decision can be better than God's law and decision? If they consider any other law better than God's law, it means they have no faith in God or the Hereafter. It should be noted here that the term 'judgement of ignorance' (*hukm al-jāhiliyyah*) is used in opposition to 'what Allah has revealed'; therefore, every law contrary to the law revealed by God is a law of ignorance, whether it was developed in the darkness of the Dark Ages or in the light of the twentieth century.

'And who is better than Allah in judgement for a people who are certain [in faith]?'

The upcoming subject (Verses 51 to 66): Next, He informed the Muslims in general, and the hypocrites in particular: Do not take the Jews and Christians as your close allies and trustees; those who take them as close allies and trustees will be counted among them despite their claim to faith, and their fate will be with them. Then, He exposed the inner state of these hypocrites, showing what motives drive their racing into disbelief, and what their ultimate fate must be. In this connection, He also clarified that this path of theirs is the path of apostasy (*irtidād*), and if they wish to choose apostasy, let them; God does not care for them. If they turn back from the faith, Allah will bring in their place people who possess all the qualities of faith and sincerity—God will love them, and they will love God. They will become the party of Allah (*Hizb Allāh*), and it is this party that will ultimately be dominant.

Afterward, He appealed to the honor of these hypocrites: How do you take as close allies and trustees those who openly make a mockery of your religion and look down upon it? Then, He rebuked the People of the Book for their mischiefs, informing them of that fate which they deserved because of their wretchedness. In this connection, He also rebuked their scholars and jurists: if they do not prevent them from their vain talk and devouring of unlawful gain, what purpose do they serve? Finally, He clarified: None of these conspiracies they carry out against the true religion will be made successful by Allah; instead, they will face defeat at every turn. It would have been better for them to adopt a good path to deserve Allah's favor, but their ruin has overcome them. In this light, recite the verses:

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا لَا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَھُوْدَ وَالنَّصٰرٰٓى اَوْلِيَاۗءَ ۘبَعْضُهُمْ اَوْلِيَاۗءُ بَعْضٍ ۭ وَمَنْ يَّتَوَلَّهُمْ مِّنْكُمْ فَاِنَّهٗ مِنْهُمْ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظّٰلِمِيْنَ 

**Addressing the Hypocrites:** Although the address is general to the Muslims, the context proves that the focus of the discourse is on those hypocrites whose mention has been running from verse 41, and whose attribute was described as 'who race into disbelief.' These people, as we explained under 'listeners for another people,' were under the influence of the Jews, and while claiming faith, practically acted as instruments and agents for the Jews' objectives. They are instructed: Do not take the Jews and Christians as your close protectors. We explained elsewhere that this prohibition of friendship is conditional upon 'against the believers' (*min dūn al-mu'minīn*), which means: do not befriending them in opposition to Islam and the Muslims. If this friendship is for the benefit of Islam and the Muslims, or at least not contrary to the interest of Islam and the Muslims, it is not prohibited.

**Dealing with the Jews and Christians on a Collective Basis:** 'They are [in fact] allies of one another' points to the reality that in this matter, individual interests and individual relationships should be given no weight. It is possible that a Muslim considers an individual or group among the Jews and Christians to be good, or has some need associated with them, or has a prior family relationship, using this as an excuse to maintain ties with them; but this is not correct. They consider Islam and the Muslims as a common threat, and to counter this threat, they are allies and helpers of one another. Therefore, the Muslims' conduct toward them must not be on an individual basis, but on a collective basis. Just as they stand as a single nation against the Muslims collectively, the Muslims must stand as a single nation against them. No group of Muslims, apart from the community, should cultivate ties of trust and friendship with any of their groups based on personal interests or personal relationships. 'And whoever is an ally to them among you—then indeed, he is [one] of them'—that is: the one who bents away from the community to take them as allies and trustees will be counted among them, and his claim to Islam will remain entirely empty of reality. 'Indeed, Allah guides not the wrongdoing people'—here, 'guidance' refers to guidance to the destination, and 'wrongdoers' (*thālimīn*) refers to those who wrong their own souls. That is: those who take the enemies of faith and Islam as allies in opposition to Islam and the believers wrong their own souls, and such people will not find the way because they have abandoned the caravan walking on the straight path.

Commentary on Verses 52-53:

فَتَرَى الَّذِيْنَ فِيْ قُلُوْبِهِمْ مَّرَضٌ يُّسَارِعُوْنَ فِيْهِمْ يَقُوْلُوْنَ نَخْشٰٓى اَنْ تُصِيْبَنَا دَاۗىِٕرَةٌ ۭفَعَسَى اللّٰهُ اَنْ يَّاْتِيَ بِالْفَتْحِ اَوْ اَمْرٍ مِّنْ عِنْدِهٖ فَيُصْبِحُوْا عَلٰي مَآ اَ سَرُّوْا فِيْٓ اَنْفُسِهِمْ نٰدِمِيْنَ ۔ وَيَقُوْلُ الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْٓا اَهٰٓؤُلَاۗءِ الَّذِيْنَ اَقْسَمُوْا بِاللّٰهِ جَهْدَ اَيْمَانِهِمْ ۙاِنَّهُمْ لَمَعَكُمْ ۭحَبِطَتْ اَعْمَالُهُمْ فَاَصْبَحُوْا خٰسِرِيْنَ 

**The Hypocrites' Inner Fear:** 'So you see those in whose hearts is disease hastening into [association with] them.' The particle *fa* (so) is used for elaboration, and the address is to the common Muslims. The point stated briefly and behind a curtain in the preceding verse is now elaborated by lifting the curtain, showing who those are cultivating ties with them, what their objective is, and what their ultimate fate must be. He stated: Those in whose hearts is disease are desperate to secure friendship with them. The word 'disease' (*marad*) was discussed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. The context here proves that it refers to hypocrisy. By using the word 'disease' for hypocrisy, the Quran clarified that this action of theirs is the result of the disease and corruption of the heart, though they consider it great wisdom and foresight. 'saying, "We fear lest a misfortune should strike us."' The verb 'to say' (*yaqūlūna*) is also used in the Quran for thoughts harbored within the self, and the Quran clarified this ahead by stating: 'so they will become, over what they concealed within themselves, regretful.' Obviously, this refers to the thought expressed in the words 'We fear lest a misfortune should strike us.' *Dā'irah* means a turn of fortune, calamity, or misfortune. The meaning is: these hypocrites harbor the fear in their hearts that in this ongoing conflict between the Muslims and their opponents, no one knows which way the wind will blow; it is possible that the ultimate victory belongs to the opponents, in which case if we remain associated solely with the Muslims, we will fall into severe trouble. Therefore, it is best to maintain ties with both sides. It should be kept in mind that during the era when these verses were revealed, Muslims had indeed become a political power, but they did not yet possess complete dominance; within their spheres, the Jews and the Quraysh also possessed power. Although this power was in decline, because the hypocrites' heart-ties were with these forces hostile to Islam, they were not ready to easily believe that no possibility remained for them to stand against Islam.

'But perhaps Allah will bring conquest or a decision from Him, and they will become, over what they concealed within themselves, regretful.' Although the particle *'asā* (perhaps) is originally used to express strong probability and expectation, when the context warrants, it serves as an eloquent style to express a promise. Here, it carries this meaning. 'Conquest' (*al-fath*) refers to that final and complete victory after which the enemy's power is entirely crushed. 'A decision from Him' (*amrin min 'indihi*) refers to any development through which the hypocrites' entire secret is exposed, leaving them no place to hide. In Surah At-Tawbah, one form of this is described as: 'The hypocrites fear lest a surah be revealed about them, informing them of what is in their hearts. Say, "Mock [as you wish]; indeed, Allah will expose that which you fear."' 'and they will become, over what they concealed within themselves, regretful' points to that thought of theirs mentioned above: their hesitation to commit fully to Islam out of fear for their future, thinking that if the victory belonged to the Jews and polytheists, they would fall into trouble.

'And those who believe will say...'—this reports the wonder the Muslims will express at the state of these hypocrites when their entire secret is exposed, saying to one another: 'Are these the ones who swore by Allah their strongest oaths that indeed they were with you?' The purpose of drawing this picture of the situation is to shake the hypocrites: how long will you attempt to hide and conceal? Ultimately, one day, public disgrace is inevitable. 'Their deeds have become worthless, and they have become losers.' This can be a part of the statement of the believers, or a statement from Allah describing the fate of these hypocrites. My inclination is toward this second aspect. The reality of 'worthless deeds' (*habt al-a'māl*) was discussed elsewhere. The productivity of deeds depends entirely upon faith and sincerity. The exhibition of piety carried out alongside hypocrisy is mere exhibition; it carries no weight on the scale of reality.

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا مَنْ يَّرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ عَنْ دِيْنِهٖ فَسَوْفَ يَاْتِي اللّٰهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُّحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّوْنَهٗٓ ۙ اَذِلَّةٍ عَلَي الْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ اَعِزَّةٍ عَلَي الْكٰفِرِيْنَ ۡ يُجَاهِدُوْنَ فِيْ سَبِيْلِ اللّٰهِ وَلَا يَخَافُوْنَ لَوْمَةَ لَاۗىِٕمٍ ۭ ذٰلِكَ فَضْلُ اللّٰهِ يُؤْتِيْهِ مَنْ يَّشَاۗءُ ۭوَاللّٰهُ وَاسِعٌ عَلِيْمٌ 

**The Hypocrites' Path is the Path of Apostasy:** Although the address is outwardly to the common Muslims, the focus of the discourse is on those hypocrites whose mention has been running. He stated: Whosoever of you turns back from his religion—Allah will stand in their place to serve His religion such people who... This reveals that their path is the path of apostasy (*irtidād*) from the religion. If they do not wish to refrain from it even after this warning, let them turn apostate; God does not care for them. In such sentences, the response to the condition (*jawāb al-shart*) is omitted according to the rule of Arabic grammar, which is clear from the context. We have opened this omitted response in the translation.

**A Mirror Before the Hypocrites:** 'Allah will bring forth a people He will love and who will love Him, humble toward the believers, powerful against the disbelievers; they strive in the cause of Allah and do not fear the blame of a critic.' Describing these attributes is meant, first of all, to show what attributes those whom Allah stands in the land as witnesses and standard-bearers of His religion possess or must possess. Secondly, it shows that these hypocrites possess attributes entirely contrary to these. Thus, instead of directly listing their faults, a mirror was placed before them in which they can see all their faults on their own. This is a highly eloquent style of expressing reality which is frequently used in the Quran.

**The Desirable Attributes of the Standard-Bearers of Religion:** 'He will love them and they will love Him.' This reveals that neither does God love these hypocrites nor do they love God; rather, God dislikes them, and they are turned away and indifferent to God. God's love is not attached to someone's name, lineage, form, or wealth, but to faith, action, character, and conduct. When, in this regard, they not only stand at zero but are characterized by attributes entirely contrary to God's liked attributes, how can they deserve God's love? Similarly, the evidence of loving God is that they must be bound by God's commands and guidance and His Prophet's way and decision. But when they abandon God's decision to seek the decision of ignorance, and instead of taking God, His Messenger, and the believers as allies, cultivate ties of friendship with the Jews, Christians, and disbelievers who oppose God and His Messenger, what greater evidence can there be of their turning away from God? On this issue, take a look at what we wrote under Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 31: 'Say, "If you should love Allah, then follow me, [so] Allah will love you..."'

'humble toward the believers, powerful against the disbelievers'—*adhillah* is the plural of *dhalīl*. In Arabic, this word, as we pointed out under Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 123, is used in both good and bad senses. When used in a good sense, as is the case here, it means gentle, soft-tempered, obedient, humble, and easy-guided. The word *dhalūl* also carries this meaning; an obedient camel is called *nāqah dhalūl*. *A'izzah* is the plural of *'azīz*. This word stands in direct opposition to *dhalīl*. It means hard, difficult, heavy, invincible, impassable, and difficult to guide. If you say of something 'it is *'azīz* upon me,' it means that thing is heavy and difficult for me, and subduing it is hard. The same meaning is carried by *shadīd 'alā*. A poet of Hamasah wrote beautifully:

*If a man fails to cultivate high resolve in his youth,*
*seeking it in old age becomes highly heavy and hard.*

The meaning is: toward the Muslims, they will be highly gentle, innocent, adaptable, and ready to blend under all circumstances; but toward the disbelievers, they will be like a stone rock. If the disbelievers attempt to exploit them for their interests, they will not find any opening. This same description of Muslims is reported in a Hadith: 'The believer is innocent and noble.' That is: the believer toward his brother is innocent, noble, and generous. Jesus (peace be upon him) instructed his disciples: 'be harmless as doves and wise as serpents,' which also keeps both of these aspects in view. Describing these attributes is also meant, as we indicated above, to reflect upon the character of the hypocrites, which stood in direct opposition to this—namely: they were highly cunning and sharp in deceiving the Muslims, not allowing anyone to touch them, but were like wax and puppets in the hands of the Jews and polytheists, who turned them and manipulated them as they wished. Detailed discussion on this will come under Surah Al-Fath, verse 29: 'Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah; and those with him are forceful against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves.'

'they strive in the cause of Allah and do not fear the blame of a critic.' 'Striving' (*yujāhidūna*) here does not refer solely to fighting, but to every effort exerted to elevate the word of Allah and establish His religion. To enter this field, the primary condition is that a person must turn away from all his other interests and concerns, paying no heed to the advice and blame of critics. The one who gives weight to the blame of critics will take one step forward and two steps back. When Arabic poets describe high resolve, bravery, and generosity, they necessarily mention the blame of critics in their introduction, because this is the oldest and most inevitable trial on this path; it is impossible for a person to rise for a task of resolve without some advisers and critics holding his hem from left and right. This is the first test of this path; if a person does not possess the courage to shake off his hem and advance, he is often defeated in this very first stage. Describing this attribute is also meant to reflect upon the character of the hypocrites: they claim faith, entering the lane of devotion, but their past interests also hold their hem, their fear of future dangers deprives them of their senses, and they maintain respect for the advice of those sympathizers and critics whose knots are tied by Satan, whereas escaping them is the task of only the highly facilitated. 'That is the favor of Allah which He bestows upon whom He wills. And Allah is all-Encompassing and Knowing'—that is: the true favor of Allah is this, of which those whom He wills deserve to partake. 'Whom He wills' refers to those who prove worthy of it in accordance with the established law of Allah. We explained in multiple places that God's will operates alongside His perfect power and His perfect knowledge and wisdom, and where the will operates alongside perfect power and perfect knowledge and wisdom, there is no question of any injustice. Referencing the attributes 'all-Encompassing and Knowing' alongside the mention of the will is meant to express this reality.

**Eliminating a Doubt:** A question may arise in someone's mind: Did there not exist among the Muslims at that time those who possessed these attributes? Indeed, the majority of them possessed these very attributes. Why then did the Quran say 'Allah will bring a people,' instead of referencing those who were present and possessed these attributes in the best form? The answer is that although the address in these verses is general in terms of words, the focus of the discourse is fundamentally on the hypocrites. They are told: If you turn apostate, it will not harm God in the least; Allah will stand in your place His other servants who possess all the high attributes to fulfill the requirements of faith. Thus, by stating this, the grief of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the sincere believers, mentioned in the verse 'let not those grieve you who race into disbelief' above, was removed—warning the Prophet and the believers not to grieve over the hypocrites' friendship with disbelief. If they leave, their leaving will not harm Allah's religion in the least; Allah will bring in their place another fresh army to serve His religion, free from all the weaknesses and diseases present within them.

Commentary on Verses 55-56:

اِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ اللّٰهُ وَرَسُوْلُهٗ وَالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوا الَّذِيْنَ يُقِيْمُوْنَ الصَّلٰوةَ وَيُؤْتُوْنَ الزَّكٰوةَ وَهُمْ رٰكِعُوْنَ ۔ وَمَنْ يَّتَوَلَّ اللّٰهَ وَرَسُوْلَهٗ وَالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا فَاِنَّ حِزْبَ اللّٰهَ هُمُ الْغٰلِبُوْنَ 

What was stated in verse 51 through a negative style is now stated through a positive aspect. That is: Do not take the Jews and Christians as your allies, but take Allah, His Messenger, and the believers as your allies. Your faith (if it exists) connects you with them, not with the others. 'And those who believe' is used here in its true sense—namely: the sincere believers.

**The Practical Expression of Faith is Prayer and Zakat:** 'those who establish prayer and give zakat, and they bow down.' This stands as an appositive (*badal*) for 'those who believe,' which reveals that the practical expression of faith is the establishment of prayer and the payment of zakat. Choosing the appositive style instead of coordination (*'atf*) is proof that from the perspective of Shariah wisdom, there is no distance between faith, prayer, and zakat; they are entirely inseparable. Wherever faith is present, prayer and zakat must be present. If they are missing, it is proof that faith is also missing; if a claim is made, it is a mere claim carrying no weight on the scale of reality.

**The True Essence of Prayer and Zakat:** 'and they bow down' (*wa-hum rāki'ūn*). 'Bowing' (*rukū'*) here is not used in its technical sense, but in its general literal sense. 'The man bowed' (*raka'a al-rajul*) means: he humbled himself or his state lowered. Therefore, humility, neediness, devotion, submissiveness, and brokenness of heart represent the true essence of this word. The bow in prayer is, in reality, a practical expression of this state of the person's heart. Placing this restriction here is meant to point to the true essence of prayer and zakat, because just as without prayer and zakat, faith remains empty of meaning and spirit, similarly, without humility and submissiveness of the heart, prayer and zakat remain entirely empty of purpose. In the early phase of Islam, the hypocrites also had to perform prayer and pay zakat, without which no one could count themselves among the Muslims in that blessed era; but it was explained in detail in Surah An-Nisa that their prayers were performed under compulsion, and the Quran used the word 'lazy' (*kusālā*) for them. Similarly, their spending was merely for show. The restriction of 'and they bow down' clarified that the prayer and zakat desired in the religion are those performed with submissiveness and humility of the heart, not with show, pride, and aversion.

**The Meaning of 'Hizb Allah' (The Party of Allah):** 'And whoever allies with Allah and His Messenger and those who have believed—then indeed, the party of Allah—they will be the predominant.' A segment is omitted here for brevity of discourse. The complete statement is as follows: Whoever takes Allah, His Messenger, and the believers as allies is of the party of Allah, and it is the party of Allah that is to be dominant. Since the final segment served as proof for the omitted segment, the former was omitted. Above, it was stated that those who take the Jews and Christians as allies will one day regret their deeds, their deeds will go to waste, and they will fail. Now, He clarified the bright fate of those who take Allah, His Messenger, and the believers as allies, labeling them with the title 'The Party of Allah' (*Hizb Allāh*), while indicating that these hypocrites who take the Jews and Christians as allies are the party of Satan (*Hizb al-Shaytān*), and since Satan's scheme is weak and fragile, their failure and ruin are built into their very foundation.

Commentary on Verses 57-58:

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا لَا تَتَّخِذُوا الَّذِيْنَ اتَّخَذُوْا دِيْنَكُمْ هُزُوًا وَّلَعِبًا مِّنَ الَّذِيْنَ اُوْتُوا الْكِتٰبَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَالْكُفَّارَ اَوْلِيَاۗءَ ۚوَاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ اِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُّؤْمِنِيْنَ ۔ وَاِذَا نَادَيْتُمْ اِلَى الصَّلٰوةِ اتَّخَذُوْهَا هُزُوًا وَّلَعِبًا ۭذٰلِكَ بِاَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْقِلُوْنَ 

**Friendship with Those Who Mock the Religion is Contrary to Honor:** Now, He appealed to their sense of honor and jealousy: How can you take as allies those among the People of the Book and the disbelievers who make a mockery of your religion and turn your religious rites into a plaything? It is human nature that a person does not tolerate the insult and degradation of that which is associated with him or to which he is associated, and if anyone tolerates it, it is proof of his lack of honor. The Arabs were highly sensitive in this regard. Religion is a great matter, but they would boil with anger and draw their swords even over the insult of their family or one of its members. From the perspective of this human nature, the Quran censured them: With what heart and soul do you befriend those who mock your religion? After that, He warned in highly stern words: 'And fear Allah, if you should be believers.' That is: if you truly claim faith, fear God lest your faith be seized and God's anger fall upon you due to this lack of honor.

'And when you call to prayer...' This mentions what they mock. This points to the Azan. Just as the wicked among the Jews committed various forms of insolence in the Prophet's assemblies, as mentioned above, their base elements would mock the Azan in a crude manner, laughing at it. The Azan is the call to the worship of Allah. Mocking it, especially by those made custodians of the Divine Book, is equivalent to degrading and mocking God Himself. Such people do not deserve to be befriended by any Muslim, and if someone befriends them while claiming faith, it means he is devoid of faithful honor, and a person devoid of faithful honor can never protect his faith. 'That is because they are a people who do not understand' indicates that respecting goodness and virtue is the requirement of being an intelligent human. If a person is devoid of it, he is not only devoid of piety but also devoid of intellect.

The Azan is among the sacred symbols of Allah (*Sha'ā'ir Allāh*). This verse also provides proof of the Azan being among the great sacred symbols of Allah. Regarding how the Azan began in the community, there may be room for difference based on differing narrations. However, there is no room for difference in the fact that in the form it exists, it has received the confirmation and approval of Allah and His Book, alongside the continuous verbal and practical consensus of the Ummah. Therefore, attempting to change it into any other form is a great audacity in the religion. The matter of sacred symbols is highly significant in the religion. We discussed this in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. On the specific issue of the Azan, we will discuss further in the commentary of Surah Al-Jumu'ah.

Commentary on Verses 59-60:

قُلْ يٰٓاَهْلَ الْكِتٰبِ هَلْ تَنْقِمُوْنَ مِنَّآ اِلَّآ اَنْ اٰمَنَّا بِاللّٰهِ وَمَآ اُنْزِلَ اِلَيْنَا وَمَآ اُنْزِلَ مِنْ قَبْلُ ۙ وَاَنَّ اَكْثَرَكُمْ فٰسِقُوْنَ ۔ قُلْ هَلْ اُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِشَرٍّ مِّنْ ذٰلِكَ مَثُوْبَةً عِنْدَ اللّٰهِ ۭ مَنْ لَّعَنَهُ اللّٰهُ وَغَضِبَ عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهُمُ الْقِرَدَةَ وَالْخَـنَازِيْرَ وَعَبَدَ الطَّاغُوْتَ ۭ اُولٰۗىِٕكَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَّاَضَلُّ عَنْ سَوَاۗءِ السَّبِيْلِ 

*Naqm* means taking revenge, avenging, and venting anger on someone.

**The Jews' Vain Talk:** In connection with the Jews' mischief mentioned above, the discourse now turned toward them. They are told on behalf of the Muslims: We cannot understand any cause for all this enmity and vain talk of yours against us except that our virtue has become vice in your eyes. What is our crime? Is it that we believed in Allah, believed in the Book revealed to us, and believed in the books revealed before? In contrast, your state is that the majority of you are defiantly disobedient, having no faith in the Book revealed to you, nor being ready to believe in the Book granted to us. He stated: Tell them: In your eyes in this world, we are the worst, and because of this you seek to harm and avenge us; but do you know who is the worst before Allah in the Hereafter in terms of their fate? These are they whom Allah cursed, upon whom was His anger, of whom He made apes and pigs, and who worshiped *taghut*. These are worst in position and farther astray from the straight path.

In 'whom Allah cursed,' we consider a noun to be omitted in the genitive construction (*mudāf mahdūf*), just as in 'but righteousness is [in] one who believes in Allah.' That is: the fate of those whom Allah cursed.

**Apes and Pigs in Human Attire:** 'apes and pigs.' Within the discussion of the Sabbath-breakers, a discussion on 'apes' passed. When the link of intellect and will is missing between a person's desire and his action, and he becomes entirely a slave to his desires, no essential difference remains between him and animals. This entirely distorts his inner self, and after the inner self is distorted, the outer form also gradually distorts. The eyes that see reality can see the reflection of character in the form, no matter how much one attempts to hide it with makeup and powder. In his physical creation, man is merely an animal made of flesh and blood. Some animals walk on two legs, some on four; man receives the beauty of humanity from that divine spark expressed in the Quran as 'and breathed into him of My spirit.' If this divine spark is extinguished, then count man also as a two-legged animal, who in his nature can be an ape, a pig, a dog, or a donkey. Indeed, the example of the Jews was given in the Quran as a dog and a donkey. In this matter, there is a great difference between eyes: for those whose reach is only to the form and attire, distinguishing between man and non-man is impossible; but the eyes accustomed to entering the inner self can estimate with the slightest reflection whether an ape, a pig, a dog, or a donkey is hidden within a form. In the spiritual visions of the prophets and gnostics, many such things are found that support what we have pointed out. Had we not feared length, we would have presented some examples here.

'and worshiped taghut' is coordinate (*'atf*) with 'whom Allah cursed and upon whom was His anger...' mentioned above, and we indicated that in 'whom Allah cursed,' a noun is omitted—namely: the fate of those whom Allah cursed. Therefore, a noun will be omitted here as well—namely: the fate of those who worshiped *taghut*. The meaning is: those who, while being the People of the Book, worshiped *taghut* will be worse in their fate. Here, the crime is mentioned instead of the punishment due to its special significance. He stated: Those who worshiped *taghut* must worry about their fate; what is gained by grinding your teeth at us? 'Those are worst in position and farther astray from the straight path'—the first segment describes their fate in the Hereafter, and the second describes their path in this world, the second segment serving as proof for the first. The meaning is: they are farther astray from the highway of religion, which is why they will be worse in terms of their fate. The word *sawā'* (even/straight) was discussed elsewhere; it denotes the middle of a thing. 'The path' (*al-sabīl*) refers to that straight path which Allah opened for the guidance of His servants. The meaning is: they have abandoned the original highway to stray far away from it.

Commentary on Verses 61-63:

وَاِذَا جَاۗءُوْكُمْ قَالُوْٓا اٰمَنَّا وَقَدْ دَّخَلُوْا بِالْكُفْرِ وَهُمْ قَدْ خَرَجُوْا بِهٖ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ اَعْلَمُ بِمَا كَانُوْا يَكْتُمُوْنَ ۔ وَتَرٰى كَثِيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ يُسَارِعُوْنَ فِي الْاِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوَانِ وَاَكْلِهِمُ السُّحْتَ ۭ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوْا يَعْمَلُوْنَ ۔ لَوْلَا يَنْهٰىهُمُ الرَّبّٰنِيُّوْنَ وَالْاَحْبَارُ عَنْ قَوْلِهِمُ الْاِثْمَ وَاَكْلِهِمُ السُّحْتَ ۭلَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوْا يَصْنَعُوْنَ 

All the difficult words of these verses have been discussed. This also mentions the Jews, but it refers to that group of Jews mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 8 to 16. We explained there in detail that among the Jews, there was a group who would come to the Muslims' assemblies and say: We are also believers; we also believe in God, His Messenger, and His Book; why then do Muslims not accept us as believers? They said this with the mental reservation: If we do not believe in Muhammad and the Book he presented, what difference does it make? After all, our prophet and our book are also sent by God. The common Muslims were deceived by such statements of theirs, falling into a kind of good expectation regarding them, which is why the Quran termed their statement 'deception' (*mukhāda'ah*). When they were told: If you are believers, why do you not believe straightforwardly like the Muslims do—meaning, why do you not confess belief in Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Quran—they would become angry and say: We do not act like fools; we want peace and order in the land, and the way to it is that if Muslims want to believe in someone as a prophet, let them, but they must not make it binding upon us; they must accept our religious status without requiring us to believe in him; if Muslims declare everyone other than themselves as disbelievers, it will cause corruption in the land, harming everyone. Under the mentioned verses of Al-Baqarah, we also explained that this theory of shared faith was presented in our country under the title 'Unity of Religions' prior to the partition of India, and its echo is still heard from time to time. In the verses under discussion, this group of Jews is mentioned. He stated: When they come to your assemblies, they say, 'We also believe.' Yet, with the very disbelief they enter, they exit; faith is with them neither when entering nor when exiting; they merely want to deceive you by making this claim before you. What is in their hearts is well known to Allah. In the verses of Al-Baqarah, the Quran exposed this secret of their hearts. Therefore, in the light of our commentary, take a look at those verses.

**Exposing the Jews' Claim to Faith:** 'And you see many of them hastening into sin and aggression and their devouring of unlawful gain'—this is the Quran exposing their claim to faith: they claim faith, but their state is that day and night, their hastening is in the path of violating rights, aggression, and devouring unlawful gain. The word 'hastening' (*yusāri'ūna*) should be kept in mind. This reveals that while the occurrence of some wrongdoing or transgression or slipping into some unlawful thing is not far from faith, if devouring unlawful gain becomes someone's very covering, and his constant effort is in the path of wrongdoing and aggression, then this is an extremely evil deed being committed alongside a claim to faith. This point was stated elsewhere in the words: if their faith commands them to do this, then it commands them to do extremely evil things.

**Rebuking the Jews' Scholars and Jurists:** 'Why do the rabbis and scholars not forbid them from their saying of sin and their devouring of unlawful gain?' This rebukes their scholars and jurists: why do they not stop them from these evils and unlawful gains? This clarifies the primary duty of scholars, and also sheds light on the final stage of decline the Jewish society had reached at that time. When the patient enters the final stage of disease, and the physicians begin to consider death itself as the cure, what doubt remains as to the destruction of the patient? 'their saying of sin' (*qawlihimu al-ithm*) refers to those sinful statements, some of which were mentioned above and some are coming ahead, and also to false testimony, which had turned into a profession among the Jews despite being made witnesses over truth and justice. The moral and faithful death of the Jewish scholars rebuked here occurred, as the context of the verse proves, because they themselves committed those very acts in which the majority of their nation was embroiled. Under such circumstances, how could their tongues open against these evils?

وَقَالَتِ الْيَھُوْدُ يَدُاللّٰهِ مَغْلُوْلَةٌ ۭغُلَّتْ اَيْدِيْهِمْ وَلُعِنُوْا بِمَا قَالُوْا ۘبَلْ يَدٰهُ مَبْسُوْطَتٰنِ ۙ يُنْفِقُ كَيْفَ يَشَاۗءُ ۭ وَلَيَزِيْدَنَّ كَثِيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ مَّآ اُنْزِلَ اِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَانًا وَّكُفْرًا ۭ وَاَلْقَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ الْعَدَاوَةَ وَالْبَغْضَاۗءَ اِلٰي يَوْمِ الْقِيٰمَةِ ۭكُلَّمَآ اَوْقَدُوْا نَارًا لِّـلْحَرْبِ اَطْفَاَهَا اللّٰهُ ۙوَيَسْعَوْنَ فِي الْاِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوَانِ وَاَكْلِهِمُ السُّحْتَ ۭ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوْا يَعْمَلُوْنَ 

**The Jews' Insolence Against Allah:** 'And the Jews say, "The hand of Allah is chained."' Above, He mentioned their mocking of the religion and its sacred symbols. Now, another insolence (sinful statement) of theirs is mentioned: they say that the hands of God are tied. 'His hand is chained'—meaning: it is tight. This is the same statement that passed in Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 181: 'Allah has certainly heard the statement of those who said, "Indeed, Allah is poor, while we are rich."' We explained there that when the Quran invited the Muslims to spend in the cause of Allah, choosing the effective style of 'who is it that will loan Allah a goodly loan,' the Jews, to humiliate Islam and the Quran and discourage the Muslims, turned this invitation into a joke, saying: These days, the God of the Muslims has become very poor, having to ask loans of His servants; God is poor, and we, His servants, are rich. The exact same statement from that occasion is reported here. The Jews say: These days, God's hand is very tight, and the situation has reached the point of asking loans from His servants. 'Chained are their hands, and they are cursed for what they said' is a parenthetical clause (*jumlah mu'taridah*), serving as a curse and repelling upon this insolence of theirs. This immediate curse and repelling occurred because the Jews were neither ignorant of Allah and His glory, nor of this eloquent style of inviting to spend. They knew everything well, but in their enmity toward the Quran and the Prophet, they were so blind and deaf that they would exploit any opportunity for humiliation and mockery, paying no heed to how far the statement would go.

**The True Cause of the Jews' Mischiefs:** 'Nay, both His hands are extended; He spends as He wills...' This refutes their statement while exposing the true cause of this insolent behavior: the jealousy over this Book revealed to you has made them so uncontrolled that they utter whatever comes to their mouths. The burning of hatred and jealousy among the Children of Israel against the Muslims because of the Quran was discussed in detail in Surah Al-Baqarah and Ali 'Imran, and some highly important indications have passed in this Surah as well. The Jews knew well that the Arabs receiving the Quran was not merely receiving a book, but along with it, that leadership and authority was also transferring to them, of which they themselves had been the sole monopolizers. This jealousy made them rebels against God, and sowed the seeds of enmity and malice within them toward the Muslims forever.

**Consolation to the Muslims that No Mischief of the Jews Will Succeed:** 'Every time they kindled the fire of war [against you], Allah extinguished it.' This is consolation to the Muslims: though in the heat of this enmity and jealousy, they will continually kindle the fire of war against you, Allah will not allow any of their corrupt efforts to succeed. Rather, whenever they kindle the fire of war, God will extinguish it. It should be kept in mind that whenever the disbelievers launched an attack against the Muslims, the conspiracy and incitement of the Jews were always involved. We pointed to this previously, and in Surah Al-Anfal, we will discuss it in detail.

**Corruption in This World is Contrary to the Divine Will:** 'And they strive throughout the land [to cause] corruption, and Allah does not love the corrupters.' This explains the reason behind the preceding statement, which is the requirement of the divine attributes. In the commentary of Al-Baqarah, we explained that all corruption in the land is caused by violating the divine law and opposing it. This causes a conflict between the physical and legislative systems of the universe, removing the blessings of this earth and opening the way for those trials through which the world becomes a playground for Satan. The Creator of the universe, who made this world, desires its reform and well-being; therefore, He grants respite to these corrupt efforts only to the extent demanded by His wisdom of testing. He does not allow them to go beyond this limit. What He likes is the effort of those who are the standard-bearers of the system of truth and justice in this world. This is what aligns with the collective system of this universe and the nature of Allah. Therefore, if they fulfill the requirements of bearing witness to truth and justice, He helps them, and despite the oppositions, plots, and wars of the corrupters, He blesses them with prosperity and victory.

Commentary on Verses 65-66:

وَلَوْ اَنَّ اَهْلَ الْكِتٰبِ اٰمَنُوْا وَاتَّقَوْا لَكَفَّرْنَا عَنْهُمْ سَيِّاٰتِهِمْ وَلَاَدْخَلْنٰهُمْ جَنٰتِ النَّعِيْمِ ۔ وَلَوْ اَنَّهُمْ اَقَامُوا التَّوْرٰىةَ وَالْاِنْجِيْلَ وَمَآ اُنْزِلَ اِلَيْهِمْ مِّنْ رَّبِّهِمْ لَاَكَلُوْا مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَمِنْ تَحْتِ اَرْجُلِهِمْ ۭمِنْهُمْ اُمَّةٌ مُّقْتَصِدَةٌ ۭ وَكَثِيْرٌ مِّنْهُمْ سَاۗءَ مَا يَعْمَلُوْنَ 

'And if only the People of the Book had believed...' The context proves that 'belief' here refers to believing in the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). That is: if these People of the Book, instead of this path of jealousy and hostility they chose, had adopted the path of faith and piety, Allah would have forgiven their past sins and entered them into the Gardens of Pleasure.

'And if only they had upheld the Torah and the Gospel...' After the reward of the Hereafter, this points to the worldly blessings of this faith: the People of the Book assume that if they accept this call, they will be deprived of that leadership and authority they have enjoyed, whereas this is merely their foolishness. If they had accepted it, the doors of blessings from both the heaven and the earth would have opened for them; but only a few among them emerged as moderate and upright, while the majority are corrupt and evil-doers. 'and what was revealed to them from their Lord' clearly refers to the Quran. Referencing the upholding of the Torah and the Gospel alongside the Quran is meant, first of all, to show that upholding this Book is not merely upholding it, but is, in reality, upholding the Torah and the Gospel as well, because in accordance with the prophecies of the Torah and the Gospel, this is what upholds them. They ruined the Torah and the Gospel, and now their love for this world prevents them from accepting this Quran, whereas accepting it did not mean being deprived of this world; if they had upheld the Torah and the Gospel, and now accepted this final Book of Allah, sharing in the effort to establish it, the heaven and the earth would have poured forth their treasures for them. In Surah Al-A'raf, this same concept is stated as: 'And if only the people of the cities had believed and feared Allah, We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth.'

'Upholding' (*iqāmah*) means establishing their connection with the affairs of life. We explained above that Allah desires His servants to spend their individual and collective lives in accordance with His commands and laws. If life is disconnected from the Book of Allah, then no matter how much praise is sung of the Book of Allah with the tongue, this is not upholding the Book of Allah, nor can anyone attain the status of the 'establishers of justice' through this praise; rather, this is open breach of the covenant and demolition of the Divine Shariah. 'Among them is a moderate community'—*qasd* means the straight path; 'he is on the path' (*huwa 'alā qasdin*) means: he is on guidance. From this is: 'so-and-so was moderate in his affair' (*iqtasada fī amrihi*)—meaning: he is on the straight path. This mentions that small group among the People of the Book who, despite the comprehensive corruption of the situation, remained firm on the truth to the extent of their capability, and ultimately embraced Islam.

The upcoming subject (Verses 67 to 86): First, He addressed the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), emphasizing: Pay no heed to these People of the Book who are intoxicated by the delusion of their superiority and authority, wanting you to also accept their presumed status. Your responsibility as a Messenger is to declare and announce this truth revealed to you from God, without fearing the blame of any critic. This is not your personal word in which you can make any alteration out of regard for someone. This is the message of God, and you have been sent as a Messenger specifically to convey this message to its addressees. If even a single letter of it is altered, it means you have fallen short in fulfilling your duty of conveyance. If they become your enemies because of it, let them; do not worry about them. Allah will protect you, and He will not allow any of their plots against you to succeed.

Afterward, in highly clear words, He announced through the tongue of the Prophet, addressing the People of the Book: Until you uphold the Torah, the Gospel, and the Quran, you possess no religious status before God; no connection is attained with God based on association with a group, but based on faith in Allah and the Last Day, and righteous deeds. Then, He detailed the disbelief of both the Jews and Christians and their disbelieving deeds and beliefs, referencing the curse Hazrat David and Hazrat Jesus pronounced upon the Jews due to their disbelief, so it may become clear that the issue of their disbelief did not arise today, but is an old story.

Finally, He praised in highly beautiful terms that group among the Christians who remained firm on the truth. Thus, they recognized the call of the Quran as the voice of their own hearts, advancing with spiritual enthusiasm to accept it. In this light, recite the verses:

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الرَّسُوْلُ بَلِّــغْ مَآ اُنْزِلَ اِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَّبِّكَ ۭوَاِنْ لَّمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهٗ ۭوَاللّٰهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكٰفِرِيْنَ 

**An Important Message to the People of the Book:** 'O Messenger, announce that which has been revealed to you from your Lord.' This verse serves as an introduction to that great message which is now being entrusted to the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to convey to the Jews and Christians exactly as it is. This message extends ahead from verse 68 to verse 86, and in it, both groups are informed in highly clear terms: Until you uphold the Torah, the Gospel, and this final Book of Allah—the Quran—you possess no religious status. No connection is attained with God based on association with a group, but based on faith and righteous deeds, and you have become entirely devoid of them. The Jews broke Allah's covenant, denied His messengers, murdered them, and obstinately became blind and deaf. The Christians, in direct opposition to the teachings of Christ, adopted the deviations spread by the polytheists as their religion, falling into disbelief and polytheism. This message was highly significant. This final blow was being struck at the religious status of both the Jews and Christians, at the very moment when they were exerting all their power to have the Muslims accept their religious status.
