For this reason, He addressed the Prophet specifically with the title 'Messenger' (*Rasūl*) before handing this message to him, which points to the fact that a messenger is God's messenger; therefore, it is his duty to convey whatever is revealed to him from God to its addressees exactly as it is, regardless of what stir is caused among them by this message and how they deal with the message and the messenger. 'and if you do not, then you have not conveyed His message' is a further emphasis: if, out of regard for the addressees or fear of their expected reaction, any shortcoming occurred in this, this shortcoming would be in the very fulfillment of that duty of conveyance for which Allah appoints someone as His messenger. It should be kept in mind that although the outward address in this further emphasis is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), in reality, it is directed toward the Jews and Christians. Allah did not choose to address them directly; therefore, He addressed the Prophet to clarify the importance of this message, and with what certainty and final decision its sending is approved. In 'And Allah will protect you from the people,' although 'the people' (*al-nās*) is general, the context proves that it refers to the People of the Book, especially the Jews. Although they were constantly engaged in plots against the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims, after this upcoming announcement, they, completely despairing of any expectation of compromise, could prepare to play their final hand. For this reason, Allah assured you: Do not care for their hostility and enmity. God will protect you from every evil of these devils. 'Indeed, Allah does not guide the disbelieving people'—Allah will not allow any of their plots against you to succeed. We explained in detail elsewhere that the verb 'to guide' (*hadā/yahdī*) also means to make someone successful in their efforts and designs. Above, in the verse 'Every time they kindled the fire of war, Allah extinguished it...', this same concept passed in other words.

Commentary on Verses 68-69:

قُلْ يٰٓاَهْلَ الْكِتٰبِ لَسْتُمْ عَلٰي شَيْءٍ حَتّٰي تُقِيْمُوا التَّوْرٰىةَ وَالْاِنْجِيْلَ وَمَآ اُنْزِلَ اِلَيْكُمْ مِّنْ رَّبِّكُمْ ۭوَلَيَزِيْدَنَّ كَثِيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ مَّآ اُنْزِلَ اِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَانًا وَّكُفْرًا ۚ فَلَا تَاْسَ عَلَي الْقَوْمِ الْكٰفِرِيْنَ ۔ اِنَّ الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا وَالَّذِيْنَ هَادُوْا وَالصّٰبِـُٔــوْنَ وَالنَّصٰرٰى مَنْ اٰمَنَ بِاللّٰهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْاٰخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُوْنَ 

**The Text of the Message:** This is the message the Prophet was commanded to convey to the People of the Book, whether Jews or Christians—namely: Until you uphold the Torah, the Gospel, and that which was revealed to you from your Lord, you possess no foundation. You have made yourselves a chosen nation, the beloved of God, the descendants of the chosen and beloved, protected from the punishment of the Hereafter, the specific family of the Lord, and who knows what else; but these are all false desires and dreams, and when your eyes open, you will see that you have been flying in the air and decorating imaginary palaces. We explained above that upholding the Torah, the Gospel, and the Quran means establishing their connection with the affairs of life. In this Surah, it was discussed in detail that these are the scriptures of God's covenant. In them, God gave His commands and laws, taking a covenant that life must be spent in accordance with them, and mutual disputes decided in accordance with them. For this responsibility, their bearers were granted the title 'establishers of justice' and 'witnesses for Allah.' If this covenant has been torn to pieces, and practically no connection remains with them, or remains only to the extent of obtaining justification for one's desires, on what basis do your claims of religious leadership, holiness, and nearness to God stand? What connection do such people have with God, and what concern does God have with such people?

**'What was revealed...' refers to the Quran:** 'and what was revealed to you from your Lord' refers to the Quran. There is no room to interpret it as anything other than the Quran. Ahead, He immediately expressed this in the words 'what was revealed to you from your Lord' (*mā unzila ilayka min rabbika*), making it completely clear that it refers to the Quran. Expressing the Quran here in the words 'what was revealed to you from your Lord' (*mā unzila ilaykum min rabbikum*) carries an aspect of completing the proof against the People of the Book. That is: in both the Torah and the Gospel, Allah took a covenant from the People of the Book that when the Prophet came to you with these attributes, carrying the final and complete Book of God, you must believe in him, follow him, help him, and bear witness to him. This is what is pointed to here, and at this moment when the Quran has been revealed in accordance with all those attributes mentioned in previous scriptures, upholding the Torah and the Gospel means that the People of the Book must uphold this Book. Upholding it is, in reality, upholding the Torah and the Gospel.

**The Quran Kindled the Jews' Hidden Jealousy:** 'And that which has been revealed to you from your Lord will surely increase many of them in transgression and disbelief'—that is: they were supposed to become the standard-bearers of this Book, thereby becoming the upholders of the Torah and the Gospel, the task upon which a covenant was taken from them, freeing themselves of the responsibility of their covenant and becoming successful before God and creation; but what is happening is that this Book has increased many of them in transgression and disbelief. Ahead, the details of the Jews' transgression and the Christians' disbelief and polytheism are coming. It is a rule that when the direct trigger of hidden jealousy presents itself, that jealousy burns with full intensity. The People of the Book already harbored anger against the Children of Ishmael because the final Prophet was to be sent from among them, but this anger was hidden; when they saw this occur as a reality, the fire of their jealousy burned with full intensity. Yet, they were supposed to realize that if they accepted the Quran and rose to establish it, they would not be serving someone else, but fulfilling their own responsibility. Upholding the Quran, as we indicated, is not merely upholding the Quran, but is, in reality, upholding the Torah and the Gospel, because this is the fulfillment of their prophecies. But when a nation's intellect is ruined, they become blind; thus, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was consoled: Do not grieve over these people; they have invited their own destruction.

'Indeed, those who believed and those who were Jews...' This verse passed exactly in Al-Baqarah, verse 62. The only difference is that there it is *Sābi'īn* and here it is *Sābi'ūn*. There, the word *Nasārā* precedes *Sābi'īn*, while here it follows it. This is merely a variation in style. *Sābi'ūn* here stands grammatically in the nominative case (*raf'*) because of coordination with the subject. All the words of this verse were discussed in the commentary of Al-Baqarah. It appears here in the exact same context as it appeared there. This is, in reality, further clarification of the statement made to the People of the Book above: that no rank or status is attained with God based on association with a group, as the Jews and Christians assume, but based on faith in Allah, faith in the Last Day, and righteous deeds. If this is missing, even if a person claims association with the Muslims, he possesses no status before God; and if this is present, even if he belongs to the Sabiens, he will find his rank with Allah based on his faith. The purpose of this verse, as explained in Al-Baqarah, is solely to nullify group pride, not to detail the components of faith. 'Those who believed' (*alladhīna āmanū*) refers to the Muslims as a group, expressing the reality that if Muslims also become indifferent to faith in Allah, faith in the Last Day, and righteous deeds, relying solely on their group association as a guarantee for salvation, the same ruling applies to them; indeed, their name is placed at the very top of the list.

Commentary on Verses 70-71:

لَقَدْ اَخَذْنَا مِيْثَاقَ بَنِيْٓ اِسْرَاۗءِيْلَ وَاَرْسَلْنَآ اِلَيْهِمْ رُسُلًا ۭكُلَّمَا جَاۗءَهُمْ رَسُوْلٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوٰٓى اَنْفُسُهُمْ ۙ فَرِيْقًا كَذَّبُوْا وَفَرِيْقًا يَّقْتُلُوْنَ ۔ وَحَسِبُوْٓا اَلَّاتَكُوْنَ فِتْنَةٌ فَعَمُوْا وَصَمُّوْا ثُمَّ تَابَ اللّٰهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ عَمُوْا وَصَمُّوْا كَثِيْرٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۭوَاللّٰهُ بَصِيْرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُوْنَ

**The Crime of the Jews:** Now, He stated the argument explaining why these People of the Book possess no religious status before Allah: the covenant taken from them to adhere to the Book and Shariah, and to renew and remind them of which Allah sent many of His prophets and messengers one after another—they broke this covenant, and whenever a messenger came to remind them of it with what was contrary to their desires, they either denied them or murdered them. The details of this murder and denial passed in Surah Al-Baqarah.

**God's Respite Increased Their Audacity:** 'And they thought there would be no trial [i.e., punishment], so they became blind and deaf.' *Fitnah* means trial and testing. Since testing can also take the form of apprehension, and the context warrants it here, we have interpreted it as apprehension. The meaning is: when the Children of Israel committed the crimes of denying and murdering the messengers, and were not immediately apprehended, they assumed that no apprehension would ever occur. Yet, the law of Allah is not that He necessarily punishes every crime immediately. Rather, He grants respite to criminals so they may repent and reform if they wish, or fill their vessel fully. The Children of Israel took this respite as a license, becoming blind and deaf. Consequently, God apprehended them, and when they repented and reformed, Allah looked upon them with mercy. But after that, they became blind and deaf again. If they choose to remain blind and deaf, let them; God is Seer of what they do, and since He sees, He will surely punish them.

To understand the indications of this verse, see Nehemiah 9:24-38.

**Two Great Destructions Upon the Jews:** This verse mentions that the Children of Israel became blind and deaf twice, and were apprehended twice. Note the grammatical omission (*hadhf*) here: before 'Then Allah accepted their repentance' (*thumma tāba Allāhu 'alayhim*), the detail is omitted that Allah apprehended them, whereupon they repented and reformed. This reveals that after the first apprehension, they did repent and reform, but after the second apprehension, they remained blind and deaf. The invitation to repentance and reform presented to them by the Quran is being evaded by them due to their wretched deeds. In this connection, my mind repeatedly thinks that this points to those two great destructions in their history which they suffered at the hands of Shalmaneser, the King of Assyria, and Nebuchadnezzar, the King of Babylon, because of their evil deeds. Surah Bani Israel, verses 6 to 8, points to these events. There, we will discuss their details, showing how the Jews, by denying the Quran, not only closed the way to escape God's second apprehension but closed the doors of His mercy upon themselves forever.

The Crime of the Christians: After the Jews, the disbelief and polytheism of the Christians is now described so that their true reality may also be made clear, showing their weight on the scale of religion. Mention of them has passed in Ali 'Imran 33–62 and An-Nisa 171–173, where many aspects were clarified. Under the relevant verses of An-Nisa, we explained that the Christians believed in both incarnation (hulūl) and the Trinity, both of which are disbelief. Here, the disbelief of incarnation is stated. In the upcoming verse, the disbelief of the Trinity is explicitly declared. References to 'Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord' from the Gospels have been cited elsewhere. The segment 'Indeed, he who associates others with Allah...' is not part of Christ's own speech, but is a warning from Allah to the Christians.

Commentary on Verses 73-76:

لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِيْنَ قَالُوْٓا اِنَّ اللّٰهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلٰثَةٍ ۘوَمَا مِنْ اِلٰهٍ اِلَّآ اِلٰهٌ وَّاحِدٌ ۭوَاِنْ لَّمْ يَنْتَھُوْا عَمَّا يَقُوْلُوْنَ لَيَمَسَّنَّ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ اَلِيْمٌ۔ اَفَلَا يَتُوْبُوْنَ اِلَى اللّٰهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُوْنَهٗ ۭوَاللّٰهُ غَفُوْرٌ رَّحِيْمٌ۔ مَا الْمَسِيْحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ اِلَّا رَسُوْلٌ ۚ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ ۭوَاُمُّهٗ صِدِّيْقَةٌ ۭكَانَا يَاْكُلٰنِ الطَّعَامَ ۭاُنْظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ الْاٰيٰتِ ثُمَّ انْظُرْ اَنّٰى يُؤْفَكُوْنَ ۔ قُلْ اَتَعْبُدُوْنَ مِنْ دُوْنِ اللّٰهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَّلَا نَفْعًا ۭوَاللّٰهُ هُوَ السَّمِيْعُ الْعَلِيْمُ 

**'Third of Three':** Discussion on the reality of the Trinity passed in Ali 'Imran and An-Nisa. Expressing this belief in this style exposes its hideousness. It shows that although the Creator and Master of the universe is Allah, uniquely one without partner, these wrongdoers divided this divinity among three, relegating Him to the status of the third of three. 'There will surely touch those who disbelieve among them a painful punishment'—the word 'among them' (minhum) shows that although the belief in the Trinity is disbelief and a painful punishment is set for those who adhere to it, the door of repentance and reform is still open for those among them who accept the call of the Quran and reform themselves. They will save themselves from this punishment, but those who still do not desist will inevitably face this punishment. To clarify this concept, the segment 'So do they not turn to Allah and seek His forgiveness...?' was joined to it.

Proof of the Humanity of Jesus: 'The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger...'—that is: you made the Messiah, son of Mary, God, whereas he is merely one of the messengers of Allah. Countless messengers passed before him. Just as they were messengers of God, he too was a messenger of God—sharing and resembling one another in temperament, character, call, servitude, fear of God, and humanity. What then is the meaning of associating one member of this very group in divinity? 'and his mother was a supporter of truth'—his mother who gave birth to him was a highly loyal and truthful servant of God: a believer, worshiper, and obedient servant. Furthermore, both mother and son used to eat food. To sustain their lives, they were in need of food and water just as every human is. The proof for all these things is present in the Gospels themselves. How then have you accepted a creature in need of food, water, and all human necessities as God or a partner in divinity?

It should be noted that while need for food and water is proof of humanity in general, among the People of the Book, particularly the Christians, it is an accepted proof of humanity. When the angels came to Abraham to bring glad tidings of a son and to deliver punishment to the people of Lot, Abraham initially took them for humans and presented a roasted calf before them for hospitality. But when they did not stretch their hands toward the food, Abraham immediately perceived that they were not humans but angels of God.

Similarly, in the Gospels, regarding Jesus himself, when his disciples feared him, thinking he was a spirit, he reassured them by eating a piece of broiled fish in front of them to show that he was not a spirit but a man. In Luke, it is recorded: 'And as they thus spake, Jesus himself stood in the midst of them, and saith unto them, Peace be unto you. But they were terrified and affrighted, and supposed that they had seen a spirit. And he said unto them, Why are ye troubled? and why do thoughts arise in your hearts? Behold my hands and my feet, that it is I myself: handle me, and see; for a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have. And when he had thus spoken, he shewed them his hands and his feet. And while they yet believed not for joy, and wondered, he said unto them, Have ye here any meat? And they gave him a piece of a broiled fish, and of an honeycomb. And he took it, and did eat before them.' (Luke 24:36-43).

'Look how We make clear to them the signs...'—the word 'look' (unthur) is used to express wonder. Since the matter is explained here with such clarity that only the extremely dull-witted or extremely obstinate could fail to understand it, He stated: Despite this clarity, look at their distorted understanding and obstinacy, and how their intellect has turned upside down.

'Say,

'—that is: man must worship only that Being who is beneficial and harmful in the true sense. Such a Being is only the Being of God. He alone is beneficial and harmful, and He alone is the Hearing, the Knowing. What is gained by worshiping others who are neither beneficial nor harmful, nor hearing nor knowing?

قُلْ يٰٓاَهْلَ الْكِتٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوْا فِيْ دِيْنِكُمْ غَيْرَ الْجَـحِيِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوْٓا اَهْوَاۗءَ قَوْمٍ قَدْ ضَلُّوْا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَاَضَلُّوْا كَثِيْرًا وَّضَلُّوْا عَنْ سَوَاۗءِ السَّبِيْلِ 

The Christians' Extremism (Ghaluw): Although the address is outwardly to the common People of the Book, its focus is on the Christians. Their extremism was discussed in detail under Surah An-Nisa, verse 171. Just as the general disease of the Jews has been deficiency (tafrīt) in religion, the general disease of the Christians has been excess (ifrāt) and extremism (ghaluw), both of which destroy the religion. It was a manifestation of this extremism that the Christians turned Jesus from a messenger into God, and then associated his mother and the Holy Spirit in divinity as well. Regarding the system of monasticism they established, the Quran explicitly stated that this too was a manifestation of their extremism.

Christianity is Entirely an Imitation of Pagan Nations: 'and do not follow the inclinations of a people who had gone astray before and misled many.' 'Inclinations' (ahwā') refers to innovations. All innovations arise from desires. When a person wants to make his desire a religion, he invents some innovation for it and attempts to give it a religious coloring. By using this word for innovations, the Quran pointed to their true source. 'A people' points to Paul and his associates who ruined the form of Christianity, playing the drama of the Trinity in imitation of pagan nations. 'who had gone astray before' points to the fact that they gathered the materials for all these innovations from their past deviations. Before entering Christianity, they attempted to give a Christian coloring to the deviations they had been embroiled in, thereby straying from the straight path themselves and misleading others. This style of expression subtly instructs the Christians: what you consider Christianity today is not your own heritage, but is entirely imported from pagan nations and imposed upon you.

Commentary on Verses 78-81:

لُعِنَ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا مِنْۢ بَنِيْٓ اِسْرَاۗءِيْلَ عَلٰي لِسَانِ دَاوٗدَ وَعِيْسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۭذٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوْا وَّكَانُوْا يَعْتَدُوْنَ ۔ كَانُوْا لَا يَتَنَاهَوْنَ عَنْ مُّنْكَرٍ فَعَلُوْهُ ۭلَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوْا يَفْعَلُوْنَ ۔ تَرٰى كَثِيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا ۭلَبِئْسَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ لَهُمْ اَنْفُسُهُمْ اَنْ سَخِـــطَ اللّٰهوَعَلَيْهِمْ وَفِي الْعَذَابِ هُمْ خٰلِدُوْنَ ۔ وَلَوْ كَانُوْا يُؤْمِنُوْنَ بِاللّٰهِ وَالنَّبِيِّ وَمَآ اُنْزِلَ اِلَيْهِ مَا اتَّخَذُوْهُمْ اَوْلِيَاۗءَ وَلٰكِنَّ كَثِيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ فٰسِقُوْنَ 

The Prophets' Curse Upon the Jews: 'Cursed were those who disbelieved among the Children of Israel by the tongue of David and of Jesus, the son of Mary...' This returns to the mention of the Children of Israel: today, they have exaggerated the accounts of their purity and selection, but their state is that from David to Jesus, the son of Mary, every prophet has lamented their state. In the Psalms of David, such passages are found in many places, showing that David's heart was deeply wounded by the Children of Israel's covenant-breaking, and he repeatedly cursed them in his prayers and supplications. See Psalms 12:1-3, 28:3-6, 40:10-17, 109, 140:6-11. Regarding Psalm 59, commentators also clarify that it contains a curse upon the Children of Israel. To illustrate the tone of these supplications, a segment of one is presented:

'But unto the wicked God saith,
What hast thou to do to declare my statutes,
or that thou shouldest take my covenant in thy mouth?
Seeing thou hatest instruction,
and castest my words behind thee.
When thou sawest a thief, then thou consentedst with him,
and hast been partaker with adulterers.
Thou givest thy mouth to evil,
and thy tongue frameth deceit.
Thou sittest and speakest against thy brother;
thou slanderest thine own mother's son.
These things hast thou done, and I kept silence;
thou thoughtest that I was altogether such an one as thyself:
but I will reprove thee, and set them in order before thine eyes.
Now consider this, ye that forget God,
lest I tear you in pieces, and there be none to deliver.' (Psalms 50:16-22).

The Curse of Jesus: Similarly, Jesus also cursed them repeatedly, examples of which are present in the Gospels. For brevity, we confine ourselves to one example. Addressing the Children of Israel, he said:

'Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye devour widows' houses, and for a pretence make long prayer... Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye compass sea and land to make one proselyte, and when he is made, ye make him twofold more the child of hell than yourselves.

Woe unto you, ye blind guides, which say, Whosoever shall swear by the temple, it is nothing; but whosoever shall swear by the gold of the temple, he is a debtor! Ye fools and blind: for whether is greater, the gold, or the temple that sanctifieth the gold? And, Whosoever shall swear by the altar, it is nothing; but whosoever shall swear by the gift that is upon it, he is guilty. Ye fools and blind: for whether is greater, the gift, or the altar that sanctifieth the gift?...

Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye pay tithe of mint and anise and cummin, and have omitted the weightier matters of the law, judgment, mercy, and faith: these ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone. Ye blind guides, which strain at a gnat, and swallow a camel.

Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye make clean the outside of the cup and of the platter, but within they are full of extortion and excess. Thou blind Pharisee, cleanse first that which is within the cup and platter, that the outside of them may be clean also.

Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye are like unto whited sepulchres, which indeed appear beautiful outward, but are within full of dead men's bones, and of all uncleanness. Even so ye also outwardly appear righteous unto men, but within ye are full of hypocrisy and iniquity.

Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! because ye build the tombs of the prophets, and garnish the sepulchres of the righteous, And say, If we had been in the days of our fathers, we would not have been partakers with them in the blood of the prophets. Wherefore ye be witnesses unto yourselves, that ye are the children of them which killed the prophets. Fill ye up then the measure of your fathers.

Ye serpents, ye generation of vipers, how can ye escape the damnation of hell? Wherefore, behold, I send unto you prophets, and wise men, and scribes: and some of them ye shall kill and crucify; and some of them shall ye scourge in your synagogues, and persecute them from city to city: That upon you may come all the righteous blood shed upon the earth, from the blood of righteous Abel unto the blood of Zacharias son of Barachias, whom ye slew between the temple and the altar. Verily I say unto you, All these things shall come upon this generation.

O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, thou that killest the prophets, and stonest them which are sent unto thee, how often would I have gathered thy children together, even as a hen gathereth her chickens under her wings, and ye would not! Behold, your house is left unto you desolate. For I say unto you, Ye shall not see me henceforth, till ye shall say, Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord.' (Matthew 23:14-39).

Referencing These Prophets is Referencing the Entire History: It should noted that while only the curse of David and Jesus is mentioned here, the study of the Torah scriptures shows that every prophet cursed the Children of Israel. The words of some prophets are even harsher than those quoted above from the Psalms and the Gospels. I believe these two prophets are mentioned to define the beginning and the end. The purpose is to show that from David to Christ, every prophet cursed this wretched nation. David marked the beginning of the Children of Israel's political glory, of whom the Jews were highly proud, and Christ was the final link in the Israelite chain of prophethood; therefore, mentioning these two names brings the entire history before the eyes.

Crime and Persistence in Crime Cause the Curse: 'That was because they disobeyed and [habitually] transgressed.' 'because they disobeyed' (bimā 'asaw) includes all those crimes relating to the rights of God and His Shariah, and 'transgressed' (yakānū ya'tadūna) includes all the rights of servants that were trampled by their hands. 'They used not to prevent one another from wrongdoing that they did' highlights the gravity of these crimes: they not only committed crimes, but when their prophets and righteous ones attempted to stop them from these crimes, they paid no heed to them and became their enemies. This is the stage of a nation's moral and faithful destruction where they deserve to be cursed according to the law of Allah.

The Lowest Depth of the Jews' Moral Decline: 'You see many of them allying with those who disbelieve.' 'Those who disbelieve' (alladhīna kafarū) refers to the polytheists of Mecca. The Jews, while proud of their selection, displayed such mental lowliness that they maintained deep relationships of friendship and trust with the polytheists of Mecca, declaring them more guided than the Muslims. Surah An-Nisa expressed wonder at this state of theirs, and cursed them for this action: 'Have you not seen those who were given a portion of the Scripture, who believe in superstition and false objects of worship and say about the disbelievers,

? Those are the ones whom Allah has cursed, and he whom Allah curses - never will you find for him a helper.' Just so, after mentioning the curse upon them here, He listed this friendship with disbelief among the causes of this curse.

'How wretched is that which they have sent far ahead for themselves...'—'that Allah has become angry with them' explains what they have sent ahead, putting the result of the action in place of the action itself, so they may see what outcome is to face them.

'And if they had believed in Allah and the Prophet and in what was revealed to him, they would not have taken them as allies...' 'Believed' (yu'minūna) is used in its true sense. 'The Prophet' (al-nabiy) refers to Moses, and 'what was revealed' (mā unzila) refers to the Torah. The meaning is: they are completely false in their claim to believe in Allah, Moses, and the Torah. If they truly believed in Allah, their Prophet, and their Book, they would never take the disbelievers and polytheists as allies. Most of them are rebellious, and this action of theirs is the greatest witness to their rebellion.

لَتَجِدَنَّ اَشَدَّ النَّاسِ عَدَاوَةً لِّلَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوا الْيَھُوْدَ وَالَّذِيْنَ اَشْرَكُوْا ۚ وَلَتَجِدَنَّ اَقْرَبَهُمْ مَّوَدَّةً لِّلَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوا الَّذِيْنَ قَالُوْٓا اِنَّا نَصٰرٰى ۭذٰلِكَ بِاَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيْسِيْنَ وَرُهْبَانًا وَّاَنَّهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُوْنَ 

The Meaning of 'Qissisin' and 'Ruhban': The Christians of Arabia used the words qissīsīn (priests) and ruhbān (monks) for their scholars and ascetics, just as the Jews used 'rabbi', rabbāni, and ahbār for their scholars and jurists. These words entered the Arabic language through the People of the Book. Since Arabic was the common language of the Jews and Christians of Arabia, and they had great poets and writers, these religious terms became well known in Arabic literature.

The Jews are Equal to the Polytheists in Enmty Toward Islam: This verse declares the Jews equal to the polytheists of Mecca in their enmity toward Islam, which serves as a further elaboration of 'You see many of them allying with those who disbelieve.' The Quran has highlighted the shared character and alignment of these two groups in their enmity toward Islam in many places. The purpose, as we indicated, is to refute the Jews' delusion of superiority and holiness, showing into what pit they have fallen: as custodians of the Book, cultivating friendship with the idolaters of Mecca out of enmity toward Islam is the lowest depth of moral and faithful decline.

The Christians Who Followed Christ's Righteous Caliph Simon: In contrast to them, He praised the Christians as being nearest to the Muslims. The context proves that this does not refer to those common Christians who follow the Christianity invented by Paul—believing in the Trinity and atonement and leading the enemies of Islam in hostility—but to those who followed Christ's righteous caliph, Simon Cephas, remaining firm on the original teachings of Christ, free from all of Paul's innovations. The remaining righteous elements among them embraced Islam upon the sending of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Negus and his associates belonged to this believing group. Some of the context supporting this view are as follows:

First, He described them as 'those who say,

' (alladhīna qālū innā nasārā). This shows that this sect, at that time, appreciated the significance and meaning of this name and was proud of it. As proven by Christian history, this sect consisted solely of the followers of Simon Cephas; regarding the followers of Paul, we explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah that they looked down upon being called Nasārā (Nazarenes), choosing instead the new name 'Christians'. William Blaikie writes in his Bible History: 'Barnabas and Paul spent a year in Antioch converting non-God-fearers into Nazarenes; it seems in that year (44 AD), those who adopted Nazarenism were first given the new and splendid name of Christian.' (Bible History, William Blaikie, p. 397). Note the words 'new and splendid name of Christian'. This clearly shows that Paul and his followers considered the word Nasārā beneath them, and modern Christianity is entirely the invention of this Paul.

Second, He described this group as having priests and monks among them, and that they are not arrogant. Obviously, this description does not apply to modern Christians. The words 'priests' and 'monks' are used here in a highly positive sense, which does not fit the priests of the modern church system. Regarding them, He stated: 'they are not arrogant'. I repeatedly think that this points to Christ's statement in the Gospels: 'Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.' How can modern Christians, whose arrogance is such that they look down upon their original name to choose a new name, be the referent of this attribute?

Third, the upcoming verses explicitly state that these people embraced faith in the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) with spiritual enthusiasm, welcoming the Quran as if they had been waiting for it for a long time.

Commentary on Verses 83-86:

وَاِذَا سَمِعُوْا مَآ اُنْزِلَ اِلَى الرَّسُوْلِ تَرٰٓي اَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيْضُ مِنَ الدَّمْعِ مِمَّا عَرَفُوْا مِنَ الْحَـقِّ ۚ يَقُوْلُوْنَ رَبَّنَآ اٰمَنَّا فَاكْتُبْنَا مَعَ الشّٰهِدِيْنَ ۔ وَمَا لَنَا لَا نُؤْمِنُ بِاللّٰهِ وَمَا جَاۗءَنَا مِنَ الْحَقِّ ۙ وَنَطْمَعُ اَنْ يُّدْخِلَنَا رَبُّنَا مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الصّٰلِحِيْنَ ۔ فَاَثَابَهُمُ اللّٰهُ بِمَا قَالُوْا جَنّٰتٍ تَجْرِيْ مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْاَنْھٰرُ خٰلِدِيْنَ فِيْهَا ۭوَذٰلِكَ جَزَاۗءُ الْمُحْسِنِيْنَ ۔ وَالَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا وَكَذَّبُوْا بِاٰيٰتِنَآ اُولٰۗىِٕكَ اَصْحٰبُ الْجَــحِيْمِ 

The Conduct of the Good Christians Regarding the Quran: This draws the picture of the enthusiastic manner in which this group welcomed the Quran and the Final Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). In this picture, it is clearly seen that they had preserved every sign and attribute of the comforter and savior Christ had promised in the Gospels, waiting for his coming. This coming one was highly beloved because all their hopes, in this world and the Hereafter, were now attached to him. His coming was to prove the truth of their scriptures and prophets, because all had announced his coming, and they were to be honored by being included in his vanguard, because they had been designated beforehand as his witnesses and introducers before the world. His manifestation was the manifestation of that kingdom of heaven on God's earth of which Christ brought glad tidings, and in which all those servants of Allah whose hearts were free from pride and filled with the light of fear of God were to share. Thus, when they hear the verses of the Quran, seeing in them the rays of that truth for which they spent long nights of waiting, their eyes overflow with tears of joy, and they cry out: O our Lord, we believe in this Book and its bringer; write us among those who bear witness. 'write us among those who bear witness' is the affirmation of that ancient covenant by which they were bound because of carrying the trusts of previous prophets and their responsibilities. That covenant, as we indicated, was that when the final Prophet came with the Book, you must advance to bear witness before creation that this is the one prophesied in our scriptures, and then believe in him yourselves and invite others to believe in him.

'And why should we not believe in Allah and what has come to us of the truth...?' This is their own argument in support of their step: when we expect God to enter us among the righteous, what right do we have to harbor this expectation without believing in Allah and this truth that has come to us?

In 'write us among those who bear witness' (faktubnā ma'a al-shāhidīn) and then 'and we hope that our Lord will admit us with the righteous people' (wa-natma'u an yudkhilanā rabbunā ma'a al-qawmi al-sālihīn), there is also a highly subtle reference against those Jews and Christians who exerted all their power to oppose this Messenger whose witnesses they were appointed to be, yet expected—indeed claimed—that the successes and elevations of the Hereafter belonged to them alone.

The upcoming subject (Verses 87 to 120): The remaining part of the Surah, to its end, serves as a conclusion. In it, answers are given to those questions that arose or could arise later regarding the points mentioned at the beginning of this Surah, indicating that these are explanatory verses. Along with this, there is a warning: the useful questions have been answered; as for unnecessary questions, do not ask them during the revelation of the Quran. By repeatedly asking unnecessary questions, the Jews had many restrictions and chains imposed upon themselves, with the result that they could not adhere to them and ultimately fell into disbelief. Finally, in connection with the great responsibility of establishing justice and bearing witness to the truth—which serves as the central subject of this Surah and was mentioned in verse 8—He detailed the testimony that the prophets (peace be upon them) will give before Allah on the Day of Resurrection, stating that they had conveyed the truth to their respective nations exactly as it was. As for what their nations did with it, that is the responsibility of those nations. This detail is meant to inform the Jews and Christians in general, and this Ummah in particular, that in accordance with this testimony, Allah will complete the proof against every nation, and whichever nation made alterations in the religion of Allah contrary to the prophet's teaching and testimony will be held responsible. Although only the example of the testimony of Jesus is presented here—for several reasons that will be detailed in their place—the purpose is to show that Allah will take testimony from all prophets in this manner. Our Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) will also bear witness in this manner, and based on this testimony, the proof will be established against his Ummah. This is like a warning to those who are now being made establishers of justice and from whom a covenant is being taken to adhere to and establish the Shariah of God: as they fulfill their responsibilities, they must keep in mind this testimony of the Hereafter. Thus, while the covenant was taken from this Ummah at the beginning of the Surah, they are reminded of its responsibilities in the Hereafter at the end of the Surah. We have pointed out several landmarks to understand the structure of the upcoming subjects. The details will come under the commentary of the verses. In this light, now recite the verses:

Commentary on Verses 87-88:

يٰٓاَيُّھَاالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا لَا تُحَرِّمُوْا طَيِّبٰتِ مَآ اَحَلَّ اللّٰهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوْا ۭاِنَّ اللّٰهَ لَا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِيْنَ ۔ وَكُلُوْا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللّٰهُ حَلٰلًا طَيِّبًا ۠وَّاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ الَّذِيْٓ اَنْتُمْ بِهٖ مُؤْمِنُوْنَ 

Clarification of 'Tayyibat' (Pure Things): 'the good things which Allah has made lawful to you.' Although everything Allah has made lawful for us is considered pure (tayyibāt), making the addition of this word seemingly unnecessary here, it carries a specific benefit: sometimes, a thing is inherently lawful, but some external cause introduces filth into it. For example, an animal is lawful, but was not slaughtered, or was slaughtered but in a name other than Allah's, or was dedicated to an altar; in these cases, the animal, though of a lawful species, will not remain pure, which is why it will be declared unlawful.

The Meaning of 'A'tada' (Transgression): 'and do not transgress' (lā ta'tadū) here stands in opposition to 'do not make unlawful' (lā tuharrimū). That is: just as it is not permissible to make the pure things made lawful by Allah unlawful, it is also not permissible to make His prohibited things lawful. This is transgression—namely: exceeding the limits prescribed by Allah. Allah created all things for us, permitting us to eat, drink, and use them. There are only a few numbered things that have been declared prohibited. These have the status of Allah's limits, and crossing the limits is not permitted. If a person has the audacity to go beyond these limits, he interferes in the domain of divine prohibitions, and Allah does not like such insolent and foolish ones—'does not like' meaning He dislikes them. This same meaning of 'transgression' is present ahead in verse 94.

Answers to Some Questions: We indicated in the introductions that those questions that arose or could arise later regarding the rulings mentioned at the beginning of this Surah are now clarified at the end. Look back at verses 1-2 of this Surah. After introducing the obligation of adhering to covenants, He stated that all cattle are lawful for you, except for several exceptions. Now, several questions can arise in the mind regarding them: for instance, if adhering to covenants made in the name of Allah is so significant in the religion, what is the ruling for a person who swears an oath to make something lawful or unlawful contrary to the Shariah of God? Or if someone breaks an oath they swore, what is the instruction regarding it? If respecting the sacred symbols, hady, and qala'id is so emphasized, what is the Shariah ruling regarding those animals that have been held sacred since ancient times based on religious customs—such as bahīrah, sā'ibah, wasīlah, and hām? These and other questions arose regarding the rulings mentioned at the beginning. Now, the Quran has taken these questions, providing their answers in the upcoming verses.

The Meaning of 'Tahrim' (Prohibiting): First, He stated this comprehensive and fundamental principle: Do not declare unlawful what the Shariah of God made lawful, nor declare lawful those things from which He prohibited you. 'Declaring unlawful' means declaring something prohibited for oneself or others under the claim or assumption that this is God's command, or that reward and punishment are attached to it, or that it is part of virtue. If there is no such claim, but a person merely avoids using something based on personal taste, health reasons, or caution and thrift, this does not fall under tahrīm.

Declaring Lawful or Unlawful Arbitrarily is Interfering in the Divine Authority: It should be kept in mind that declaring things lawful or unlawful arbitrarily is an interference in the domain of divine sovereignty and a backdoor to breaking the covenant. This interference occurred both by making the prohibited lawful and by making the lawful unlawful. The path of making the prohibited lawful was opened primarily by the incitement of desires, but in making the lawful unlawful, superstitious pagan beliefs and whims played a major role. Thus, the pagan Arabs, based on their pagan concepts, had declared many things unlawful for themselves. Ahead in verse 103, bahīrah, sā'ibah, wasīlah, and hām are mentioned. Further details are given in Surah Al-An'am: 'And they say,

by their claim. And there are cattle whose backs are forbidden [to on], and cattle over which they do not mention the name of Allah, inventing [lies] against Him. He will recompense them for what they used to invent. And they say,

He will punish them for their description. Indeed, He is Wise and Knowing.' (138-139). Similarly, regarding the Jews, it is recorded in the Quran that they declared many things unlawful for themselves based on their arbitrary verdicts, futile questions, and pagan superstitions. The Quran declared such arbitrary choosing of lawfulness and unlawfulness contrary to monotheism and faith, commanding: Eat, drink, and use all the lawful and pure things God blessed you with, not avoiding them based on the superstitions of your times of ignorance, and fear Allah in whom you believe.

لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ اللّٰهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِيْٓ اَيْمَانِكُمْ وَلٰكِنْ يُّؤَاخِذُكُمْ بِمَا عَقَّدْتُّمُ الْاَيْمَانَ ۚ فَكَفَّارَتُهٗٓ اِطْعَامُ عَشَرَةِ مَسٰكِيْنَ مِنْ اَوْسَطِ مَا تُطْعِمُوْنَ اَهْلِيْكُمْ اَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ اَوْ تَحْرِيْرُ رَقَبَةٍ ۭ فَمَنْ لَّمْ يَجِبْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلٰثَةِ اَيَّامٍ ۭذٰلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ اَيْمَانِكُمْ اِذَا حَلَفْتُمْ ۭ وَاحْفَظُوْٓا اَيْمَانَكُمْ ۭ كَذٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللّٰهُ لَكُمْ اٰيٰتِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُوْن 

The Significance of Oaths in the Shariah: 'Allah will not impose blame upon you for what is meaningless in your oaths, but He will impose blame upon you for [breaking] what you intended of oaths.' The concept stated in this segment passed, with minor variations in wording, in Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 224-225, where we discussed it in detail. There, it appeared in connection with the issue of ila' (oath of abstention); here, it appears in connection with the aforementioned issue of declaring things lawful or unlawful. The meaning is: Allah will not hold His servants accountable for their unintentional oaths, but He will hold them accountable for those oaths sworn with the intent and resolve of the heart—through which a covenant is bound, upon which rights and duties are affected, or which affect the lawfulness and unlawfulness of the Shariah in any way. Making the name of Allah a target for contrary or false oaths is not permitted, as stated in Al-Baqarah. Oaths are the foundation of statements, testimonies, and covenants; and covenants represent the foundation of all social, collective, and political rights and duties. Furthermore, as we explained under the first verse of this Surah, covenants also represent the foundation of that primordial covenant we bound with our Lord. Therefore, it is necessary for a person to be highly cautious in the matter of oaths, swearing no oath unnecessarily or contrary to the Shariah. Whichever oath is sworn, if not contrary to the Shariah, must be fulfilled, and if broken, its expiation must be paid, so that the person does not become reckless, indifferent, and entirely unreliable regarding oaths, for such a person is neither worthy of carrying the responsibilities of society nor the responsibilities of the divine covenant.

Expiation for Oaths: 'so its expiation is the feeding of ten needy people from the average of that which you feed your [own] families or their clothing or the freeing of a slave. But whoever cannot find [or afford] - then a fast of three days.'

The Meaning of Explanatory Verses in the Quran: 'Thus does Allah make clear to you His verses that you may be grateful' points to the fact that these are explanatory verses revealed, as we indicated, in response to questions that arose later. The Shariah and guidance are entirely a favor and blessing from Allah upon His servants, and if some of its brief aspects are clarified by Allah Himself, this is a further favor. In this explanation, if many aspects of ease are kept in view for the servants—as is the case here with unintentional oaths and expiation—multiple aspects of favor are gathered. The natural requirement of this is that the servants must be highly grateful to their Lord. If, after all this explanation and detail, they fail to appreciate this blessing, it is extreme ingratitude.

Commentary on Verses 90-91:

يٰٓاَيُّھَاالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْٓا اِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْاَنْصَابُ وَالْاَزْلَامُ رِجْسٌ مِّنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطٰنِ فَاجْتَنِبُوْهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُوْنَ ۔ اِنَّمَا يُرِيْدُ الشَّيْطٰنُ اَنْ يُّوْقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَدَاوَةَ وَالْبَغْضَاۗءَ فِي الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللّٰهِ وَعَنِ الصَّلٰوةِ ۚ فَهَلْ اَنْتُمْ مُّنْتَهُوْنَ 

Every Intoxicant is 'Khamr': Khamr (intoxicants) and maysir (gambling) were discussed under Al-Baqarah 219, while ansāb (altars) and azlām (divining arrows) were discussed under Al-Ma'idah 3. Wine and gambling, as we explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, are both national diseases. At least in the pagan Arab society, this was their status. The claim that khamr applies solely to grape wine is baseless; it is not supported by the speech of the Arabs, and even if it were, the core cause for the prohibition of wine, as is clear from An-Nisa verse 43, is the presence of intoxication. Therefore, every intoxicating thing will carry the same ruling, whether made of grapes or not. In accordance with the wise principle of the Shariah that whatever intoxicates in large quantities is prohibited in small quantities as well, any amount of it will be prohibited, so the door of trial is closed completely.

Gambling and Wine are Satanic Inventions: 'defilement from the work of Satan.' Rijs (defilement) and najas (impurity) were discussed elsewhere; there is no difference in meaning between them. 'from the work of Satan'—meaning: these are among his inventions and designs, which he developed specifically to fulfill his oath to mislead the descendants of Adam from the straight path. If some aspect of benefit is seen in them, it is merely a deceptive coloring. Their harm, compared to their benefit, is so great that this petty benefit carries no value, as explained in Al-Baqarah.

The Impacts of Gambling and Wine on Society: 'Satan only wants to cause between you animosity and hatred through intoxicants and gambling'—the preposition fī (through/in) in fī al-khamri wa-al-maysir expresses the meaning of engagement and absorption. That is: Satan developed these trials specifically to keep you engaged in them, continually kindling the fire of enmity and revenge among you. Indeed, if this epidemic spreads in a society, either the sense of chastity, honor, and modesty is wiped out—as is observed in Westernized society today, which is a great tragedy in itself—or if some remnant of it remains, swords will inevitably be drawn day after day. The Arabs were highly sensitive in matters of chastity, self-respect, and honor, which was among their greatest virtues, but they were also lovers of wine and gambling; consequently, this game of cup and dice was proving highly costly for them. If someone, in his drunken state, insulted someone's honor, degraded him, or cheated in gambling (and these are inseparable from gambling and wine), both sides would draw their swords, and this individual fight would transform into a war between tribes and nations in the blink of an eye, starting an endless cycle of revenge after revenge that could not be extinguished even after a century. Indeed, Arab history records wars whose fire was kindled by gambling or wine, and which could not be extinguished for a century. In any case, this either makes a person a cuckold or ruins his home, and neither of these is tolerable to any healthy society.

Negligence of God is Negligence of the Realities of Life: 'and to avert you from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer.' Mentioning 'prayer' (al-salāh) after 'remembrance of Allah' (dhikr Allāh) is mentioning the specific after the general, which clarifies the connection between the two. Islam has attached the entire elevation and greatness of life to the remembrance of Allah. The one who becomes negligent of God becomes oblivious to his own value. 'They forgot Allah, so He made them forget themselves' points to this reality. The characteristic of remembering God is that it never allows a person to become indifferent to the true realities of life, with the benefit that he never goes astray. If a slip occurs, remembering God helps him. In contrast, the primary effect of wine is to remove a person from the realities of life, throwing him into an imaginary world of which he becomes so mad that, just as a rabid dog fears water, he begins to fear the true realities of life. He never musters the courage to face them, and in this state, his life's story ends. Now, reflect on the misfortune of this human who spent his life dreaming, never waking to reflect on his life's end.

Various Meanings of Interrogation in Arabic: 'So will you not desist?' Interrogation in the Arabic language, as explained by the teacher-Imam in his book Asalīb al-Quran, can denote emphasis, affirmation, warning, denial, rebuke, command, or depreciation. Here, the context warrants that it carries the meaning of a command, just as in Surah Hud, verse 14: 'so will you then be Muslims?' (fahal antum muslimūn). This style adds rebuke, advice, emphasis, warning, and the completion of proof to the command. If you reflect, the style of the discourse reveals that the details of the corruptions of wine and gambling have been placed before you at such different times and from such different angles that no room for confusion remains for anyone; say then, do you desist now or not? Obviously, a simple command style could not accommodate this entire meaning.

وَاَطِيْعُوا اللّٰهَ وَاَطِيْعُوا الرَّسُوْلَ وَاحْذَرُوْا ۚ فَاِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَاعْلَمُوْٓا اَنَّمَا عَلٰي رَسُوْلِنَا الْبَلٰغُ الْمُبِيْن 

This is a further emphasis of the preceding warning: emerge from the net spread by Satan to enter the obedience of Allah and His Messenger, and beware of disobedience and rebellion. If, after this explanation and warning, you turn away, the responsibility lies on you; the Messenger's responsibility is solely to inform you clearly of God's commands and prohibitions, and this duty the Messenger has fulfilled, after which the proof from Allah is completed against you. After this completion of proof, those who choose to go astray must do so while prepared to face its consequences, because after the completion of proof, Allah's apprehension is highly severe.

لَيْسَ عَلَي الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا وَعَمِلُوا الصّٰلِحٰتِ جُنَاحٌ فِيْمَا طَعِمُوْٓا اِذَا مَا اتَّقَوْا وَّاٰمَنُوْا وَعَمِلُوا الصّٰلِحٰتِ ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَّاَمَنُوْا ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَّاَحْسَنُوْا ۭ وَاللّٰهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِيْنَ 

The Demand of the Divine Shariah: We pointed to the rebuke and censure hidden within the style of 'So will you not desist?' above. The intensity of the style must have affected those who had been utilizing the concession regarding wine. They must have felt that it would have been better had they repented of wine at the very first warning, like many other cautious and far-sighted Companions did. They might have feared that this delay on their part would be counted as a shortcoming in the Hereafter and be apprehended. Particularly, regarding those who had passed away during this period, cautious people must have felt anxious: how would they be dealt with? The verse under discussion removed all such doubts: before Allah, there will be no questioning regarding eating or drinking something where no explicit prohibition was violated. The demand of the Divine Shariah is solely that once a limit is defined, people must respect that limit; then, if some addition is made to that limit by the Shariah, they must adopt it firmly, and if further addition is made, they must adopt that as well, fulfilling it with excellence, caution, and sincerity.

Gradual Revelation in the Shariah is for the Ease of the Servants: One thing known from this verse is that the revelation of rulings in the Shariah occurred gradually, and Allah chose this gradual revelation for the ease of His servants. Therefore, He will not apprehend those who utilized the ease He granted. Nonetheless, those who recognized the direction of the wind to take the final step of caution and piety in the very first stage of a matter will find their rank in racing to good deeds. For example, the prohibition of wine was revealed gradually. But those whose religious sensitivity was sharper repented of it at the very first stage. This is proof of their being upon the highest stage of the nature of Islam, but Allah will also show mercy to those who, though failing to attain this stage of perfection, did not transgress the limits of Allah at any stage.

The Stages of Gradual Revelation: The second thing that emerges from the words of the verse is that this gradual revelation has three stages. This is generally the case, not an absolute rule. In some matters, the final and decisive command was revealed at the very first instance; in some, at the second; and in some, the completion of the command occurred at the third instance. For example, the prohibitions regarding food and drink were mentioned in Surah Al-An'am, which is a Meccan Surah; after that, their details appeared at the beginning of this Surah (Al-Ma'idah), clarifying some brief aspects of Al-An'am; then, some further questions arose, because of which the prohibition of certain things was stated here at the end of this Surah, and this Surah, as we explained, is the final Surah regarding Shariah rulings. Thus, in this third stage, the rulings regarding food and drink reached completion. This verse clarifies that those who, during these three stages, followed the revealed commands and limits of Allah will not be apprehended for eating those things they ate at a time when their explicit prohibition had not been revealed.

Piety (Taqwa), Faith (Iman), and Excellence (Ihsan): The third thing that emerges is that the word 'piety' (taqwa) is mentioned thrice in the verse. The first time, it is mentioned alongside faith and righteous deeds; the second time, alongside faith; the third time, alongside excellence (ihsān). The meaning of taqwa was discussed elsewhere: fundamentally, this word is used for guarding the limits of Allah. Here, its threefold mention corresponds to those three gradual stages of rulings to which we pointed above. As for referencing faith and righteous deeds alongside piety, it expresses the reality that before Allah, merely avoiding something is not valid unless that avoiding is accompanied by faith and righteous deeds. This restriction was necessary because, in avoiding certain things (especially food and drink), many people are highly cautious and ascetic though they have no connection to faith and righteous deeds. Aside from yogis and monks, those who love base actions also impose many restrictions on themselves, guarding them with great rigidity; obviously, this carries no value in the religion. Only that caution is valid which is accompanied by faith and righteous deeds. Finally, referencing excellence (ihsān) alongside piety shows that the piety desired and accepted in the religion does not arise from mere outward show and the routine of rites, but its final condition is excellence. The intent of excellence is that a person avoids violating the limits of Allah in the manner that avoiding them deserves, performing every command as if he sees God, and keeping his inner self illuminated with the certainty that if he does not see God, God certainly sees him. This excellence is the true soul of piety and the actual guardian of the limits of Allah. Without this, while displaying piety and abstinence outwardly, a person can create thousands of backdoors to break the limits of Allah. After all, despite displaying piety, how many ways did the Jews open to make God's prohibited things lawful? The ruse they invented for the Sabbath is referred to ahead. The same occurred in this Ummah, to the extent that the 'Book of Ruses' (Kitāb al-Hiyal) became a part of our jurisprudence. Such devisers of religion have no value before God. Allah loves those characterized by the attribute of excellence. 'And Allah loves those who do good' points to this reality.

Commentary on Verses 94-96:

يٰٓاَيُّھَاالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا لَيَبْلُوَنَّكُمُ اللّٰه بِشَيْءٍ مَّنَ الصَّيْدِ تَـنَالُهٗٓ اَيْدِيْكُمْ وَرِمَاحُكُمْ لِيَعْلَمَ اللّٰه مَنْ يَّخَافُهٗ بِالْغَيْبِ ۚ فَمَنِ اعْتَدٰي بَعْدَ ذٰلِكَ فَلَهٗ عَدَابٌ اَلِيْم يَاَيُّھَاالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا لَا تَقْتُلُوا الصَّيْدَ وَاَنْتُمْ حُرُمٌ ۭ وَمَنْ قَتَلَهٗ مِنْكُمْ مُّتَعَمِّدًا فَجَــزَاۗءٌ مِّثْلُ مَا قَتَلَ مِنَ النَّعَمِ يَحْكُمُ بِهٖ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنْكُمْ هَدْيًۢا بٰلِــغَ الْكَعْبَةِ اَوْ كَفَّارَةٌ طَعَامُ مَسٰكِيْنَ اَوْ عَدْلُ ذٰلِكَ صِيَامًا لِّيَذُوْقَ وَبَالَ اَمْرِهٖ ۭعَفَا اللّٰه عَمَّا سَلَفَ ۭ وَمَنْ عَادَ فَيَنْتَقِمُ اللّٰه مِنْهُ ۭ وَاللّٰه عَزِيْزٌ ذُو انْتِقَامٍ ۔ اُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَحْرِ وَطَعَامُهٗ مَتَاعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِلسَّيَّارَةِ ۚ وَحُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَرِّ مَا دُمْتُمْ حُرُمًا ۭ وَاتَّقُوا اللّٰه الَّذِيْٓ اِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُوْنَ 

Regarding the prohibition of hunting in the state of Ihram, mentioned at the beginning of the Surah in verse 1, several details and answers to questions that arose later are explained here.

Informing of the Upcoming Trials: 'Allah will surely test you with something of the game at which your hands and spears [can] reach.' This informs of the trial that can present itself due to game suddenly appearing while in the state of Ihram. Since this prohibition was made specifically to test people's faith and piety, they are forewarned that situations will arise when you are in the state of Ihram and you will see herds of deer or wild cattle right within reach of your spears. The meaning is: in these moments of trial, protect your resolve and faith, and do not slip the way the Children of Israel slipped regarding the Sabbath.

The Significance of This Warning: To understand the significance of this warning, keep several things in mind: first, hunting is inherently highly desirable, especially for the Arabs whose recreation and livelihood largely depended on hunting in that era; second, when a restriction is placed on something desirable, the desire for it becomes even stronger—the Arabic proverb says: 'Man is greedy for what is forbidden'; this greed also has the psychological effect of making a person see that very thing everywhere; third, since these prohibitions were made fundamentally for testing, it is not unlikely that Allah would create situations where the purpose of the test is fulfilled.

Similarity Between the Tests of the Children of Israel and the Muslim Ummah: It should be remembered that for this Ummah, this test is similar to the test the Children of Israel faced regarding the Sabbath. In the Quran, it is mentioned as follows: 'when they transgressed in the matter of the Sabbath - when their fish came to them openly on their Sabbath day, and the day they had no Sabbath they did not come to them. Thus did We test them because they were defiantly disobedient.' (Al-A'raf: 163).

If you reflect on both places, the similarity becomes clear. The nature of the Children of Israel's test was that on the day of their Sabbath, fish would appear openly on the surface of the water, but on other days, they would not appear. This tempted them to invent a ruse to hunt on the Sabbath. Similarly, regarding this Ummah's test, He stated: while in the state of Ihram, you will sometimes see game right under your hands and spears; beware lest this tempt you into a trial similar to the trial of the Children of Israel.

In 'with something' (bi-shay'in), particularly its indefiniteness (tankīr), there is an indication that although this trial will present itself, it will not be highly severe, but light. This reflects the character of this final Shariah: that in its every aspect, human nature is fully respected.

The Core Aspect of Rulings of Trial: 'that Allah may make evident who fears Him unseen.' This explains the purpose of this trial. We explained elsewhere that 'to make evident' (li-ya'lama) also means to distinguish. The meaning is: Allah placed this test specifically to distinguish those who fear Him while He is unseen. The corresponding segment is omitted—namely: to distinguish them from those who do not fear Him while He is unseen. Regarding the rulings of trial, we explained at the beginning of this Surah that their primary purpose is to test the servants' loyalty to God. Outwardly, from the perspective of human interests, they seem devoid of wisdom to an ordinary person, but in reality, they are the primary criterion to test faith in the unseen and fear of the unseen. 'And whoever transgresses after that'—the force in 'after that' (ba'da dhālik) is on this warning and reminder given here. The meaning is: after the prohibition of hunting while in the state of Ihram, and after this warning that you will face trials similar to those faced by the previous nation, whoever still violates the limits of Allah will face a painful punishment.

Expiation for Intentional Hunting in the State of Ihram: 'O you who have believed, do not kill game while you are in the state of ihram. And whoever of you kills it intentionally...' Now, He answered the question: if someone intentionally commits this sin while in the state of Ihram, what is his ruling? He stated: Such a person must pay an expiation, which takes three forms. First, he must send a domestic animal of the same class as the hunted animal to the Kaaba as an offering. If this is not possible, he must feed needy people in proportion to the value of that animal; if this is difficult, he must fast as many days as the number of needy people he was required to feed. As for deciding which domestic animal matches the hunted animal, and in the case of its impossibility, deciding its value, the number of needy people, or the fasts—this task shall be performed by two upright men from among the Muslims, so no possibility of self-partiality remains for the perpetrator.

The Ruling in the Case of Error and Some Related Issues: From the words of the Quran, I find this view sound. Many people make no distinction between error and intent in this matter. In the light of the Quran's words, I find robust the school of thought of Sa'id bin Jubayr, who did not advocate for expiation in the case of error. This is also supported by a statement of Hazrat Hasan. Similarly, those who advocate for deciding the match of the hunted animal solely through its value, and then granting the choice to either offer an animal of that value, feed needy people in that proportion, or fast, do not seem highly robust. When domestic animals matching the hunted animals are clearly available—such as a goat or sheep for a deer, a cow for a wild cow or wild ass—why should the value be the sole criterion for deciding the match? If a match for the hunted animal is not available, the value can certainly serve as its substitute, but making it the criterion under all circumstances is contrary to the words of the Quran. It also does not seem highly robust that the person has equal choice among the three forms of expiation; rather, an order of preference seems to exist. Although the particle aw (or) is for choice, when context warrants, it also implies an order of preference, as in verse 102 of this Surah. For this reason, I consider robust the school of thought of Imam Ahmad and Zufar, who advocate for an order of preference here.

A Stern Warning: 'And whoever returns [to killing game] - Allah will take retribution from him' is a warning similar to the one in 'And whoever transgresses after that - for him is a painful punishment' above. This warning is highly severe, and the reason for this severity is what we pointed to above: that this is a test similar to the test of the Children of Israel regarding the Sabbath, in which their failure brought them an exemplary punishment. The expiation Allah has set alongside it is beneficial only when accompanied by sincere repentance. If the turning of the heart is not included, and a person continues to disobey while thinking that if apprehended he will merely pay the expiation, such people cannot escape the apprehension of God.

'Lawful to you is game of the sea and its food as provision for you and the travelers, but forbidden to you is game of the land as long as you are in the state of ihram...'

Difference Between the Tests of the Children of Israel and the Muslim Ummah: This describes what is excluded from the aforementioned prohibition. For the Jews, as we indicated above, marine hunting was also prohibited on the Sabbath. For this Ummah, land hunting was prohibited in the state of Ihram, but marine hunting remained permissible. This difference might be because the Arabs found greater attraction in land hunting, whereas the Jews, based on their location, found it in marine hunting. This is a test, and if this aspect is kept in view, it aligns with its character.

Just as the word sayd (game) in 'not permitting hunting' is used in its verbal noun sense—and He clarified this by stating 'and when you come out of ihram, then hunt'—it is, in our view, used in its verbal noun sense here as well. The meaning is: as far as the land is concerned, hunting it is prohibited for the person in Ihram, but eating game caught by someone not in Ihram, provided the person in Ihram had no role in it, is not prohibited for him. As for marine game, both catching it and eating what others caught are permissible for the person in Ihram. Allah granted this concession to make things easy for those in Ihram and travelers regarding provisions. On a land journey, if provisions run out, other ways to obtain food remain open. On a marine journey, if this trouble presents itself, no other way remains except hunting. It should also be remembered that the prohibition of hunting for the Jews was only for one day a week. Therefore, there was no fear of any unresolvable trouble presenting itself. For this Ummah, this prohibition was for the entire duration of Ihram. Travelers on marine journeys could face unresolvable trouble under certain circumstances, which is why this concession was granted regarding marine game.

Commentary on Verses 97-100:

جَعَلَ اللّٰهُ الْكَعْبَةَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرَامَ قِــيٰمًا لِّلنَّاسِ وَالشَّهْرَ الْحَرَامَ وَالْهَدْيَ وَالْقَلَاۗىِٕدَ ۭذٰلِكَ لِتَعْلَمُوْٓا اَنَّ اللّٰهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِي السَّمٰوٰتِ وَمَا فِي الْاَرْضِ وَاَنَّ اللّٰهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيْمٌ۔ اِعْلَمُوْٓا اَنَّ اللّٰهَ شَدِيْدُ الْعِقَابِ وَاَنَّ اللّٰهَ غَفُوْرٌ رَّحِيْمٌ۔ مَا عَلَي الرَّسُوْلِ اِلَّا الْبَلٰغُ ۭوَاللّٰهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُوْنَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُوْنَ ۔ قُلْ لَّا يَسْتَوِي الْخَبِيْثُ وَالطَّيِّبُ وَلَوْ اَعْجَبَكَ كَثْرَةُ الْخَبِيْثِ ۚ فَاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ يٰٓاُولِي الْاَلْبَابِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُوْنَ 

Emphasizing Respect for All Sacred Symbols: The preceding statement relates fundamentally to respecting the sacred symbols of Allah; therefore, He followed it by reminding of other well-known sacred symbols—the House of Allah, the sacred months, hady, and qala'id—instructing them to guard the guidelines given regarding their respect. Let no aspect of their sanctity be compromised. The Kaaba is explained here through the words 'the Sacred House' (al-bayta al-harām) to draw attention to this aspect: that this is the house made sacred by God, whose respect has defined boundaries that must be guarded under all circumstances. We wrote in detail on 'as a support for mankind' (qiyāman lil-nās) under Surah Al-Baqarah, within the discussion of 'a place of return for the people and security', showing that the entire religious, political, and economic integration of Abraham's descendants occurred by virtue of this House, and after the Prophet's sending, this House is the Qiblah and center of the entire Ummah. 'The sacred month' (al-shahra al-harām) does not refer to a specific month, but is used as a generic noun for all the sacred months. We discussed the religious and worldly blessings of these months in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Hady and qala'id were explained at the beginning of this Surah. In our view, the sentence is structured as follows: Allah made the Kaaba, the Sacred House, as a support for mankind, and the sacred months, hady, and qala'id as sacred symbols. Since this was clear from the context, the word 'sacred symbols' was omitted.

The Wisdom of Sacred Symbols: 'That is so you may know that Allah knows...' The word 'that' (dhālik) points to the aforementioned sacred symbols, and this explains the wisdom behind designating these symbols: Allah designated them specifically to firmly root within you the belief that Allah knows everything through His actions and attributes. We pointed to this under verse 94, within the discussion of 'that Allah may make evident who fears Him unseen', showing that sacred symbols are designated fundamentally for testing. Allah tests people through them to see who fears Him while He is unseen, and who does not. Obviously, only those who possess the established knowledge that God knows whatever is in the heavens and the earth can pass this test. Those who have this belief firm are those who fear God while He is unseen, and this fear of the unseen represents the soul of all fear of God, Islam, and faith. Here, Allah described His knowledge both through His actions and His attributes, because God's knowledge encompasses the past, present, future, open, hidden, unseen, present, and concealed, and it is this belief of man regarding God's knowledge that produces within him fear of the unseen.

Warning and Glad Tidings: 'Know that Allah is severe in penalty and that Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.' This presents both the warning and glad tidings together. The meaning is: those who, devoid of fear of God, desecrate His sacred symbols will be severely punished by Allah, and those who fear Him while He is unseen, respecting His symbols as they deserve to be respected, will find Him Forgiving and Merciful. 'Not upon the Messenger is [responsibility] except notification...' is the second warning. The meaning is: our Messenger's responsibility is solely to convey our guidance clearly. This duty the Messenger has fulfilled. Now, the responsibility lies on you. Whether you accept it or not. If you accept it, it is for your own good; if not, you will see its end yourself. Remember that God knows what you reveal and what you conceal.

The Abundance of an Evil is No Proof for its Permissibility: 'Say,

So fear Allah, O people of understanding, that you may succeed.'

This is also a warning within this sequence, and a great one. We explained elsewhere that 'evil' (khabīth) and 'good' (tayyib) apply to both bad and good things, and bad and good persons. Similarly, they apply to things that are bad and good physically, and things that are bad and good intellectually and morally. Here, both things and persons are in view, but as far as goodness and badness are concerned, they are discussed solely from the moral aspect. The meaning is: before God, evil and good, right and wrong, rebellion and piety, the righteous and the wicked are not equal. God is absolute good and entirely truth and justice. For this reason, He likes only good, not liking evil; He will accept only the good, while for the evil He has nothing except the fire of Hell. Those who make themselves evil by disobeying God and desecrating His symbols must remember that God will cast them into Hell, and success will be attained only by those who fear God, respecting His commands and symbols and making themselves good. This is like a completion of the preceding statement: 'Know that Allah is severe in penalty and that Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.' After that, by stating 'although the abundance of the evil might please you,' He informed of the greatest trial on this path—namely: for many foolish ones, the abundance of an evil serves not only as an incitement to imitate it but also as a proof for its permissibility and goodness. Whichever trial becomes common and whichever evil becomes the fashion, they open their doors to it. First, their conscience feels no aversion to it, and if it feels some initially, they satisfy it with the childish consolation: how can anyone keep themselves apart from this in this era? The result is that seeing others naked in the bathhouse, they also strip. Thus, the entire society gradually becomes a society of the naked, and the situation reaches the point where the noble and refined is not the one who wears clothes, but those who display their nudity. If you ask them for proof of their behavior, whatever justification they construct, at its bottom will lie solely this: what can be done, this is the way of the times. That is: the action of the majority becomes their guide, and they walk on the track the entire herd is walking on. The words of the Quran clearly show that although the dominance of an evil and the abundance of the bad carry an attraction, those who are subdued by this attraction to submit themselves to it are all included among the foolish; the people of understanding (ulū al-albāb) are those who remain safe from its effects even in this general epidemic, continuing to walk on the path of piety. These are the ones who will succeed, because before God, evil and good will not be equal, nor will the evil become good because its quantity is very large.

Commentary on Verses 101-102:

يٰٓاَيُّھَاالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا لَا تَسْــــَٔـلُوْا عَنْ اَشْيَاۗءَ اِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ ۚ وَاِنْ تَسْــــَٔـلُوْا عَنْهَا حِيْنَ يُنَزَّلُ الْقُرْاٰنُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ ۭعَفَا اللّٰهُ عَنْهَا ۭ وَاللّٰهُ غَفُوْرٌ حَلِيْمٌ۔ قَدْ سَاَلَهَا قَوْمٌ مِّنْ قَبْلِكُمْ ثُمَّ اَصْبَحُوْا بِهَا كٰفِرِيْنَ 

Prohibiting Unnecessary Questions: This is also a timely warning. We indicated that the questions that arose or could arise regarding the rulings mentioned at the beginning of the Surah have been answered. Now, He warned: The useful questions have been answered, but do not ask about things which, if clarified, would be contrary to your temperament and desires, making you feel bad. Simultaneously, He clarified that this is the era of the Quran's revelation. This era is similar to the days of rain: just as every seed sprouts during the rainy season, any question you ask during this era can bring down a revealed answer. Therefore, reflect and ask only those questions that are beneficial for you in this world and the Hereafter and increase your knowledge of the Shariah. Do not open the way to increase your restrictions by asking unnecessary questions; God is Forgiving and Forbearing. For this reason, He has overlooked unnecessary questions. If their answers had been given, it is possible you could not have adhered to them, thereby sowing thorns in your own path and inviting God's anger.

Afterward, He referenced a previous nation as an example. This refers to the Jews, but they are mentioned as an indefinite noun (qawmun), which expresses aversion and dislike. Pointing to them, He stated: They also asked such questions of their prophet, but when the answers were given, they became deniers of them. The nature of their questions was made clear in the story of the cow in Surah Al-Baqarah, and in previous Surahs we explained in detail that it was indeed through such questions of theirs that they increased those chains and restrictions upon themselves which the Quran termed 'burdens and shackles'.

This shows that it is a special favor of Allah upon this Ummah that He overlooked unnecessary questions, saving us from burdens and shackles, and leaving these matters to our intellect and research where our intellect and nature are sufficient to guide us. There was also this wisdom in it: this Shariah is the final and complete Shariah; therefore, nothing in it can be contrary to the highest standard, because no prophet is to come after the Seal of the Prophets (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to adjust it. In contrast, the Jews' Shariah was a temporary Shariah, and its burdens and shackles, as the Quran explicitly states, were removed through the final Prophet. In 'A people before you asked them' (qad sa'alahā qawmun), the referent of the pronoun is the mentioned questions, but this does not imply that they asked the exact same questions; rather, it means they asked questions of this nature. Pronouns are also used in this manner in Arabic, and parallels exist in the Quran. We will discuss this in detail under the relevant contexts.

Commentary on Verses 103-105:

مَا جَعَلَ اللّٰهُ مِنْۢ بَحِيْرَةٍ وَّلَا سَاۗىِٕبَةٍ وَّلَا وَصِيْلَةٍ وَّلَا حَامٍ ۙ وَّلٰكِنَّ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا يَفْتَرُوْنَ عَلَي اللّٰهِ الْكَذِبَ ۭ وَاَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُوْنَ ۔ وَاِذَا قِيْلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا اِلٰى مَآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهُ وَاِلَى الرَّسُوْلِ قَالُوْا حَسْبُنَا مَا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ اٰبَاۗءَنَا ۭ اَوَلَوْ كَانَ اٰبَاۗؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُوْنَ شَـيْــــًٔـا وَّلَا يَهْتَدُوْنَ ۔ يٰٓاَيُّھَاالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا عَلَيْكُمْ اَنْفُسَكُمْ ۚلَا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَّنْ ضَلَّ اِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ ۭ اِلَى اللّٰهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيْعًا فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُوْنَ 

Instruction to Avoid Unlawful Symbols: In verse 97 above, those things were mentioned which Allah designated as sacred symbols. After several timely warnings, He now mentioned those pagan things which the polytheists had designated as sacred symbols, wrapping them in a religious covering. Yet, Allah did not prescribe any of these things. The polytheists invented them arbitrarily, establishing a tradition of respecting them, and then attributed these innovations to Allah, claiming He had commanded their respect. Thus, after mentioning the prescribed, He mentioned the contrary unprescribed things so the Muslims would avoid them.

In 'Allah has not appointed' (mā ja'ala Allāhu), the verb 'appointed' (ja'ala) means prescribed. That is: Allah has not prescribed these things. Bahīrah refers to a she-camel that had given birth to five offspring, the last of which was a male. They would slit her ears and let her loose, neither riding her nor milking her.
