Sā'ibah refers to a she-camel whose owner pledged during his illness that if he was cured, he would let her loose, neither riding her nor milking her. Wasīlah refers to a sheep: if she gave birth to a female, they kept it; if a male, they dedicated it to their deities; and if she gave birth to both a male and a female together, they called it wasīlah, considering the male as spared from being sacrificed to their idols. Hām refers to a male camel that had sired ten generations of offspring. They would let him loose, neither riding him nor loading burdens upon him.

All these were the offerings and pledges of the pagan Arabs. Such animals wandered loose, drinking from whichever water-place they wished and grazing in whichever pasture they pleased. They possessed such a degree of religious sanctity that everyone trembled at the thought of harming them. The Quran clarified that these carry no Shariah status. Shariah status belongs solely to hady and qala'id. These things are merely the inventions of superstition, and attributing them to the Shariah is an open slander against Allah and His Shariah. Those devoid of intellect have attributed these foolish things to Allah.

'And when it is said to you,

'—this explains the statement 'but most of them do not understand' (wa-aktharuhum lā ya'qilūna) in the preceding verse. The meaning is: when they are invited to leave these futile things to come to the Book revealed by Allah and the way shown by the Messenger, they reply with pride: 'Sufficient for us is that upon which we found our fathers.' He stated: Will they still make their fathers' tradition their proof even if their fathers knew nothing and were not guided? That is: the mere fact that a way has been inherited from the fathers cannot serve as proof for its correctness; it is also necessary to know whether the fathers chose it based on some knowledge and proof, or chose it arbitrarily. If this distinction is made unnecessary, then the intellect—which is the distinguishing attribute of humanity—remains a completely useless thing.

Consolation to the Muslims that Your Duty is Solely to Convey the Truth: 'O you who have believed, upon you is [responsibility for] yourselves. Those who have gone astray will not harm you when you have been guided. To Allah is your return all together...' Just as He consoled the Prophet in verse 99, stating that the Messenger's responsibility is solely to convey the truth clearly, and accepting or rejecting it is the people's own responsibility, similarly, in this verse, He consoled the Muslims: Leave those who have sold their intellect to follow their fathers blindly to their state; you have conveyed the word of truth, and if they do not accept it, they will harm themselves, not you; whoever chooses the path of deviation will see its end himself. The one who shows the correct path suffers no harm from it. You have done your part to help them; if they do not change, worry about yourselves and do not exhaust yourselves in grief over them. This concept is mentioned in many places in the Quran. We will discuss it further under Surah Al-An'am, verses 15, 48, 51, 52, and 69. Some fell into the doubt because of this verse that Muslims have no concern with the guidance or misguidance of others, having to worry only about their own souls. But this view—aside from being contrary to the Muslims' primary duty of bearing witness over mankind—is entirely disconnected from this verse itself. What emerges from this verse is that upon seeing the disbelief of the disbelievers, the Companions, like the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself, would sometimes think: perhaps this is because of some shortcoming in fulfilling the duty of conveyance laid upon them. Upon this, Allah consoled the Companions, just as He consoled the Prophet: the fault is not yours, but is the corruption of their own nature; you have fulfilled your duty well, and you carry no responsibility for their disbelief or faith. No questioning regarding it will be asked of you.

يٰٓاَيُّھَاالَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا شَهَادَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ اِذَا حَضَرَ اَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ حِيْنَ الْوَصِيَّةِ اثْنٰنِ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنْكُمْ اَوْ اٰخَرٰنِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ اِنْ اَنْتُمْ ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي الْاَرْضِ فَاَصَابَتْكُمْ مُّصِيْبَةُ الْمَوْتِ ۭ تَحْبِسُوْنَهُمَا مِنْۢ بَعْدِ الصَّلٰوةِ فَيُقْسِمٰنِ بِاللّٰهِ اِنِ ارْتَبْتُمْ لَا نَشْتَرِيْ بِهٖ ثَـمَنًا وَّلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبٰى ۙ وَلَا نَكْتُمُ شَهَادَةَ ۙاللّٰهِ اِنَّآ اِذًا لَّمِنَ الْاٰثِمِيْنَ 

Covenant Upon All Areas of Testimony: This is also a perfecting and completing ruling, whose nature is to clarify some of the rulings mentioned at the beginning of the Surah. At the beginning of the Surah, in verse 8, Muslims were instructed: 'O you who have believed, be persistently standing firm for Allah, witnesses in justice, and let not the hatred of a people prevent you from being just. Be just; that is nearer to righteousness. And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Acquainted with what you do.' There, as we explained, the general testimony is described, the responsibility of which is laid upon this Ummah toward all creation. Now, by explaining this testimony in a specific internal matter of the Muslims, He has completed this subject, joining this verse with the mention of the great testimony that is coming from verse 109—the final subject upon which this great Surah ends. The placement of this verse in this context shows, on one hand, that once a covenant is taken upon this completing ruling, it is as if a covenant is taken upon all areas of testimony; on the other hand, it shows that although this is a specific ruling, in its essence it is a part of that great testimony which the prophets (peace be upon them) were commanded to carry out in this world, and for which Allah will stand them in the Hereafter.

Instructions Regarding the Testimony of Wills: 'O you who have believed, testimony [should be taken] among you when death approaches one of you at the time of bequest - [that of] two just men from among you...' 'when death approaches one of you' (idhā hadara ahadakumu al-mawt) and 'at the time of bequest' (hīna al-wasiyyah) both relate grammatically to 'testimony among you' (shahādatu baynikum), and 'two just men from among you' (ithnāni dhawā 'adlin minkum) is structured similarly to 'but righteousness is [in] one who believes in Allah.' The meaning is: if the time of death approaches someone, and he wishes to make a bequest regarding his property, he must make two upright men from among the Muslims witnesses to it. 'or two others from outside you if you are traveling through the land and the disaster of death befalls you...'—if a person is on a journey and the stage of death approaches him on the journey, and he cannot find two Muslims to make witnesses, he may, out of necessity, make two non-Muslims witnesses. 'You detain them after the prayer, and they swear by Allah if you doubt [them, saying],

' This relates to the primary ruling: 'two just men from among you'. That is: regarding the two trustworthy witnesses you select for testimony from among your own people, if there is a fear that they might alter their testimony out of partiality or violate someone's right, you may, to strengthen it, detain them after some prayer in the mosque, taking an oath from them in the name of Allah that they will not alter their testimony for any worldly benefit or partiality toward anyone, even if he should be their relative, and that if they alter it, they will be of the sinful. In 'after the prayer' (min ba'di al-salāh), 'prayer' is used as a generic noun; whichever prayer after which the bequeather finds the opportunity, he may do this. The benefit of restricting it to 'after the prayer' is that an oath and testimony carried out after prayer and in the mosque in the presence of congregants is expected to keep a believer firm, preventing him from altering it for any temptation. Indeed, regarding oaths and treaties, it has been the custom since ancient times that they are generally completed before temples. Since the effect of this is a natural thing, Islam also gave it significance. A condition is also mentioned here—namely: if you have a doubt regarding the witnesses. If there is no fear, the witnesses being of such a standard of trustworthiness and uprightness that no suspicion exists, there is no need to demand an oath of them. In 'we will not withhold the testimony of Allah' (lā naktumu shahādata Allāhi), this testimony is termed the testimony of Allah to highlight its greatness, because every testimony of a believer, small or great, is a part of that great duty He commanded in 'be persistently standing firm for Allah, witnesses in justice'; if any betrayal occurs in it, he becomes a betrayer not only of servants but of his Lord as well.

We have restricted this oath to the primary ruling—namely: when the witnesses are from among your own people, the Muslims. This is because for non-Muslims, the prayer, the mosque, an oath in the name of Allah, and that too in the words 'we will not withhold the testimony of Allah; indeed, we would then be of the sinful' are entirely ineffective things. First, why would they tolerate these things contrary to their religious sentiments? And even if they did, what effect would it have upon them? Their testimony is tolerated merely as an exception out of necessity, relating to an rare situation; therefore, utilizing this arrangement to protect their testimony is entirely disconnected.

فَاِنْ عُثِرَ عَلٰٓي اَنَّهُمَا اسْتَحَقَّآ اِثْمًا فَاٰخَرٰنِ يَقُوْمٰنِ مَقَامَهُمَا مِنَ الَّذِيْنَ اسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْاَوْلَيٰنِ فَيُقْسِمٰنِ بِاللّٰهِ لَشَهَادَتُنَآ اَحَقُّ مِنْ شَهَادَتِهِمَا وَمَا اعْتَدَيْنَآ ڮ اِنَّآ اِذًا لَّمِنَ الظّٰلِمِيْنَ 

*'Athara 'alā al-sirr* means he became aware of the secret. Awlayān is the dual of awlā, which means more deserving: 'indeed, the people most deserving of Abraham are those...'—namely: those most deserving of testimony (al-awlayāni bil-shahādah). This refers to those two witnesses who were made the primary witnesses of the bequest, because by virtue of their office, they are the ones most deserving of testimony; this is why they are expressed through the word awlayān. This also points to the fact that since they are the ones most deserving of testimony, they must respect their office, committing no such bad practice that their testimony would be nullified by the oaths of others.

This is a further check on those witnesses. He stated: If it becomes known that they have committed a sin—altering the bequest out of partiality or violating someone's right—then two others shall stand in their place from among those whose right was violated, swearing by Allah: 'Our testimony is truer than their testimony, and we have not transgressed. Indeed, we would then be of the wrongdoers.'

ذٰلِكَ اَدْنٰٓي اَنْ يَّاْتُوْا بِالشَّهَادَةِ عَلٰي وَجْهِهَآ اَوْ يَخَافُوْٓا اَنْ تُرَدَّ اَيْمَانٌۢ بَعْدَ اَيْمَانِهِمْ ۭوَاتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ وَاسْمَعُوْا ۭ وَاللّٰهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفٰسِقِيْنَ 

This explains the benefit of this check: it is nearer to making them give testimony in its true form, otherwise they will fear that if any bad practice is committed by them, their oaths will be nullified by the oaths of others. This shows that if those whose right was violated swear the mentioned oath, the testimony of the original witnesses of the bequest will be rejected despite their being the ones most deserving of testimony. In 'lest oaths be rejected after their oaths' (an turadda aymānun ba'da aymānihim), the indefiniteness (tankīr) of 'oaths' is similar to the indefiniteness in 'some faces'. 'And fear Allah and listen; and Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people'—that is: fear Allah and obey the Prophet; those who do not fear Allah and do not listen to the Prophet are rebels, and Allah will not guide such rebels; they will wander in this world and face their evil end in the Hereafter.

يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللّٰهُ الرُّسُلَ فَيَقُوْلُ مَاذَآ اُجِبْتُمْ ۭ قَالُوْا لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا ۭاِنَّكَ اَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوْبِ 

The Responsibility of the Divine Covenant in This World and the Hereafter: From here, the final segment of this Surah begins. This clarifies that on the Day of Resurrection, Allah will gather all the messengers, asking them what teaching they had given to their respective nations, and what behavior and response their nations displayed. At the end of the Surah, this is a reminder of the reality that the responsibility of the divine covenant lies on both the prophet and the nation, and both will be questioned regarding it on the Day of Resurrection. Although the upcoming question and answer relate solely to Jesus, he is mentioned as an example. Through this example, it is made clear that similar questions and answers will occur with every prophet regarding his nation, and Allah will clarify before every nation, in the presence of their prophet, what violations they committed regarding God's covenant contrary to their prophet's teaching. The wisdom of choosing Jesus as an example is that he is the final link in the Israelite chain of prophethood. His testimony can serve as a lesson for both the Jews and Christians, and also for this Ummah. The testimony of our Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is not mentioned at this stage because his Ummah was still in its formative phase; its entire character had not yet manifested.

The Question to the Prophets Concerns Their Nations' Conduct: 'what response did you receive?'—the true context of the question is clear from its context. This question will not be asked of the prophets regarding their deniers, but, as is clear, regarding their nations: what was the behavior and response of those you entered into the covenant of Allah? The prophets (peace be upon them) will express their lack of knowledge because of the nature of the question, as their knowledge was limited solely to the period of their presence in the world, whereas the correct answer to this question depends on knowing the subsequent changes made by their nations. Since they will have no knowledge of subsequent changes, they will refer the answer to God. Ahead, Jesus' statement is reported: 'And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness.'

اِذْ قَالَ اللّٰهُ يٰعِيْسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ اذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِيْ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلٰي وَالِدَتِكَ ۘ اِذْ اَيَّدْتُّكَ بِرُوْحِ الْقُدُسِ ۣ تُكَلِّمُ النَّاسَ فِي الْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًا ۚ وَاِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ الْكِتٰبَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَالتَّوْرٰىةَ وَالْاِنْجِيْلَ ۚ وَاِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ الطِّيْنِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ بِاِذْنِيْ فَتَنْفُخُ فِيْهَا فَتَكُوْنُ طَيْرًۢا بِاِذْنِيْ وَتُبْرِئُ الْاَكْـمَهَ وَالْاَبْرَصَ بِاِذْنِيْ ۚ وَاِذْ تُخْرِجُ الْمَوْتٰى بِاِذْنِيْ ۚ وَاِذْ كَفَفْتُ بَنِيْٓ اِسْرَاۗءِيْلَ عَنْكَ اِذْ جِئْتَهُمْ بِالْبَيِّنٰتِ فَقَالَ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا مِنْهُمْ اِنْ هٰذَآ اِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِيْن 

All the miracles of Jesus mentioned here have been described in Surah Ali 'Imran, where we explained them. 'And when I restrained the Children of Israel from you' points to those conspiracies the Children of Israel hatched to murder and crucify Jesus.

Questions to Disgrace the Christians: On the Day of Resurrection, Allah will address all these statements to Jesus to complete the proof against the Christians. In the presence of Jesus, it will be made clear to the Christians that every favor bestowed upon Jesus and his mother was from Allah, all the miracles he displayed were by Allah's permission and command, and it was Allah who saved him from the dangers the Jews put him in. Since Allah performed all this, and its greatest witness is Jesus himself, let the Christians say on whose command they made him God. The repetition of 'by My permission' (bi-idhnihi) is highly eloquent. Allah will repeat it for every single point, and Jesus will express agreement and truth to each; obviously, when the miracles based on which the Christians made Jesus God are shown to have occurred by God's permission, with the performer of the miracles himself confessing it, what will remain for the Christians except disgrace and humiliation?

Commentary on Verses 111-115:

وَاِذْ اَوْحَيْتُ اِلَى الْحَوَارِيّٖنَ اَنْ اٰمِنُوْا بِيْ وَبِرَسُوْلِيْ ۚ قَالُوْٓا اٰمَنَّا وَاشْهَدْ بِاَنَّنَا مُسْلِمُوْنَ ۔ اِذْ قَالَ الْحَوَارِيُّوْنَ يٰعِيْسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ هَلْ يَسْتَطِيْعُ رَبُّكَ اَنْ يُّنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا مَاۗىِٕدَةً مِّنَ السَّمَاۗءِ ۭ قَالَ اتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ اِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُّؤْمِنِيْنَ ۔ قَالُوْا نُرِيْدُ اَنْ نَّاْكُلَ مِنْهَا وَتَطْمَىِٕنَّ قُلُوْبُنَا وَنَعْلَمَ اَنْ قَدْ صَدَقْتَنَا وَنَكُوْنَ عَلَيْهَا مِنَ الشّٰهِدِيْنَ ۔ قَالَ عِيْسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ اللّٰهُمَّ رَبَّنَآ اَنْزِلْ عَلَيْنَا مَاۗىِٕدَةً مِّنَ السَّمَاۗءِ تَكُوْنُ لَنَا عِيْدًا لِّاوَّلِنَا وَاٰخِرِنَا وَاٰيَةً مِّنْكَ ۚ وَارْزُقْنَا وَاَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرّٰزِقِيْنَ ۔ قَالَ اللّٰهُ اِنِّىْ مُنَزِّلُهَا عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ فَمَنْ يَّكْفُرْ بَعْدُ مِنْكُمْ فَاِنِّىْٓ اُعَذِّبُهٗ عَذَابًا لَّآ اُعَذِّبُهٗٓ اَحَدًا مِّنَ الْعٰلَمِيْنَ 

The Wisdom of Discussing the Disciples: The word 'inspired' (awhaytu) is used here in its literal sense, not its technical sense—namely: placing an intent within the heart. The word 'disciple' (hawāriyyūn) was discussed elsewhere. These are the disciples who, out of the entire nation, believed in Jesus, helped him in his call, and became his caliphs. Because of their significance, Allah will also place those realities before them on the Day of Resurrection through which the proof will be completed against the Christians. Several aspects of this completion of proof are mentioned here:

First, the religion the disciples accepted was Islam, not Christianity or Zoroastrianism. Second, the disciples addressed Jesus as 'Jesus, son of Mary,' harboring no concept of his divinity. They believed in Allah alone as the Lord of Jesus and of the entire universe.

The Purpose of the Supplication for the Table: Third, they did not believe Jesus was the performer of miracles inherently, but merely a means for their manifestation. Consequently, the request they made for the table to be sent down was not directed to Jesus, asking him to send it down, but asking him to supplicate to his Lord for it, if it was not contrary to His wisdom, so their hearts would find reassurance.

When all these points are brought to light in the presence of Jesus, the disciples, and the Christians, all the lies the Christians invented against Jesus and his disciples, through which they entangled them in their innovations, will be exposed.

The Question Concerns God's Wisdom, Not His Power: Regarding 'Can your Lord send down...?' it should be remembered that the disciples' question did not concern God's power, but His wisdom: whether displaying such an open sign would align with His wisdom or not. The disciples were believers; they knew well that their request was similar to the request the Children of Israel made to see God, which brought them the lightning. Although miracles are extraordinary, they appear within the veil of means. The veils are not entirely lifted. For this reason, Allah never encouraged such requests where desires exceed the limits set in the law of God for the manifestation of miracles. Consequently, Jesus also discouraged them, and when he supplicated for it upon their repeated request, Allah did not favor the request, stating: I will send it down to you, but remember that whoever disbelieves after seeing such open signs will be punished with a punishment I will not inflict upon anyone else. It seems the disciples desisted from their request after this. A group of commentators also holds the view that it was not sent down. No mention of its sending is found in the Gospels either.

Commentary on Verses 116-118:

وَاِذْ قَالَ اللّٰهُ يٰعِيْسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ ءَاَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُوْنِيْ وَاُمِّيَ اِلٰــهَيْنِ مِنْ دُوْنِ اللّٰهِ ۭ قَالَ سُبْحٰنَكَ مَا يَكُوْنُ لِيْٓ اَنْ اَقُوْلَ مَا لَيْسَ لِيْ ۚ اِنْ كُنْتُ قُلْتُهٗ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهٗ ۭ تَعْلَمُ مَا فِيْ نَفْسِيْ وَلَآ اَعْلَمُ مَا فِيْ نَفْسِكَ ۭاِنَّكَ اَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوْبِ ۔ مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ اِلَّا مَآ اَمَرْتَنِيْ بِهٖٓ اَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللّٰهَ رَبِّيْ وَرَبَّكُمْ ۚ وَكُنْتُ عَلَيْهِم| شَهِيْدًا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيْهِمْ ۚ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِيْ كُنْتَ اَنْتَ الرَّقِيْبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۭواَنْتَ عَلٰي كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيْدٌ۔ اِنْ تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَاِنَّهُمْ عِبَادُكَ ۚ وَاِنْ تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَاِنَّكَ اَنْتَ الْعَزِيْزُ الْحَكِيْمُ 

Disgracing the Christians in the Hereafter: Although the purpose of mentioning the preceding points is also, as we indicated, to disgrace the Christians, that was indirect. Now, He mentioned the question Allah will ask Jesus regarding the Christians' core deviation: whether he had instructed the Christians to take him and his mother as deities besides Allah. Jesus will reply: How could I utter what I had no right to? Neither did You authorize me to say it, nor did I have any share in creation to claim it. The purpose of this question and answer is to completely disgrace the Christians, who associated Jesus and his mother with God, in the presence of the entire gathering. We discussed 'Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord' in the commentary of Surah Ali 'Imran, showing that it represents the correct expression of Jesus' statement 'my Father and your Father'; see it there. 'And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them'—'witness' (shahīd) here means observer. The meaning is: as long as I was present among them, I observed what they were doing; but when You took me up, I had no knowledge of what they did and corrupted. We pointed to this under 'what response did you receive?' above—showing that the prophets (peace be upon them) will express their lack of knowledge because of this.

Jesus Absolves Himself of Interceding for the Christians: 'If You should punish them - indeed they are Your servants; and if You should forgive them - indeed You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise.' The eloquence of this statement of Jesus cannot be described. Outwardly, the desire would be for the statement to be: 'if You forgive them, they are Your servants; and if You punish them, You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise'; but if it were structured that way, it would serve as an explicit intercession for the Christians, whereas it is known that the prophets (peace be upon them) will not intercede for polytheists. For this reason, Jesus will express the matter in a style that is true, fitting for the divine court, compassionate, yet carrying no responsibility of interceding for polytheists and corrupters of the religion. If you reflect on the words: 'If You should punish them - indeed they are Your servants; and if You should forgive them - indeed You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise,' you will find that this statement accommodates all the virtues we pointed to, while allowing Jesus to completely absolve himself of interceding for them.

Commentary on Verses 119-120:

قَالَ اللّٰهُ هٰذَا يَوْمُ يَنْفَعُ الصّٰدِقِيْنَ صِدْقُهُمْ ۭ لَهُمْ جَنّٰتٍ تَجْرِيْ مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْاَنْھٰرُ خٰلِدِيْنَ فِيْهَآ اَبَدًا ۭ رَضِيَ اللّٰهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوْا عَنْهُ ۭ ذٰلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيْمُ ۔ لِلّٰهِ مُلْكُ السَّمٰوٰتِ وَالْاَرْضِ وَمَا فِيْهِنَّ ۭ وَهُوَ عَلٰي كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيْرٌ

We discussed the word 'truthful' (sādiqīn) in the commentary of Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 17. Here, 'the truthful' refers to those who proved completely truthful to their word, promise, and covenant with Allah, making no alteration or distortion in it, and remaining firm on the Shariah of Allah with resolve through all the ups and downs of life. 'Among the believers are men who have been true to their covenant with Allah...'

The Day of Success for the Truthful: The meaning is: Allah will state: Today is the day when the triumph and success of the truthful is made manifest. For the covenant-breakers, betrayers, and those who spent their lives in false desires, there is nothing today except regret and failure. Those who fulfilled their covenants with Allah with sincerity and truth will have gardens beneath which rivers flow, where they will abide forever. Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him—meaning: their Lord fulfilled what He desired of them in accordance with His pleasure, and they found their expectations and hopes in their Lord fulfilled thousands and millions of times beyond their expectations. He stated: This is the great success. To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and all that is in them, and He is competent over all things.

These are the final lines of the commentary of this Surah written down. And our final call is: Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.
